topics.title: Anxiety and worry
core_guidance: State clearly that anxiety is a real stress response, not a personal failure.
Use one immediate regulating skill first: paced breathing, grounding, or a body scan.
Break the problem into the smallest next action and keep the plan concrete.
Encourage gradual exposure only when the person is calm and the situation is safe.
Reinforce sleep, hydration, movement, nutrition, and reduced stimulant use.
red_flags: Panic with chest pain, fainting, severe shortness of breath, or new neurologic symptoms.
Avoidance or fear that is rapidly shrinking daily functioning.
Reassurance-seeking that is escalating instead of settling.
support_steps: Paced breathing (inhale 4, hold 2, exhale 6) for 60-90 seconds.
Name five things you can see, four you can feel, three you can hear.
Write the fear in one sentence, then answer with one realistic next step.

topics.title: Low mood and depression
core_guidance: Validate the pain directly and avoid minimizing it.
Shift from abstract coping to one tiny, doable action in the next 10 minutes.
Ask about safety and social support early when hopelessness or numbness appears.
Encourage professional care if symptoms persist, worsen, or impair function.
Use behavioral activation: action before motivation, not after.
red_flags: Any suicidal thoughts, self-harm urges, or wishing not to wake up.
Marked inability to function, psychotic symptoms, or severe weight/sleep change.
Withdrawal from everyone and inability to complete basic daily tasks.
support_steps: Pick one small task now and one later; keep both under 10 minutes.
Get daylight exposure early and keep sleep/wake times regular.
Send one brief check-in to a trusted person today.
If safety is uncertain, ask directly whether they are in immediate danger.

topics.title: Burnout and chronic stress
core_guidance: Differentiate burnout from depression, but treat both as serious when functioning drops.
Push for boundaries, reduced load, and protected recovery time.
Use micro-breaks, reduced stimulation, and fewer simultaneous demands.
Replace perfectionism with realistic minimum-viable goals.
red_flags: Persistent exhaustion that does not improve with rest.
Depersonalization, cynicism, or severe loss of functioning.
Burnout plus hopelessness or suicidal thinking.
support_steps: Schedule micro-breaks every 60-90 minutes.
Defer or delegate one task immediately.
Set a 2-week recovery plan with fewer commitments and more rest.
Protect sleep, meals, and a hard stop for work.

topics.title: Sleep difficulties
core_guidance: Use sleep hygiene: consistent schedule, morning light, and less late caffeine.
Limit time in bed awake; use a brief, repeatable wind-down routine.
Treat sleep as a routine problem, not a character flaw.
Short relaxation skills can reduce arousal before bed.
red_flags: Sleep disruption with severe mood symptoms or suicidal thoughts.
Loud snoring, gasping, or major daytime sleepiness suggesting sleep apnea.
Several nights without sleep or near-total insomnia.
support_steps: Set a consistent wake time.
Reduce screens 60 minutes before bed.
Try a 10-minute body scan or slow breathing routine.
If they cannot sleep, encourage getting out of bed briefly instead of forcing it.

topics.title: Grief and loss
core_guidance: Normalize grief as a human response while still taking the pain seriously.
Encourage remembrance rituals, support, and basic daily structure.
Watch for grief that becomes prolonged, rigid, or severely impairing.
Do not force closure or timelines.
red_flags: Persistent desire to die, reunite with the deceased, or join them.
Severe functional impairment that does not improve over time.
Inability to care for self or complete daily tasks.
support_steps: Allow feelings without forcing a timeline.
Keep one or two daily routines stable.
Suggest one gentle check-in with a supportive person today.
Offer remembrance or prayer if that fits their beliefs.

topics.title: Medication information (non-prescriptive)
core_guidance: Provide only general, non-prescriptive information.
Encourage a licensed clinician or pharmacist for decisions and medication changes.
Explain that benefits can take weeks and side effects vary by person.
Never suggest dose changes, substitutions, or stopping suddenly.
red_flags: Severe adverse effects, sudden mood changes, or overdose concerns.
Medication interactions, allergic reactions, or confusion about use.
The person wants to stop or double medication without medical advice.
support_steps: Suggest keeping a symptom log.
Encourage speaking with a clinician or pharmacist promptly.
If safety is uncertain, advise emergency help rather than more self-management.

topics.title: Headache and migraine support
core_guidance: Encourage hydration, rest, and reducing sensory triggers.
Track patterns and triggers without overcomplicating the plan.
Avoid diagnosis; give only general supportive guidance.
Prioritize urgent evaluation when the headache pattern is unusual or severe.
red_flags: Sudden severe headache ('worst of life').
New neurologic symptoms such as weakness, vision loss, confusion, or collapse.
Headache after head injury or with fever and neck stiffness.
support_steps: Dim lights and reduce noise.
Use gentle neck/shoulder relaxation.
Encourage clinical evaluation if recurrent, severe, or changing.
If red flags appear, treat it as urgent rather than routine.

topics.title: Seizure safety support
core_guidance: Provide safety guidance without diagnosis.
Emphasize emergency evaluation for first-time or prolonged events.
Keep the focus on protection, timing, and when to call for help.
red_flags: First-ever seizure or seizure lasting more than 5 minutes.
Breathing difficulty, injury, repeated seizures, or failure to return to baseline.
Seizure with pregnancy, diabetes, or head injury.
support_steps: Place the person on their side if safe.
Protect the head and remove nearby hazards.
Time the event and call emergency services for prolonged or first-time events.
Do not restrain movements or put anything in the mouth.

topics.title: Trauma-related distress
core_guidance: Prioritize immediate safety and present-moment grounding before deeper reflection.
Avoid forcing detailed retelling of traumatic events.
Normalize survival responses while reinforcing current safety.
Encourage trauma-informed professional support when symptoms persist.
red_flags: Dissociation, severe disorientation, or inability to identify present safety.
Self-harm urges, extreme panic, or shutdown states that impair basic functioning.
Escalating nightmares, flashbacks, or persistent hypervigilance with exhaustion.
support_steps: Use orientation: name date, location, and three safe objects nearby.
Ground through cold water, feet-to-floor pressure, and slow exhale breathing.
Reduce stimulation and contact a trusted person for co-regulation.
If safety deteriorates, seek urgent in-person support.

topics.title: Relationship conflict and emotional rupture
core_guidance: Validate emotional injury without escalating blame or retaliation.
Shift from global judgments to one specific issue and one specific request.
Use boundary-centered communication and cooling-off periods before hard talks.
Encourage supportive adults, mediators, or counseling when conflict is persistent.
red_flags: Threats, coercion, stalking, or any fear for personal safety.
Verbal abuse escalating to intimidation or physical risk.
Isolation from social support and severe emotional destabilization.
support_steps: Pause escalation: 20-30 minute cool-down before discussing sensitive topics.
Use one I-statement and one clear boundary without insults.
Write one realistic next step that protects dignity and safety.
If abuse or threat is present, prioritize a safety plan and immediate help.

topics.title: Fever and common infections
core_guidance: A fever is generally the body's immune response; most adult fevers under 39°C resolve with rest and fluids.
Encourage hydration (water, oral saline) and rest as first-line self-care.
For adults, paracetamol or ibuprofen (if not contraindicated) can reduce fever and discomfort — follow the package dose.
Fever lasting more than 3 days, rising above 39.5°C, or accompanied by red flags warrants medical evaluation.
Do not diagnose the infection type — distinguish viral (usually self-limiting) from bacterial (may need antibiotics) only clinically.
red_flags: Fever above 40°C or persistent fever beyond 3 days without improvement.
Severe headache, stiff neck, or sensitivity to light (possible meningitis).
Difficulty breathing, rapid breathing, or bluish lips.
Altered consciousness, extreme confusion, or inability to wake properly.
Fever with rash, severe abdominal pain, or inability to keep fluids down.
support_steps: Rest and drink plenty of fluids — water, dilute oral saline solution.
Use a damp cool cloth on forehead; avoid bundling up if feeling hot.
Take paracetamol per the recommended dose; avoid aspirin in children.
Monitor temperature every few hours and track how long the fever has lasted.
Seek medical care if fever exceeds 39.5°C, lasts more than 3 days, or red flags appear.

topics.title: Chest discomfort and heart palpitations
core_guidance: Chest discomfort has many causes — musculoskeletal, acid reflux, anxiety, or cardiac; never dismiss it.
Any new or unexplained chest pain warrants medical evaluation, especially with exertion, sweating, or arm/jaw pain.
Occasional palpitations are common and often benign (stress, caffeine, fatigue), but persistent or symptomatic palpitations need evaluation.
Encourage the person to describe onset, duration, and associated symptoms clearly to their doctor.
Do not attempt to reassure away chest pain — always err on the side of evaluation.
red_flags: Chest pain with sweating, nausea, shortness of breath, or pain radiating to the arm or jaw — possible heart attack.
Sudden severe chest pain with tearing sensation — possible aortic dissection.
Palpitations with dizziness, fainting, or shortness of breath.
Chest pain in a person with known heart disease or cardiac risk factors.
Any chest pain lasting more than 15 minutes.
support_steps: If cardiac emergency signs are present, call 999 immediately.
Note the exact start time, what was happening, and associated symptoms.
Avoid strenuous activity until evaluated by a doctor.
For palpitations: reduce caffeine, ensure adequate sleep and hydration.
Schedule an urgent medical appointment — do not self-diagnose or dismiss.

topics.title: Cough, cold, and respiratory symptoms
core_guidance: Most upper respiratory infections are viral and self-limiting; antibiotics do not help viral infections.
For dry cough: stay hydrated, use honey in warm water, avoid irritants like smoke.
For productive cough with coloured phlegm lasting more than a week, medical evaluation is advisable.
Asthma symptoms (wheezing, cough, chest tightness) need prescribed medication; encourage adherence.
Encourage rest, fluids, and steam inhalation for congestion relief.
red_flags: Shortness of breath at rest, rapid breathing, or bluish lips.
Coughing up blood or rust-coloured sputum.
High fever with productive cough (possible pneumonia).
Worsening asthma that does not respond to a reliever inhaler.
Cough lasting more than 3 weeks without improvement.
support_steps: Rest and drink warm fluids; honey in warm water can soothe a dry cough.
Use steam inhalation or saline nasal spray for congestion.
Gargle warm salt water for sore throat.
Avoid smoking and secondhand smoke.
Seek medical evaluation if symptoms worsen, persist beyond 10 days, or red flags appear.

topics.title: Digestive and gastrointestinal symptoms
core_guidance: Most acute GI symptoms (nausea, diarrhea) are self-limiting; the main risk is dehydration.
Oral rehydration (water, dilute saline, coconut water) is the most important first step for diarrhea/vomiting.
Indigestion and heartburn often respond to dietary changes, avoiding late meals, and antacids.
Chronic or recurrent GI symptoms need medical evaluation to rule out underlying conditions.
Severe abdominal pain — especially localized, sudden, or with fever — should not be managed at home.
red_flags: Severe or sudden abdominal pain, particularly if localized to one area.
Blood in vomit or stool (red or dark/tarry stools).
Signs of dehydration: no urination for 8+ hours, extreme thirst, sunken eyes, confusion.
Abdominal pain with fever above 38.5°C.
Persistent vomiting unable to keep any fluids down for more than 12 hours.
support_steps: Start with clear fluids — water, dilute oral saline solution, or electrolyte drinks.
Eat bland, easily digestible foods (rice, toast, banana) once nausea settles.
For indigestion: avoid fatty/spicy food, eat smaller meals, and avoid lying down after eating.
Monitor hydration closely — urine should remain pale yellow.
Seek medical care for severe pain, blood in stool/vomit, or inability to keep fluids down.

topics.title: High blood pressure (hypertension)
core_guidance: Hypertension is often asymptomatic; the only way to know is regular monitoring.
Lifestyle changes are first-line: reduce salt, increase physical activity, maintain healthy weight, limit alcohol, quit smoking.
Prescribed antihypertensive medication must not be stopped without medical advice — even if readings normalise.
Encourage regular home monitoring and keeping a log to share with the doctor.
Stress management is an important adjunct to medical treatment.
red_flags: Sudden severe headache with vision changes or confusion (hypertensive emergency).
Chest pain or shortness of breath alongside very high blood pressure.
Sudden weakness, facial drooping, or speech difficulty (stroke signs).
Blood pressure above 180/120 mmHg — seek emergency care.
support_steps: Reduce daily salt intake and processed foods.
30 minutes of moderate exercise most days.
Avoid smoking and limit caffeine.
Take prescribed medication consistently — do not self-adjust doses.
If readings are consistently high, book an urgent medical appointment.

topics.title: Diabetes and blood sugar management
core_guidance: Diabetes management centres on blood glucose control through diet, exercise, medication, and monitoring.
For suspected low blood sugar (hypoglycemia): 15g of fast-acting carbohydrate (sugar, juice, glucose tablet), then recheck in 15 minutes.
For high blood sugar: do not adjust insulin without medical guidance; increase hydration.
Encourage consistent meal timing, carbohydrate awareness, and regular glucose monitoring.
Annual checks for complications (kidney, eyes, feet, nerves) are essential.
red_flags: Severe hypoglycemia with confusion, seizure, or unconsciousness — call 999.
Diabetic ketoacidosis signs: fruity breath, deep rapid breathing, confusion, excessive thirst.
Non-healing wounds, especially on the feet.
Blood sugar consistently above 14 mmol/L despite medication.
Any sudden vision changes, chest pain, or numbness in limbs.
support_steps: For mild low blood sugar: 4-5 glucose tablets or a small glass of fruit juice; recheck after 15 minutes.
Keep a glucose source accessible at all times.
Take prescribed medication consistently; do not skip doses.
Follow a consistent meal schedule with balanced carbohydrates.
Contact your doctor or diabetes care team for persistent high readings or any red flags.

topics.title: Muscle and joint pain
core_guidance: Most acute muscle and joint pain is self-limiting and responds to RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation).
Back pain is very common; most acute episodes resolve in 4–6 weeks with activity and gentle movement.
Avoid prolonged bed rest for back pain — gentle movement aids recovery better than immobilisation.
Paracetamol or NSAIDs (if not contraindicated) help manage pain during recovery.
Persistent, worsening, or neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness) warrant medical evaluation.
red_flags: Back pain with bladder or bowel dysfunction (possible cauda equina — emergency).
Joint swelling with fever (possible septic arthritis — emergency).
Pain after significant trauma or fall in elderly persons.
Persistent night pain, unexplained weight loss, or pain that does not improve with rest.
Numbness, tingling, or weakness radiating down a limb.
support_steps: For acute injury: rest the area, apply ice for 15–20 minutes several times a day.
After 48 hours, gentle warmth and movement help healing.
Paracetamol or ibuprofen (if tolerated) for pain relief; follow package dosing.
Gentle stretching and movement, avoiding the positions that cause sharp pain.
Seek medical evaluation if pain is severe, worsening after 2 weeks, or red flags appear.

topics.title: Skin rashes and conditions
core_guidance: Most rashes are contact reactions, eczema, or heat rash — and respond to simple measures.
Identify and avoid triggers: new soap, detergent, food, plant, or fabric.
Keep skin moisturised for eczema; avoid scratching to prevent infection.
Hives (urticaria) are often allergic reactions — antihistamines help mild cases.
Do not apply strong topical steroids without medical advice.
red_flags: Rash spreading rapidly, especially with fever, difficulty breathing, or swelling of face/lips.
Rash that looks like blood spots under the skin (petechiae/purpura) — can indicate a serious infection.
Infected rash: increasing redness, warmth, pus, or red streaking from the area.
Any skin ulcer or non-healing wound, especially in diabetic patients.
Sudden widespread rash after starting a new medication.
support_steps: Wash the area gently with mild soap and water; pat dry.
Apply a fragrance-free moisturiser for dryness or eczema.
An oral antihistamine can relieve itching for allergic rashes.
Avoid scratching — cut nails short and wear loose cotton clothing.
If rash worsens, spreads rapidly, or red flags appear, seek medical evaluation promptly.

topics.title: Women's health and menstrual wellbeing
core_guidance: Menstrual irregularities and discomfort are very common; many have treatable causes.
Dysmenorrhea (painful periods): NSAIDs like ibuprofen taken at the start of pain are effective; heat also helps.
Heavy or prolonged periods lasting more than 7 days or needing frequent pad changes warrant evaluation.
Vaginal discharge changes in colour, odour, or amount should be assessed by a doctor.
Any pregnancy concerns — missed period, positive test, or unusual symptoms — merit medical consultation.
red_flags: Sudden severe pelvic or abdominal pain (possible ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion).
Very heavy bleeding — soaking more than one pad per hour for several hours.
Fever with pelvic pain and abnormal discharge (possible pelvic inflammatory disease).
Pregnancy with vaginal bleeding or severe pain.
Persistent irregular cycles with weight gain, hair growth, or acne (may indicate PCOS or thyroid issue).
support_steps: Ibuprofen or naproxen taken at the first sign of period pain; apply a heat pad to the lower abdomen.
Track your cycle — apps can help identify patterns and flag irregularities.
Maintain iron intake if periods are heavy to prevent anaemia.
Consult a doctor for persistent pain, heavy bleeding, or changes in discharge.
For pregnancy or fertility questions, seek advice from a qualified obstetrician or gynaecologist.

topics.title: First aid for minor wounds and injuries
core_guidance: For minor cuts: apply direct pressure with a clean cloth for 5–10 minutes; clean with clean water; cover with a sterile bandage.
For minor burns: cool with running water for at least 10 minutes; do not use ice, butter, or toothpaste.
For sprains: RICE — Rest, Ice (wrapped in cloth), Compression (bandage), Elevation.
Wound infection signs (redness spreading, warmth, pus, fever) need medical attention.
For animal bites or deep puncture wounds, medical evaluation for tetanus and infection risk is important.
red_flags: Bleeding that does not stop after 10 minutes of direct pressure.
Deep, gaping wounds that may need stitches.
Burns larger than a palm, burns on face/hands/genitals, or deep burns with white/black tissue.
Suspected broken bone: severe pain, deformity, or inability to bear weight.
Any animal bite, especially from unknown or unvaccinated animals.
support_steps: Cuts: press firmly with a clean cloth, clean with cool water, apply antiseptic, cover with a plaster.
Burns: cool with running water 10+ minutes; cover loosely with cling film or clean cloth; do not burst blisters.
Sprains: rest the joint, apply an ice pack wrapped in cloth for 15–20 min, wrap with a bandage, keep elevated.
Change wound dressings daily and monitor for infection signs.
Seek emergency care for severe bleeding, large burns, suspected fractures, or animal bites.

topics.title: Allergies and immune reactions
core_guidance: Allergies range from mild (hay fever, food intolerance) to life-threatening (anaphylaxis).
Antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) are effective first-line for mild allergic symptoms.
Identifying and avoiding triggers is the most effective long-term strategy.
Food allergies and intolerances differ — allergy involves immune response; intolerance is digestive.
Encourage those with known severe allergies to carry an adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen).
red_flags: Swelling of the throat, difficulty swallowing, or breathing difficulty after exposure — anaphylaxis.
Widespread hives with rapid progression, dizziness, or drop in blood pressure.
Allergic reaction that worsens rapidly despite antihistamine use.
First severe allergic reaction — needs medical evaluation to assess risk.
support_steps: For mild symptoms: oral antihistamine; cool compress for itchy skin or eyes.
Identify the trigger and avoid re-exposure.
For hay fever: use nasal corticosteroid spray and antihistamines; keep windows closed during high pollen.
Keep a food diary to identify food triggers.
For known severe allergy: always carry an EpiPen and wear a medical alert bracelet; seek emergency care immediately if exposed.

topics.title: Nutrition, hydration, and general wellness
core_guidance: A balanced diet with vegetables, fruits, whole grains, lean protein, and healthy fats supports overall health.
Adequate hydration (approximately 8 glasses per day, more in heat or exercise) is foundational.
Unexplained persistent fatigue warrants investigation — iron deficiency, thyroid issues, and vitamin D deficiency are common causes.
Avoid extreme or restrictive diets without medical supervision.
Regular physical activity (150 minutes moderate per week) benefits nearly every health condition.
red_flags: Severe unintentional weight loss without dietary change.
Persistent extreme fatigue not explained by lifestyle factors.
Signs of severe nutritional deficiency: hair loss, brittle nails, frequent infections, confusion.
Eating disorders with extreme restriction, purging, or binging patterns.
support_steps: Fill half the plate with vegetables and fruit; a quarter with whole grains; a quarter with protein.
Drink water consistently through the day rather than waiting until thirsty.
Get blood tests for iron, vitamin D, and thyroid if fatigue is persistent.
Build physical activity into the daily routine — even short walks count.
Consult a registered dietitian for personalized nutrition guidance.

topics.title: OTC pain and fever medications
core_guidance: Paracetamol (Napa/Ace in BD): 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours; max 4000 mg/day. Safe for most adults, pregnancy, and children. Take with or without food.
Ibuprofen (Brufen/Nurofen): 200–400 mg every 4–6 hours with food; max 1200 mg/day OTC. Avoid with stomach ulcers, kidney disease, last trimester of pregnancy, or on blood thinners.
Mefenamic acid (Ponstan): 500 mg every 8 hours with food; max 3 days for acute pain. Especially effective for period pain (dysmenorrhea). Not for kidney/liver disease.

Aspirin: 300–600 mg every 4–6 hours for adults. NEVER give to children under 16 (Reye's syndrome risk). Good for headache/mild pain in adults. Also used 75–100 mg daily as a blood thinner (doctor-prescribed).
For fever: paracetamol is safest first choice. If fever is above 39°C and paracetamol is insufficient, alternate with ibuprofen every 4 hours.
Avoid taking paracetamol and other paracetamol-containing products (many cold/flu combos contain it) simultaneously — total dose must stay under 4000 mg/day.
red_flags: Paracetamol overdose (even accidental): nausea, vomiting, right-side abdominal pain — medical emergency.
Stomach bleeding with ibuprofen: black tarry stools, vomiting blood — stop immediately and seek care.
Fever above 40°C or lasting more than 3 days despite medication.
Pain that is severe, worsening, or accompanied by other concerning symptoms.
support_steps: For fever/mild pain: Paracetamol 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours; max 4000 mg/day.
For period pain or musculoskeletal pain: Mefenamic acid 500 mg 3x daily with food, or Ibuprofen 400 mg 3x daily with food.
Children's paracetamol: 10–15 mg/kg every 4–6 hours. Syrup 120 mg/5 ml (for infants) or 250 mg/5 ml (for older children).
Do not combine two NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen + mefenamic acid) — increased GI and kidney risk.
If one painkiller is not working well after 2–3 days, see a doctor rather than escalating doses.

topics.title: OTC cold, allergy, and antihistamine medications
core_guidance: Cetirizine (Zetid/Alatrol/Histac in BD): 10 mg once daily. Non-drowsy in most people. Good for hay fever, allergic rhinitis, hives, allergic skin reactions. Children 2–5 years: 2.5 mg; 6–11 years: 5 mg.
Loratadine: 10 mg once daily. Non-drowsy. Safe in pregnancy (second trimester onwards if needed). For allergic rhinitis, hives.
Fexofenadine (Allegra): 120 mg once or 180 mg once daily. Completely non-drowsy. Ideal for people who need to drive or work.
Chlorphenamine (Piriton): 4 mg every 4–6 hours. Causes drowsiness — useful if itching disrupts sleep, but avoid driving. Children: 2 mg for under-12.

For nasal congestion with allergy: saline nasal spray (plain salt water) is safe for all ages. Xylometazoline/Oxymetazoline decongestant sprays (Otrivin) give fast relief but use max 3–5 days only — longer use causes rebound congestion.

Combination cold/flu tablets (e.g., Decolgen, Sinarest) typically contain paracetamol + decongestant + antihistamine — effective for multi-symptom colds but do not add more paracetamol alongside them.
red_flags: Antihistamines causing severe drowsiness, confusion, or urinary retention in elderly patients — switch to non-drowsy option.
Decongestant sprays used for more than 5 days — causes worsening congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).
Allergic symptoms rapidly worsening or affecting breathing — possible anaphylaxis, call 999.
support_steps: For everyday allergy/hay fever: Cetirizine 10 mg once daily (morning) or Loratadine 10 mg daily.
For itchy rash or hives disrupting sleep: Chlorphenamine 4 mg at night.
For nasal congestion: saline spray freely; Otrivin decongestant spray max 3–5 days.
For cold with multiple symptoms: one combination tablet (Decolgen) covers most — check total paracetamol dose.
Non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) are preferred for daytime use.

topics.title: OTC stomach, digestive, and GI medications
core_guidance: Antacids (Magnesium/Aluminium hydroxide — Maalox, Mylanta): chew 1–2 tablets or 10 ml liquid 20–60 minutes after meals and at bedtime. Immediate but short-lived relief for acidity, heartburn, indigestion.
Omeprazole (Losec, Omidon in BD): 20 mg once daily before breakfast. For GERD, peptic ulcer, gastritis. Effect builds over 2–3 days. Use 4–8 week courses; not for indefinite self-treatment.
Pantoprazole: 40 mg once daily before meals. Same uses as omeprazole, slightly different profile.
Domperidone (Domstal): 10 mg 3 times daily, 15–30 minutes before meals. For nausea, vomiting, bloating, gastric stasis. Avoid in people with heart rhythm problems.
Metoclopramide (Plasil): 10 mg 3 times daily. For nausea/vomiting. Can cause drowsiness and restlessness — short courses only.

Loperamide (Imodium): 2 mg after first loose stool, then 2 mg after each subsequent stool; max 8–16 mg/day. For non-infectious diarrhea. Do NOT use if there is fever with bloody diarrhea — infection needs treatment, not slowing.
ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts — Saline): dissolve one sachet in 250 ml clean water. Drink after every loose stool. Most important treatment for diarrhea in all ages.

Metronidazole (Flagyl/Metro): 400–500 mg 3 times daily for 5–7 days for amoebic dysentery, Giardia, or anaerobic bacterial infections. Take with food. Avoid alcohol completely during course. Technically prescription, but widely available OTC in Bangladesh.
red_flags: Diarrhea with high fever, blood/mucus in stool, or severe dehydration — needs medical evaluation.
Vomiting blood or black tarry stools — GI bleeding emergency.
Heartburn with difficulty swallowing, weight loss, or persistent despite 2 weeks of treatment.
Metronidazole + alcohol causes severe vomiting reaction — never combine.
support_steps: Acidity/heartburn: antacid after meals for quick relief; Omeprazole 20 mg before breakfast for sustained control.
Nausea/vomiting: Domperidone 10 mg before meals (preferred) or Metoclopramide 10 mg 3x daily.
Diarrhea: ORS after every stool is the most critical step. Loperamide for convenience when stools are frequent but no fever/blood.
Suspected amoeba or Giardia (chronic diarrhea, travel history): Metronidazole 400 mg 3x daily for 5 days.
Heartburn persisting more than 2 weeks on omeprazole, or with alarming symptoms — see a doctor for investigation.

topics.title: OTC topical and skin medications
core_guidance: Clotrimazole cream 1% (Candid, Funazole in BD): apply twice daily for ringworm, athlete's foot, jock itch, candida/thrush. Use for minimum 2–4 weeks, continue 1 week after visible clearance. Also in vaginal form for vaginal thrush (1 applicator nightly for 6 nights).
Terbinafine cream 1% (Lamisil): apply once daily for fungal infections. Faster and more effective than clotrimazole for dermatophytes (ringworm, athlete's foot). Use 1–2 weeks.

Hydrocortisone 1% cream: apply twice daily for mild eczema, contact dermatitis, insect bite reactions, mild allergic rash. Use max 7 days on the face; do not use on infected skin. Not for children under 10 without advice.
Betamethasone (Betnovate — stronger steroid): effective for moderate-severe eczema/psoriasis patches. Use sparingly. Do not use long-term on the face or skin folds — causes skin thinning.
Mupirocin ointment (Bactroban): apply 3 times daily for small infected wounds, impetigo, infected eczema. Excellent for Staphylococcal skin infections.
Povidone-iodine (Betadine): antiseptic for wound cleaning. Apply to clean wound twice daily; do not use on large wounds or for more than 2 weeks.
Calamine lotion: soothing for chickenpox rash, insect bites, mild sunburn, itchy rash. Apply freely as needed.
Benzoyl peroxide 2.5–5% (Clean & Clear, OXY): apply once nightly for acne. Start low (2.5%) to minimize dryness. Keep away from eyes/mouth. Takes 4–6 weeks for noticeable improvement.
red_flags: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, or red streaking from a wound — bacterial infection needs oral antibiotics.
Fungal infection not responding after 4 weeks of correct antifungal treatment.
Skin condition rapidly worsening, spreading, or with fever.
Long-term steroid cream causing skin thinning, stretch marks, or rebound flare on stopping.
support_steps: Fungal skin infections: Terbinafine once daily (preferred) or Clotrimazole twice daily — complete the full course.
Mild eczema/allergic rash: Hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily + fragrance-free moisturiser.
Small infected wound: clean with saline/Betadine, then Mupirocin ointment 3x daily, covered with a plaster.
Acne: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5% nightly — be consistent for 4–6 weeks before judging results.
If skin infection is spreading or not responding to topical treatment in 3–5 days, oral antibiotics are needed — see a doctor.

topics.title: Antibiotic guidance — when to use and what
core_guidance: Antibiotics treat BACTERIAL infections only. They have NO effect on viral infections (common cold, flu, most sore throats, most coughs). Misuse drives resistance.
Common conditions that DO NOT need antibiotics: common cold, viral sore throat (most), flu, most coughs, most diarrhea (unless bacterial/amoebic confirmed).

Conditions that often DO need antibiotics (and a prescription): bacterial pneumonia, confirmed strep throat, bacterial UTI, severe skin infections (cellulitis), ear infections in young children, typhoid, tuberculosis.
Amoxicillin (Moxacil/Amoclav): for strep throat, ear infections, chest infections. 500 mg 3x daily for 5–7 days. Not for penicillin allergy.
Azithromycin (Zithromax/Azimic in BD): for atypical pneumonia, chlamydia, mild community pneumonia. 500 mg once daily for 3–5 days. Useful in penicillin allergy.
Ciprofloxacin (Cipro/Ciprox in BD): for UTI, Salmonella/typhoid, traveler's diarrhea. 500 mg twice daily. Not first-choice for respiratory infections. Avoid with antacids (space by 2 hours).
Cotrimoxazole (Bactrim/Septran): for UTI, some skin infections, Pneumocystis pneumonia. 960 mg twice daily. Widely available; resistance common in UTI in Bangladesh — culture first when possible.
Complete the full course: stopping antibiotics early when feeling better drives resistance and allows relapse.

For antibiotics in Bangladesh: prescriptions are technically required but many pharmacies dispense without. It is still strongly advisable to get a doctor's guidance, especially for serious infections.
red_flags: Severe allergic reaction to an antibiotic: rash, tongue swelling, difficulty breathing — stop immediately, call 999.
Symptoms worsening after 48–72 hours on antibiotics — the antibiotic may not cover the organism; see a doctor.
High fever with chills, confusion, or rapid deterioration — possible sepsis — emergency care.
C. difficile diarrhea (watery, frequent, foul-smelling after antibiotic use) — medical evaluation needed.
support_steps: Before recommending or starting antibiotics: determine if infection is likely bacterial (purulent sputum, bacterial UTI symptoms, skin infection) vs viral (watery discharge, started as typical cold).
For suspected bacterial throat infection: Amoxicillin 500 mg 3x daily for 7 days (or Azithromycin 500 mg daily for 5 days if penicillin-allergic).
For UTI: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 3–5 days (women) or 7 days (men); or Cotrimoxazole 960 mg twice daily for 3–5 days.
For mild skin infection / cellulitis: Flucloxacillin 500 mg 4x daily (or Cloxacillin in BD) for 5–7 days.
Always complete the full prescribed course, even if feeling better after 2–3 days.

topics.title: Vitamins, minerals, and supplements
core_guidance: Vitamin D3: 1000–2000 IU daily for prevention; 4000–5000 IU daily for 8–12 weeks to treat confirmed deficiency; then maintenance 1000–2000 IU. Take with a fatty meal for best absorption. Most people in Bangladesh are deficient due to limited sun exposure.

Iron (Ferrous sulfate 200 mg = 65 mg elemental iron): 200 mg 2–3 times daily for iron deficiency anemia. Take on empty stomach or with Vitamin C for better absorption. Causes dark/black stools (harmless) and constipation. Continue for 3–6 months after normalisation. Common brands in BD: Ferrolex, Ferrotab.

Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin/methylcobalamin): 500–1000 mcg daily for oral supplementation of deficiency. Common in vegetarians, people with gastric issues, or those on metformin. Also available as IM injection (1000 mcg monthly) for severe deficiency with neurological symptoms.
Folic acid: 400 mcg daily (all women of childbearing age); 5 mg daily (prescribed, for women planning pregnancy or with risk factors). Essential for neural tube defect prevention.
Zinc: 10–20 mg elemental zinc daily for deficiency; standard doses in children's diarrhea management (20 mg/day for 10–14 days). Present in many ORS-zinc combination sachets in Bangladesh.

Calcium + Vitamin D: 500–600 mg elemental calcium twice daily (with meals) + Vitamin D3 for osteoporosis prevention in post-menopausal women and elderly. Do not take more than 600 mg at once — absorption drops.
Vitamin C: 500–1000 mg daily; modest immune support, enhances iron absorption. Megadoses (>2000 mg) can cause kidney stones in susceptible people.
Omega-3 (fish oil): 1–2 g EPA+DHA daily for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefit. Safe with most medications. Best taken with meals to reduce fishy reflux.
red_flags: Severe anaemia (Hb <7 g/dL), confusion, or extreme weakness — may need IV iron or blood transfusion urgently.
Vitamin B12 deficiency with tingling, numbness, or balance problems — neurological damage; oral supplementation may be insufficient; injections needed.
Vitamin D toxicity (very rare, only from very high prolonged doses): nausea, kidney stones, hypercalcaemia — get blood levels checked if taking >5000 IU for months.
Iron overdose in children is dangerous — keep iron tablets away from children.
support_steps: For fatigue + suspected iron deficiency: Ferrous sulfate 200 mg twice daily with orange juice (Vitamin C improves absorption). Check blood count before and after.
For most adults in Bangladesh: Vitamin D3 1000–2000 IU daily is reasonable maintenance.
For women planning pregnancy: start Folic acid 400–800 mcg at least 1 month before conception and continue through first trimester.
For children with diarrhea: Zinc 20 mg daily for 10–14 days alongside ORS reduces severity and duration.
Get blood levels (Vitamin D, B12, ferritin/Hb) before high-dose supplementation to confirm deficiency.

topics.title: Children's health and pediatric dosing
core_guidance: Paracetamol for children: 10–15 mg/kg every 4–6 hours; max 4 doses in 24 hours. Syrup: 120 mg/5 ml (infants under 2), 250 mg/5 ml (2–12 years). Suppositories available for vomiting children.
Ibuprofen for children (over 3 months and over 5 kg): 5–10 mg/kg every 6–8 hours with food. Syrup: 100 mg/5 ml. Do not use in children under 3 months.
For high fever in children: alternate paracetamol and ibuprofen every 3–4 hours if one alone doesn't control it well.
Cetirizine for children: 2–5 years: 2.5 mg once daily; 6–11 years: 5 mg once daily; 12+: 10 mg. Syrup: 5 mg/5 ml.
ORS for diarrhea in children: 50–100 ml after each loose stool in infants; 100–200 ml in older children. Continue breast-feeding and age-appropriate feeding throughout diarrhea.
Zinc for children's diarrhea: 10 mg/day for infants under 6 months; 20 mg/day for children 6 months–5 years; for 10–14 days.
Children's cough: avoid cough suppressants in children under 6. Honey (1/2 tsp, not for under-1-year) and warm fluids are safer and effective.
Fever in newborns under 3 months is always a medical emergency — do not manage at home, go to hospital.
red_flags: Fever in infant under 3 months — always urgent medical evaluation.
Child with fever and stiff neck, sensitivity to light, or purpuric rash — possible meningitis, call 999.
Child not responsive, floppy, or refusing all fluids for more than 8 hours.
Rapid or laboured breathing, grunting, or flaring nostrils — respiratory distress.
Child with high fever + febrile seizure: stay calm, protect from injury, time the seizure, go to hospital.
support_steps: Child fever: Paracetamol syrup 10–15 mg/kg per dose. Give every 4–6 hours as needed; lukewarm sponging if above 39°C.
Child diarrhea: ORS sachet dissolved in 250 ml clean water — give small sips frequently after every loose stool.
Child allergy/runny nose: Cetirizine syrup (5 mg/5 ml) — half teaspoon (2.5 mg) for children 2–5 years.
Teething discomfort: chilled (not frozen) teething ring, gentle gum massage. Paracetamol if clearly distressed.
Any doubt about a child's medication dose — double-check by weight (mg/kg) not just age.

topics.title: Eye and ear infections and care
core_guidance: Bacterial conjunctivitis: red eye with thick yellow/green discharge, matted shut in morning. Use Chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops (Chloroptic) — 1–2 drops every 2 hours for 2 days, then 4 times daily for 5 days. Or Gentamicin 0.3% eye drops 4x daily for 5–7 days.
Viral conjunctivitis: watery discharge, often follows cold. No antibiotic needed — resolves in 1–2 weeks. Saline eye wash, cool compresses, artificial tears for comfort.
Allergic conjunctivitis: itching is the main symptom. Antihistamine eye drops (Olopatadine/Ketotifen) or oral cetirizine 10 mg daily.

Ear infection (otitis media) in children: often presents with ear pain, fever, irritability. Most mild cases in children over 2 years resolve without antibiotics in 2–3 days. Paracetamol/ibuprofen for pain. If not improving or severe: Amoxicillin 500 mg 3x daily (or weight-based in children) for 5–7 days.
Swimmer's ear (otitis externa): Acetic acid/aluminium acetate ear drops or Ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone ear drops. Keep ear dry. Avoid cotton buds.
Ear wax blockage: soften with olive oil or sodium bicarbonate drops (Waxsol) twice daily for 3–5 days; ear syringing by a nurse or pharmacist can clear it.
red_flags: Sudden vision loss, severe eye pain, or eye injury — emergency ophthalmology.
Eye infection with contact lens use — remove lenses immediately, ophthalmology review needed.
Ear infection with mastoid pain (behind the ear), facial weakness, or dizziness — possible complications, urgent care.
Conjunctivitis in a newborn — always urgent evaluation.
support_steps: Bacterial eye infection: Chloramphenicol 0.5% drops every 2 hours for 2 days then 4x daily; wash hands before applying; use separate drops/cloth for each eye.
Viral conjunctivitis: saline wash, cool compress, artificial tears; stay off school/work for 48 hours to reduce spread.
Ear pain while awaiting treatment: Paracetamol or Ibuprofen for pain; warm cloth held over the ear for comfort.
Ear wax: Waxsol/olive oil drops for 3–5 nights, then gentle irrigation. Never use cotton buds inside the ear canal.
If bacterial conjunctivitis not improved after 2 days of drops, or if ear infection persists beyond 72 hours with worsening pain, see a doctor.

topics.title: Dental pain and oral health
core_guidance: Toothache: Ibuprofen 400 mg every 4–6 hours is most effective for dental pain (better than paracetamol alone). Combine with Paracetamol 500 mg for stronger pain control. Clove oil (eugenol) on the affected area can give temporary relief.

Dental abscess: presents with severe throbbing pain, facial swelling, and possibly fever. This needs urgent dental treatment. While waiting: Metronidazole 400 mg 3x daily + Amoxicillin 500 mg 3x daily for 5 days covers the polymicrobial infection. Antibiotics alone will NOT resolve the abscess — drainage needed.

Mouth ulcers (aphthous): apply Triamcinolone acetonide paste (Kenalog in Orabase) 3–4x daily, or use Benzocaine gel (Bonjela) for pain. Chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash twice daily accelerates healing. Most resolve in 7–14 days.

Gum bleeding (gingivitis): aggressive but gentle twice-daily brushing with a soft brush + flossing. Chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash twice daily for 2 weeks. Vitamin C deficiency can cause gum bleeding — ensure adequate intake.
Sensitive teeth: use Sensodyne or Colgate Sensitive toothpaste twice daily and rub on sensitive area after brushing. Avoid acidic food/drinks.
Wisdom tooth pain: Ibuprofen 400 mg for pain. Warm salt water gargle to keep clean. Surgical extraction usually needed eventually — see a dentist.
red_flags: Dental abscess with facial swelling spreading to the neck or floor of the mouth — life-threatening Ludwig's angina, call 999.
Difficulty swallowing, speaking, or breathing from dental/jaw swelling — emergency.
Mouth ulcer not healing after 3 weeks — needs investigation to rule out serious causes.
High fever with dental pain and facial swelling — spreading infection.
support_steps: Toothache: Ibuprofen 400 mg every 6 hours; apply clove oil carefully on the tooth. Book urgent dentist appointment.
Dental abscess: start Metronidazole 400 mg + Amoxicillin 500 mg both 3x daily for 5 days; dentist urgently for drainage.
Mouth ulcers: apply Bonjela/Kenalog paste directly; Chlorhexidine mouthwash twice daily; avoid spicy/acidic food.
Gum bleeding: soft brush twice daily + floss + chlorhexidine rinse for 2 weeks; reassess at dentist.
All dental pain ultimately requires dental assessment — antibiotics and pain relief are bridging measures.

topics.title: Drug interactions and medication safety
core_guidance: Paracetamol + Ibuprofen: SAFE to take together — they work via different mechanisms and can be alternated or combined for better pain/fever control.
Ibuprofen/NSAIDs + Blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel): SIGNIFICANT interaction — increased bleeding risk. Avoid NSAIDs; use paracetamol instead.
Antibiotics + Antacids/Calcium: Ciprofloxacin and Tetracyclines are significantly REDUCED by calcium, magnesium, iron, or antacids. Space them by at least 2 hours.
Metronidazole (Flagyl) + Alcohol: SEVERE reaction — flushing, severe vomiting, rapid heartbeat. No alcohol during treatment AND for 48 hours after.

Warfarin + Many drugs: Warfarin levels are affected by antibiotics (raise levels), NSAIDs (bleeding risk), and many herbal supplements. If on warfarin, always check any new medication with a doctor or pharmacist.

Metformin: taken with or after food to minimise GI side effects. Hold dose if undergoing contrast dye imaging procedure. Side effects: nausea, diarrhea (usually settle in first few weeks), rarely lactic acidosis.
Statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin): take at night. Side effects: muscle aches (myalgia) — if severe, stop and contact doctor. Avoid grapefruit juice with some statins (simvastatin, lovastatin).
SSRIs/Antidepressants: do not stop suddenly — taper under doctor supervision. Common side effects include nausea, sleep changes, and sexual dysfunction (usually improve over time).
OCP (oral contraceptive pill): efficacy reduced by rifampicin and some anticonvulsants. Broad-spectrum antibiotics historically flagged but evidence is weak — double contraception if concerned.
red_flags: Any signs of internal bleeding (black stools, blood in urine, unexplained bruising) when on blood thinners + NSAIDs.
Severe muscle pain or weakness on statins — possible rhabdomyolysis, stop statin and go to doctor.
Serotonin syndrome: agitation, tremor, rapid heart rate, high temperature after combining multiple serotonergic drugs.
support_steps: Before combining any two medications: ask the pharmacist — they have drug interaction databases and can check in minutes.
Keep an up-to-date list of all medications (including OTC and supplements) to show doctors and pharmacists.
Paracetamol + Ibuprofen together: safe and more effective for pain than either alone.
Metronidazole: zero alcohol — not even cooking with wine or alcohol-containing foods.
If a new medication is started and unexpected symptoms appear within days (rash, nausea, muscle pain), consider the new drug as a possible cause and contact a doctor.

topics.title: CPR — Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation
core_guidance: Call 999 first — put on speakerphone so you can keep doing CPR.
Hands-only CPR: place heel of hand on centre of chest (lower half of breastbone), interlock hands, arms straight — push hard and fast at 100–120/min, at least 5–6 cm depth.
Allow full chest recoil between compressions; do not lean on the chest.
For trained responders: 30 compressions to 2 rescue breaths (head tilt, chin lift, seal, 1-second breath).
Hands-only CPR is equally effective for adults in the first few minutes — do not delay compressions.
AED: apply as soon as available, follow voice prompts, resume CPR immediately after shock.
Do not stop until paramedics take over, the person starts breathing, or you are physically unable to continue.
red_flags: Gasping ('agonal breathing') is not normal breathing — begin CPR.
Do not waste time checking for pulse if the person is unresponsive and not breathing normally.
Every minute without CPR reduces survival by 7–10%.
support_steps: Position: firm flat surface, kneel beside chest.
Hand position: heel of one hand on lower half of breastbone, other hand on top, fingers off the chest.
Compressions: hard (5–6 cm), fast (100–120/min), full recoil, no leaning.
If trained: 30 compressions → 2 rescue breaths. Repeat.
Use AED as soon as available; continue CPR while it charges.

topics.title: Choking — airway obstruction first aid
core_guidance: If person can cough forcefully: encourage coughing — do not interfere.
If they cannot cough, speak, or breathe: give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand, leaning them forward.
If back blows fail: 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) — stand behind, fist above navel, sharp inward-upward thrusts.
Alternate 5 back blows + 5 abdominal thrusts until object clears or person collapses.
If unconscious: start CPR — look in mouth before each rescue breath and remove any visible object.
Infant under 1 year: never use abdominal thrusts. Hold face-down, 5 back blows; turn face-up, 5 chest pushes (2 fingers on breastbone); alternate.
Call 999 if object does not clear within 2–3 cycles.
red_flags: Silent choking (cannot cough, speak, or make noise) is more dangerous than noisy choking.
Cyanosis (bluish lips/face) means severe hypoxia — act with maximum urgency.
Even after successful removal, anyone who had a significant choking episode should be evaluated for internal injury from abdominal thrusts.
support_steps: Encourage cough if able. If not: lean forward, 5 back blows between shoulder blades.
5 abdominal thrusts: stand behind, hands just above navel, sharp inward-upward thrust.
Alternate back blows and abdominal thrusts. Check mouth after each set.
Infant: face-down back blows → face-up chest thrusts. Alternate. No abdominal thrusts.
If unresponsive: CPR, look in airway before breaths.

topics.title: Severe bleeding — haemorrhage control
core_guidance: Direct pressure is the most effective first-line haemorrhage control — press hard with clean cloth, do not lift to check for minimum 10 minutes.
Add layers on top if blood soaks through — do not remove the first layer.
Elevate the bleeding limb above heart level when possible.
Tourniquet for life-threatening limb bleeding: apply 5–7 cm above wound, tighten until bleeding stops, note time — do not remove.
Do not remove embedded objects (glass, knives) — pack tightly around them.
Nosebleed: lean forward (not back), pinch soft part of nose firmly for 10–15 minutes continuously, cold compress to bridge.
Seek emergency care for bleeding that does not stop within 10–15 minutes, suspected arterial injury, or signs of shock.
red_flags: Signs of haemorrhagic shock: pale/cold/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion, loss of consciousness.
Pulsatile (spurting) bright red blood indicates arterial injury — tourniquet needed immediately.
Nosebleed after head injury requires emergency evaluation.
Any uncontrolled bleeding in a person on anticoagulants (warfarin, rivaroxaban) is an emergency.
support_steps: Direct pressure: hard, continuous, 10 minutes minimum. No peeking.
Tourniquet: 5–7 cm above wound, tight enough to stop bleeding, note time written on skin.
Elevate limb above heart level if possible.
Nosebleed: forward lean, pinch soft part, 10–15 minutes, cold compress.
If signs of shock or uncontrolled bleeding: call 999 and keep person lying down, legs slightly raised.

topics.title: Anaphylaxis — severe allergic reaction
core_guidance: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening — act within seconds to minutes.
EpiPen: press firmly to outer thigh through clothing, hold 10 seconds until click and release.
Position: sit upright for breathing difficulty; lay flat with legs raised if pale/faint; recovery position if unconscious.
Give a second EpiPen 5–15 minutes after the first if symptoms return or do not improve.
Call 999 even if EpiPen given — all anaphylaxis requires hospital observation for biphasic reaction (can occur 4–12 hours later).
Antihistamines and steroids are NOT adequate for anaphylaxis — adrenaline is the only life-saving treatment.
Remove the trigger if identifiable (e.g., remove a sting).
red_flags: Stridor (high-pitched breathing noise), severe wheeze, or inability to swallow.
Loss of consciousness with anaphylaxis — CPR if not breathing.
Reaction without EpiPen available — call 999 and keep absolutely still.
support_steps: EpiPen: outer thigh, through clothing, press firmly and hold 10 seconds.
Sit upright (if breathing trouble) or lie flat legs raised (if pale/dizzy).
Second EpiPen if needed after 5–15 min.
Call 999 even after successful EpiPen use.
Do not stand up or walk — cardiovascular collapse risk.

topics.title: Stroke — FAST recognition and first response
core_guidance: FAST: Face (one side drooping), Arms (one arm drifts down), Speech (slurred/absent), Time (call 999 NOW).
Note the exact time symptoms started — thrombolysis (clot-busting drug) window is 4.5 hours; thrombectomy up to 24 hours for eligible patients.
Do not give food, water, or oral medication — swallowing is often impaired after stroke.
Keep the person calm and still; do not move unless in immediate danger.
Other signs: sudden severe headache ('worst of my life'), sudden vision loss in one eye, sudden loss of balance.
Do NOT let the person sleep it off — strokes worsen without urgent treatment.
If unconscious and not breathing: start CPR.
red_flags: Every minute of stroke = 1.9 million neurons lost.
Symptoms that resolve within an hour may be a TIA (mini-stroke) — still requires emergency evaluation; 10% have a full stroke within 2 days.
Young people can have strokes — do not dismiss symptoms based on age.
support_steps: FAST check: Face, Arms, Speech — any positive → call 999 immediately.
Note exact time symptoms started — tell paramedics.
Nothing by mouth — no food, water, or medicine.
Keep calm and still, comfortable position.
If unconscious and not breathing: CPR.

topics.title: Burns — immediate treatment
core_guidance: Cool the burn: cool (not cold) running water for a minimum of 10 minutes — start within 3 hours; this is the most effective immediate intervention.
Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, egg white, or any household substance — these cause further damage and infection.
Remove clothing and jewellery near the burn but NOT if they are stuck to the skin.
Cover with cling film (loosely, do not wrap tightly) or a clean non-fluffy cloth.
Do not burst blisters — they protect against infection.
Stop, Drop, Roll for clothing on fire — smother with a blanket.
Chemical burns: brush off dry chemicals first, then 20 minutes continuous water flush; remove contaminated clothing carefully.
red_flags: Burns larger than 3 cm, or any size on face/hands/feet/genitals/over joints — all require hospital evaluation.
Deep burns (white, brown, or black tissue, painless centre) — always hospital.
All chemical and electrical burns — require evaluation regardless of appearance.
Burns in children, elderly, or anyone with diabetes/vascular disease.
Inhalation injury: singed nose hairs, hoarse voice, soot in mouth — airway emergency.
support_steps: Cool running water for 10+ minutes (not ice, not cold water bath for large burns).
Remove jewellery/loose clothing near burn — not if adhered to skin.
Cover with cling film or clean non-fluffy cloth.
Do not pop blisters.
Go to hospital for any burn larger than a postage stamp, on face/hands/genitals, deep, or chemical/electrical.

topics.title: Recovery position and unconscious patient care
keywords: how to put someone in recovery position
core_guidance: The recovery position is for unconscious people who ARE breathing — it keeps the airway open and prevents choking on vomit or blood.
Steps: arm at right angle → far hand to cheek → pull up far knee → roll onto side → adjust head back slightly to open airway.
Monitor breathing continuously — if breathing stops, start CPR immediately.
Never leave an unconscious person alone.
Call 999 for any unconscious person — even if breathing normally.
For fainting: lay flat, raise legs above heart level (unless head/neck injury suspected); loosen tight clothing; do not sit them up immediately.
If no breathing after fainting: start CPR.
red_flags: Unconscious person NOT breathing normally — this is cardiac arrest, start CPR.
Suspected spinal injury (fallen from height, diving accident, vehicle crash): keep head and neck still — modified log-roll only if airway must be opened.
Unconsciousness that does not resolve within 1–2 minutes — emergency evaluation even if breathing.
support_steps: Check response (shout, tap shoulders), check breathing (look, listen, feel — 10 seconds).
If breathing: recovery position. Call 999.
If NOT breathing: 30 compressions + 2 breaths (CPR). Call 999.
Recovery position steps: near arm out → far hand to cheek → pull bent knee → roll → head tilted back.
Keep monitoring breathing every 2 minutes until help arrives.

topics.title: ADHD and attention difficulties
core_guidance: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition — not laziness or poor parenting; validate the person's experience.
Core features: inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity, varying by subtype (inattentive, hyperactive, combined).
Non-medication strategies: structured routines, time-blocking, written task lists, body-doubling, regular physical activity.
Medication (methylphenidate, amphetamines, atomoxetine) is effective but requires psychiatric/specialist assessment.
Co-occurring anxiety, depression, and sleep problems are common — address these alongside ADHD.
red_flags: Severe impairment in school, work, or relationships despite strategies.
Co-occurring depression, anxiety, or substance use needing separate treatment.
Adult ADHD newly suspected — formal assessment needed for diagnosis and medication access.
support_steps: Break tasks into 15–25 minute chunks with built-in breaks (Pomodoro technique).
Use external reminders (phone alarms, visible sticky notes) rather than relying on memory.
Physical exercise for 20–30 minutes daily reduces ADHD symptoms significantly.
Reduce environmental distractions — noise-cancelling headphones, single-task focus.
Seek assessment from a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist for formal diagnosis and treatment options.

topics.title: OCD and intrusive thoughts
core_guidance: OCD involves intrusive thoughts (obsessions) and repetitive behaviours (compulsions) performed to reduce anxiety — but compulsions strengthen OCD long-term.
Intrusive thoughts are not wishes or intentions — they are ego-dystonic and cause distress, which distinguishes OCD from dangerous intent.
Gold-standard treatment: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) therapy — face the feared thought or situation without performing the compulsion.
SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine) at higher doses than depression are effective — effect takes 8–12 weeks.
Reassurance-seeking is a compulsion — avoid providing repeated reassurance.
red_flags: OCD thoughts involving harm to self or others — assess intent carefully; most OCD thoughts are ego-dystonic (unwanted), not plans.
Severe OCD causing inability to leave home, eat, or function.
OCD plus depression or suicidal thoughts — prioritise safety.
support_steps: Label the thought: 'This is an OCD thought, not a fact or desire.'
Delay the compulsion by 5 minutes — gradually extend the delay over days.
Do not seek reassurance from others — this feeds the OCD cycle.
ERP therapy with a trained therapist is the most effective treatment — seek referral.
SSRI medication alongside ERP gives the best outcomes for moderate-severe OCD.

topics.title: Social anxiety disorder
core_guidance: Social anxiety is fear of negative evaluation in social situations — far beyond shyness; it significantly impairs daily functioning.
Core distortion: overestimating the probability and severity of negative judgement by others.
CBT with social exposure exercises is the most effective treatment.
SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram) or SNRIs reduce social anxiety; beta-blockers (propranolol) help performance anxiety acutely.
Avoidance maintains and worsens social anxiety — gradual exposure is essential.
red_flags: Social anxiety leading to complete isolation, job loss, or inability to attend school.
Alcohol or substance use to manage social situations.
Co-occurring depression or suicidal ideation.
support_steps: Challenge the thought: 'What is the actual evidence people will judge me harshly?'
Gradual exposure ladder: start with least anxiety-provoking situations, build up.
Focus outward (on the conversation/task) rather than inward (monitoring yourself).
Post-event processing: resist replaying conversations negatively; limit this to 5 minutes maximum.
Seek CBT referral — group CBT for social anxiety has strong evidence.

topics.title: Eating disorders — anorexia, bulimia, binge eating
core_guidance: Eating disorders have the highest mortality of any mental health condition — take all presentations seriously.
Anorexia: severe restriction, distorted body image, intense fear of weight gain.
Bulimia: binge-purge cycles (vomiting, laxatives, exercise); weight may be normal — making it less visible.
Binge eating disorder: recurrent uncontrolled eating without compensatory behaviours.
Recovery requires specialist eating disorder service input — do not attempt to manage alone or push rapid weight gain.
red_flags: BMI below 17.5 or rapid weight loss — medical emergency if below 15.
Fainting, muscle weakness, irregular heartbeat — electrolyte imbalance from purging.
Suicidal thoughts or severe self-harm alongside eating disorder.
support_steps: Avoid commenting on weight or food intake — focus on emotions and wellbeing.
Encourage eating with others and regular meal times without restriction.
Refer urgently to an eating disorder specialist or CAMHS (if under 18).
Monitor physical health: weight, electrolytes (potassium), ECG if purging.
Family-based therapy (FBT) is first-line for adolescent anorexia; CBT-E for adult bulimia.

topics.title: Bipolar disorder
core_guidance: Bipolar disorder involves episodes of mania/hypomania (elevated mood, reduced sleep, impulsivity) and depression.
Bipolar I: full manic episodes lasting 7+ days. Bipolar II: hypomanic episodes plus depressive episodes.
Mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, quetiapine, lamotrigine) are the backbone of treatment.
Lithium requires regular blood level monitoring (target 0.6–0.8 mmol/L), kidney and thyroid checks.
Sleep disruption is often the first warning sign of a manic episode — protect sleep rigorously.
red_flags: Manic episode with reckless behaviour (spending, sexual, substances), psychosis, or aggression.
Suicidal thoughts — highest risk is during mixed episodes and depression.
Lithium toxicity: tremor, vomiting, confusion, ataxia — stop lithium and seek emergency care.
support_steps: Maintain a regular sleep schedule — sleep deprivation triggers mania.
Mood diary to track early warning signs and patterns.
Take mood stabiliser consistently — do not stop suddenly even when feeling well.
Have a crisis plan: identify early warning signs and who to contact.
Psychoeducation and CBT for bipolar alongside medication significantly reduces episodes.

topics.title: Substance use and addiction
core_guidance: Addiction is a brain disorder with compulsive substance use despite harm — not a moral failing.
Alcohol withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures, delirium tremens) — medical supervision essential for heavy drinkers.
Motivational interviewing and harm reduction approaches are more effective than confrontation.
Medications for alcohol use disorder: disulfiram, naltrexone, acamprosate. For opioid use: methadone, buprenorphine.
Co-occurring mental health conditions (depression, trauma, anxiety) are very common and must be addressed.
red_flags: Alcohol withdrawal: tremor, sweating, agitation, hallucinations, or seizures — medical emergency.
Opioid overdose: pinpoint pupils, unconsciousness, very slow breathing — naloxone if available, call 999.
Active suicidal thoughts alongside substance use.
support_steps: For alcohol dependence: do not stop suddenly — taper or seek medically supervised withdrawal.
Naloxone (if opioid use): ensure person and family know how to use it.
12-step programmes (AA, NA) or SMART Recovery as peer support.
Set one small reduction goal per week rather than all-or-nothing.
Refer to substance use services for structured support and medication-assisted treatment.

topics.title: Postpartum depression and anxiety
core_guidance: Baby blues (first 2 weeks): tearfulness, mood swings, normal hormonal adjustment — reassurance and support.
Postpartum depression (weeks to months): persistent low mood, bonding difficulties, inability to cope — needs treatment.
Postpartum anxiety is equally common and often underrecognised — constant worry, panic, hypervigilance.
Postpartum psychosis (rare, 1–2 per 1000): confusion, hallucinations, mania — psychiatric emergency.
SSRIs (sertraline preferred as safest during breastfeeding) are effective; CBT also first-line.
red_flags: Thoughts of harming self or baby — emergency psychiatric assessment.
Postpartum psychosis: confusion, hearing voices, not sleeping, erratic behaviour — call 999.
Complete inability to care for self or baby.
support_steps: Normalise the experience — postpartum depression affects 1 in 7 mothers.
Encourage practical help (sleep, meals, infant care) — do not manage alone.
Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) for screening.
Sertraline is first-line antidepressant — safe with breastfeeding.
Urgent psychiatric referral for any psychosis or safety concerns.

topics.title: Anger and anger management
core_guidance: Anger is a normal emotion; the problem is when it becomes disproportionate, frequent, or leads to harm.
Physiological arousal (heart rate, adrenaline) peaks 20–30 minutes into anger — the 'cool-down' window is real.
Cognitive triggers: catastrophising, mind-reading, 'should' statements, personalisation.
Anger often masks hurt, fear, shame, or grief underneath — explore the primary emotion.
CBT and anger management programmes have strong evidence; domestic violence perpetrator programmes if applicable.
red_flags: Physical violence or threats — safety risk to others requires immediate intervention.
Anger with property destruction, loss of control, or fear by others in the household.
Intermittent explosive disorder or rage disproportionate to triggers.
support_steps: Leave the situation for 20–30 minutes when anger escalates — return to discuss when calm.
Physical exercise immediately after anger trigger — burns adrenaline.
Identify the thought fuelling anger: 'What am I telling myself right now?'
STOP technique: Stop, Take a breath, Observe what you feel, Proceed with intention.
Seek anger management therapy if episodes are frequent or causing relationship/legal problems.

topics.title: Seasonal affective disorder (SAD)
core_guidance: SAD is depression with a seasonal pattern — typically worse in autumn/winter, improving in spring.
Reduced sunlight affects serotonin and melatonin regulation.
Light therapy (10,000 lux, 20–30 minutes each morning within 1 hour of waking) is first-line treatment.
SSRIs (especially fluoxetine) are effective if light therapy is insufficient.
Regular outdoor daytime exposure, even on cloudy days, and exercise have supporting evidence.
red_flags: Depression symptoms severe enough to impair functioning — not just low mood.
Summer depression (reverse SAD — rarer) with insomnia and agitation.
Suicidal thoughts at any season.
support_steps: Light therapy box: 10,000 lux, 20–30 min, morning use, within 1 hour of waking.
Get outside within 1 hour of sunrise daily — even overcast daylight helps.
Maintain regular sleep and wake times — do not hibernate.
Exercise 30 minutes daily — outdoor exercise in daylight is optimal.
Start light therapy in early September before symptoms develop.

topics.title: Exam and academic stress
core_guidance: Academic stress is extremely common and a leading cause of mental health problems in young people.
Distinguish performance anxiety (manageable) from clinical anxiety or depression needing treatment.
Spaced repetition and active recall are far more effective than passive re-reading.
Sleep and exercise are not luxuries during exams — they are performance enhancers.
Perfectionism and all-or-nothing thinking ('I must get an A or I'm a failure') increase stress without improving results.
red_flags: Inability to eat, sleep, or leave the house due to exam anxiety.
Panic attacks during study or before exams.
Self-harm or suicidal thoughts linked to academic pressure.
support_steps: Structured study plan with specific goals per session — avoid open-ended 'study all day'.
Pomodoro: 25 min focused study, 5 min break, 4 cycles then 30 min break.
Sleep 7–9 hours — information consolidates during sleep; all-nighters impair performance.
Breathing exercise before exam: 4-count inhale, 4 hold, 6 exhale — activates parasympathetic system.
Talk to a school counsellor or GP if anxiety is preventing studying despite effort.

topics.title: Loneliness and social isolation
core_guidance: Chronic loneliness has health impacts equivalent to smoking 15 cigarettes a day — take it seriously.
Loneliness is about the perceived quality of connection, not the quantity of people around you.
Social media use paradoxically increases loneliness for many — passive scrolling vs. active interaction.
Small, regular social contact is more beneficial than occasional large events.
Community involvement, volunteering, and shared-interest groups build connection more reliably than dating apps.
red_flags: Loneliness accompanied by depression, hopelessness, or suicidal thoughts.
Complete social withdrawal for weeks — possible depression or psychosis.
Elderly person alone with no social contact and declining self-care.
support_steps: One small social interaction daily — even brief (shopkeeper, neighbour) builds connection.
Join one activity group based on a genuine interest — consistency matters more than size.
Reach out to one person per week — a short message counts.
Limit passive social media consumption; replace with direct messaging or calling.
If depression is underlying the isolation, treat the depression — loneliness often improves with it.

topics.title: Caregiver burnout and compassion fatigue
core_guidance: Caregiver burnout is emotional, physical, and mental exhaustion from sustained caregiving without adequate support.
Guilt about needing a break is almost universal — validate it and challenge it directly.
Respite care is not abandonment — it allows sustainable caregiving.
Carers have higher rates of depression, anxiety, and physical illness than the general population.
Identifying and naming the loss (of the person they knew, of their own life plans) is part of the grief process.
red_flags: Carer considering harming themselves or the person they care for — safeguarding concern.
Complete inability to function or care for oneself.
Social isolation with no support network left.
support_steps: Accept help when offered — name specific tasks others can take on.
Respite care: formal or informal cover to allow a genuine break.
Carer support groups provide validation and practical information.
Set aside 15–30 minutes daily for self-care — non-negotiable.
Speak to a GP about depression screening and carer support services.

topics.title: Heart failure
core_guidance: Heart failure = the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's needs. It does not mean the heart has stopped.
Symptoms: breathlessness on exertion, orthopnoea (breathless lying flat), ankle/leg swelling, fatigue.
Key medications: ACE inhibitors/ARBs, beta-blockers, diuretics, aldosterone antagonists — all reduce mortality.
Daily weight monitoring: weight gain of >2 kg in 2 days indicates fluid retention — adjust diuretics per plan.
Salt restriction (<2 g/day), fluid restriction if advised, and regular moderate activity as tolerated.
red_flags: Sudden severe breathlessness at rest, pink frothy sputum — acute pulmonary oedema, call 999.
Rapid weight gain >2 kg in 2 days with worsening breathlessness.
Syncope (fainting) or severe dizziness in a known heart failure patient.
support_steps: Weigh every morning after urinating, before eating — record in a diary.
Restrict salt: no added salt, avoid processed foods, check food labels.
Take diuretics as prescribed; contact care team if weight rises >2 kg in 2 days.
Elevate legs when sitting; use extra pillows at night if breathless lying flat.
Annual flu vaccine and pneumococcal vaccine — respiratory infections worsen heart failure.

topics.title: Atrial fibrillation (AF)
core_guidance: AF is an irregular, often rapid heart rhythm caused by chaotic electrical activity in the atria.
Main risk: stroke (5x higher than general population) — anticoagulation (warfarin or DOACs) is critical.
CHA2DS2-VASc score determines stroke risk and anticoagulation need.
Rate control (beta-blockers, digoxin) vs. rhythm control (cardioversion, ablation) — specialist decision.
Triggers: alcohol, caffeine, sleep deprivation, stress — modifying these reduces AF burden.
red_flags: New-onset AF with chest pain, breathlessness, or low BP — emergency.
Fast AF (rate >150 bpm) with haemodynamic compromise.
Stroke symptoms (FAST) in a patient with known AF — call 999 immediately.
support_steps: Take anticoagulant as prescribed — never miss doses; this prevents stroke.
Monitor pulse daily — learn to detect irregularity.
Reduce or eliminate alcohol — even moderate use increases AF frequency.
Attend regular INR checks if on warfarin.
Wearable devices (smartwatch with ECG) can help detect AF episodes — share data with doctor.

topics.title: Coronary artery disease and stable angina
core_guidance: CAD: atherosclerotic plaque narrows coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to heart muscle.
Stable angina: predictable chest tightness/pressure on exertion, relieved by rest or GTN spray within 5 minutes.
GTN spray: 1–2 puffs sublingually at onset of angina; repeat after 5 minutes; if not relieved after 2 doses — call 999.
Secondary prevention: aspirin 75mg daily, statin, ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker — all reduce events.
Lifestyle: smoking cessation (single most impactful), Mediterranean diet, regular aerobic exercise.
red_flags: Angina at rest, not relieved by GTN, or lasting >20 minutes — possible unstable angina/NSTEMI — call 999.
New or worsening angina pattern.
Angina with sweating, vomiting, or arm/jaw pain.
support_steps: GTN spray: 1–2 puffs under tongue; sit down; repeat in 5 min; call 999 if still not resolved.
Aspirin 75mg daily (if prescribed) — do not miss.
Stop smoking — reduces cardiac event risk by 50% within 1 year.
Cardiac rehab programme after any cardiac event.
Know your targets: BP <130/80, LDL <1.8 mmol/L, HbA1c <53 if diabetic.

topics.title: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism
core_guidance: DVT: blood clot in deep vein, usually leg — presents with calf/leg pain, swelling, warmth, redness.
PE: clot travels to lung — sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, rapid heart rate.
Diagnosed by D-dimer blood test + ultrasound (DVT) or CT pulmonary angiogram (PE).
Treatment: anticoagulation (DOACs first-line: rivaroxaban, apixaban) for 3–6 months minimum.
Provoked (surgery, immobility, pregnancy) vs. unprovoked — unprovoked requires longer treatment.
red_flags: Suspected PE: sudden breathlessness, chest pain, or collapse — call 999 immediately.
Massive PE with haemodynamic compromise (low BP, loss of consciousness).
DVT in pregnancy — requires urgent haematology review and LMWH.
support_steps: Any suspected DVT (leg swelling + pain) — same-day medical review; do not massage the leg.
Start anticoagulant as prescribed — full adherence is critical to prevent extension and PE.
Compression stockings reduce post-thrombotic syndrome — wear for 2 years after DVT.
For long flights/immobility: compression stockings, calf exercises every hour, hydration.
Avoid prolonged sitting after DVT — walking is encouraged.

topics.title: Low blood pressure (hypotension) and fainting
core_guidance: Orthostatic hypotension: BP drops >20 mmHg systolic on standing — causes dizziness, near-fainting.
Common causes: dehydration, medications (antihypertensives, diuretics, alpha-blockers), prolonged standing, heat.
Simple fainting (vasovagal syncope): triggered by pain, stress, standing — preceded by warning (nausea, tunnel vision), recovers quickly lying flat.
Increase fluid and salt intake (unless contraindicated), avoid prolonged standing, rise slowly from sitting/lying.
If on antihypertensive medications — review with doctor if readings consistently low.
red_flags: Fainting with no warning, during exercise, or in a person with heart disease — cardiac cause possible, urgent evaluation.
Prolonged loss of consciousness or post-fainting confusion.
BP <90/60 with symptoms in a non-athletic person.
support_steps: Rise slowly from bed: sit on edge for 30 seconds before standing.
Increase fluid intake to 2–2.5 L/day; modest salt increase if not contraindicated.
Compression stockings for orthostatic symptoms.
Avoid standing still for long periods — muscle-pump walking movements help.
Review blood pressure medications with doctor if consistently symptomatic.

topics.title: High cholesterol and lipid management
core_guidance: LDL ('bad') cholesterol drives atherosclerosis; HDL ('good') is protective; triglycerides are independently harmful.
Statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin) are first-line — they reduce cardiovascular events, not just cholesterol numbers.
Dietary changes reduce LDL by 10–20%: reduce saturated fat, increase soluble fibre, plant stanols, omega-3.
Target LDL: <2.6 mmol/L for moderate-risk; <1.8 mmol/L for high-risk (known CVD, diabetes).
Muscle aches on statins (myalgia): common — try a lower dose, different statin, or alternate-day dosing; stop if severe.
red_flags: Severe myalgia or weakness on statins — check CK; stop if markedly elevated (rhabdomyolysis risk).
Familial hypercholesterolaemia (very high LDL, family history of early heart disease) — specialist referral.
Pancreatitis from severely elevated triglycerides (>10 mmol/L).
support_steps: Replace saturated fat (butter, red meat) with unsaturated fat (olive oil, oily fish, nuts).
30g oat bran or psyllium husk daily lowers LDL by 5–10%.
Take statin at night (most effective for LDL reduction for simvastatin/pravastatin; time-independent for atorvastatin).
Recheck lipids 3 months after starting statin.
If statin-intolerant: ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors (specialist prescription).

topics.title: Heart attack — recognition and immediate response
core_guidance: Classic presentation: central crushing chest pain radiating to left arm or jaw, sweating, nausea.
Atypical presentations (especially women, elderly, diabetics): jaw pain, fatigue, nausea, breathlessness, back pain without chest pain.
Immediate: call 999, chew aspirin 300mg (if not allergic and not already on it), rest in comfortable position.
Primary PCI (angioplasty) must happen within 90 minutes of hospital arrival — time is muscle.
Do not drive yourself to hospital — ambulance arrival triggers pre-hospital ECG and faster cath lab activation.
red_flags: Any suspected heart attack — call 999 immediately, do not wait to see if it passes.
Diabetics and women often present without typical chest pain — high index of suspicion needed.
Cardiac arrest (no pulse, not breathing) — start CPR immediately.
support_steps: Call 999 immediately — state 'possible heart attack'.
Chew (not swallow whole) aspirin 300mg if available and not allergic.
Sit or lie in a comfortable position — do not walk around.
Loosen tight clothing; stay calm; unlock door for paramedics.
Do not eat or drink anything — surgery may be imminent.

topics.title: Asthma management
