core_guidance: Asthma: reversible airway narrowing causing wheeze, cough, breathlessness, chest tightness — often worse at night.
Reliever inhaler (salbutamol/albuterol — blue): for immediate symptom relief. Use signals poor control if needed >2x/week.
Preventer inhaler (inhaled corticosteroid — brown/orange): daily use reduces inflammation and prevents attacks.
LABA (salmeterol) added to ICS if uncontrolled on ICS alone — never use LABA without ICS.
Asthma action plan: traffic light system — green (well), amber (worsening), red (emergency).
red_flags: Severe attack: unable to complete sentences, RR >25, HR >110, peak flow <50% best.
Life-threatening: silent chest (no wheeze = no air moving), cyanosis, exhaustion.
Using reliever >every 4 hours — escalate treatment.
support_steps: Mild-moderate attack: 4–10 puffs salbutamol via spacer every 20 minutes for 1 hour; call GP/999 if not improving.
Use spacer device — doubles drug delivery compared to MDI alone.
Take preventer inhaler every day — even when well.
Rinse mouth after ICS inhaler — prevents oral candidiasis.
Review triggers: dust mites, pet dander, mould, NSAIDs (in aspirin-sensitive asthma).

topics.title: COPD management
core_guidance: COPD: progressive airflow obstruction, mainly from smoking — irreversible unlike asthma.
Smoking cessation is the only intervention that slows progression.
Bronchodilators (SABA, LABA, LAMA) reduce symptoms and exacerbation frequency.
Pulmonary rehabilitation (supervised exercise + education) improves quality of life more than most medications.
Exacerbations: increased sputum, worsening breathlessness — may need oral prednisolone 30–40mg for 5 days and antibiotics.
red_flags: Acute exacerbation with severe breathlessness, cyanosis, or confusion — call 999.
SpO2 <88% or rapidly falling — may need controlled oxygen (not high-flow, risks CO2 retention).
More than 2 exacerbations/year — review management with respiratory specialist.
support_steps: Stop smoking immediately — most important single intervention.
Salbutamol MDI for breathlessness relief; tiotropium daily for maintenance.
Annual flu and pneumococcal vaccinations.
Pulmonary rehabilitation referral — 6–8 week programme reduces hospital admissions.
Develop a written exacerbation action plan with your respiratory team.

topics.title: Pneumonia
core_guidance: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP): fever, productive cough, breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain.
CURB-65 score determines severity and need for hospital admission.
Typical CAP (Streptococcus pneumoniae): amoxicillin 500mg 3x daily for 5–7 days.
Atypical CAP (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydophila): clarithromycin or doxycycline.
Most community cases can be treated at home with oral antibiotics; rest and hydration are essential.
red_flags: CURB-65 score ≥2: confusion, urea >7, RR ≥30, BP <90/60, age ≥65 — hospital admission likely needed.
SpO2 <94% on room air.
Failure to improve after 48–72 hours of antibiotics — reassess and consider resistant organism or complication.
support_steps: Amoxicillin 500mg 3x daily for 5 days (add clarithromycin for atypical cover if unwell).
Rest and drink 2–3 L fluids daily.
Paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever and pleuritic pain.
Deep breathing exercises and gentle walking to aid expectoration.
Return if: not improving in 48 hours, developing confusion, or breathlessness worsening.

topics.title: Sleep apnoea and snoring
core_guidance: Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA): repeated collapse of upper airway during sleep, causing apnoeas and arousals.
Symptoms: loud snoring, witnessed apnoeas, unrefreshing sleep, excessive daytime sleepiness, morning headache.
Risk factors: obesity (BMI >30), male sex, large neck circumference, alcohol, sedatives.
Diagnosis: overnight sleep study (polysomnography or home oximetry).
CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) is gold-standard treatment — eliminates apnoeas, improves all symptoms.
red_flags: Witnessed apnoeas (partner reports stopping breathing) with severe daytime sleepiness — urgent sleep study.
OSA in a commercial driver or heavy machinery operator — legal and safety implications.
OSA with uncontrolled hypertension or cardiac arrhythmia.
support_steps: Weight loss of 10% body weight reduces apnoea-hypopnoea index by ~25%.
Avoid alcohol within 3 hours of bedtime — relaxes pharyngeal muscles.
Sleep on side rather than back (positional OSA).
Refer for sleep study if snoring plus any of: daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeas, morning headaches.
CPAP: use every night including naps — adherence is the most important factor in outcomes.

topics.title: Sinusitis
core_guidance: Acute sinusitis: facial pain/pressure, nasal blockage, thick coloured discharge — mostly viral, self-limiting in 10–14 days.
Antibiotics NOT needed for most acute sinusitis — overused and ineffective for viral cases.
Symptomatic treatment: saline nasal irrigation, intranasal corticosteroid spray, steam inhalation.
Bacterial sinusitis suspected (symptoms >10 days, fever, severe unilateral pain): amoxicillin 500mg 3x daily for 5–7 days.
Chronic sinusitis (>12 weeks): daily intranasal steroid spray is first-line; ENT referral for persistent cases.
red_flags: Periorbital swelling, redness, or proptosis — orbital cellulitis from sinus extension, emergency.
Severe headache with neck stiffness — meningitis complication.
Visual changes or diplopia with sinus symptoms.
support_steps: Saline nasal rinse (neti pot or squeeze bottle) twice daily — clears mucus and reduces symptoms.
Intranasal steroid spray (Flonase/Beconase): daily, takes 1–2 weeks for full effect.
Steam inhalation with menthol for symptomatic relief.
Decongestant spray (Otrivin) maximum 3–5 days to avoid rebound.
Antibiotics only if not improving after 10 days or symptoms worsening with fever.

topics.title: Tuberculosis (TB)
core_guidance: TB classic triad: persistent productive cough (>3 weeks), night sweats, unexplained weight loss.
Pulmonary TB: most common; transmitted by airborne droplets — isolation until non-infectious (2 weeks of treatment).
Diagnosis: sputum AFB smear/culture, chest X-ray, Mantoux or IGRA blood test.
Standard treatment: RHEZ for 2 months, then RH for 4 months (total 6 months) — all must be completed.
DOT (Directly Observed Therapy) recommended in Bangladesh — critical for treatment completion and resistance prevention.
red_flags: Haemoptysis (coughing blood) — active TB or complication.
MDR-TB (multidrug-resistant): requires specialist management, longer treatment (18–24 months).
TB meningitis: headache, neck stiffness, fever — emergency.
support_steps: Complete the full 6-month course — stopping early causes resistance.
Cover mouth when coughing; ventilate living spaces.
Notify household contacts for screening.
Take rifampicin on empty stomach; it turns urine/sweat orange (harmless).
Free TB treatment available through National TB Programme in Bangladesh.

topics.title: COVID-19 management
core_guidance: Most COVID-19 cases are mild-moderate: fever, cough, fatigue, sore throat, myalgia, anosmia — managed at home.
Supportive care: paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever/pain, hydration, rest.
High-risk groups (elderly, immunocompromised, severe obesity): antivirals (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, molnupiravir) within 5 days of symptom onset.
Isolation: stay home for at least 5 days or until fever-free for 24 hours.
Long COVID: fatigue, brain fog, breathlessness persisting >4 weeks — refer to post-COVID clinic.
red_flags: SpO2 <94% on pulse oximeter at rest — seek emergency care.
Rapid breathing (>30/min), persistent chest pain, or confusion.
Happy hypoxia: feeling well despite very low oxygen — check SpO2 proactively in high-risk patients.
support_steps: Paracetamol 1g every 4–6 hours for fever and pain; ibuprofen 400mg if paracetamol inadequate.
Hydrate well; monitor SpO2 with pulse oximeter if available.
Rest: pacing is important for recovery — avoid overexertion.
Isolate for 5 days; wear mask if you must be around others.
Contact doctor urgently if SpO2 drops below 94%, or for breathlessness at rest.

topics.title: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
core_guidance: IBS: chronic gut symptoms (pain, bloating, altered bowel habit) without structural cause — diagnosis of exclusion.
Subtypes: IBS-D (diarrhea-predominant), IBS-C (constipation-predominant), IBS-M (mixed).
Low-FODMAP diet reduces symptoms in 70% — remove fermentable carbohydrates for 4–6 weeks, then reintroduce systematically.
Gut-brain axis: stress and anxiety significantly worsen IBS — psychological therapy (CBT, gut-directed hypnotherapy) has strong evidence.
Antispasmodics (mebeverine, hyoscine) for pain; loperamide for IBS-D; laxatives for IBS-C.
red_flags: Rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss, or symptom onset after age 50 — needs investigation to exclude cancer.
Nocturnal symptoms waking from sleep.
Family history of colorectal cancer, IBD, or coeliac disease.
support_steps: Regular meals, reduce caffeine, alcohol, and carbonated drinks.
Low-FODMAP trial for 4–6 weeks with a dietitian if possible.
Soluble fibre (oats, psyllium) for IBS-C; avoid insoluble fibre (bran) which worsens IBS-D.
Peppermint oil capsules (enteric-coated) reduce pain and bloating.
Stress management — IBS flares correlate with stress; CBT reduces severity.

topics.title: GERD and acid reflux
core_guidance: GERD: gastric acid reflux into oesophagus causing heartburn, regurgitation, chest pain, or chronic cough.
Lifestyle: lose weight, avoid trigger foods (fatty/spicy/citrus/chocolate/coffee/alcohol), elevate head of bed 15–20 cm, no meals within 3 hours of sleep.
PPIs (omeprazole 20mg, pantoprazole 40mg) before breakfast for 4–8 weeks — most effective medical treatment.
Antacids for immediate relief; H2-blockers (ranitidine, famotidine) for mild-moderate symptoms.
Long-term PPI use: check for hypomagnesaemia, B12 deficiency after 1 year.
red_flags: Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), odynophagia (painful swallowing), or food getting stuck — oesophageal pathology.
Unintentional weight loss with reflux symptoms.
Anaemia, haematemesis, or melaena — possible Barrett's oesophagus or malignancy.
support_steps: Omeprazole 20mg before breakfast for 4–8 weeks.
Elevate head of bed — use blocks under bedposts, not just extra pillows.
Avoid eating within 3 hours of lying down.
Identify and eliminate personal trigger foods.
Endoscopy if symptoms persist on PPI, or alarm symptoms present.

topics.title: Peptic ulcer disease
core_guidance: Peptic ulcers: mucosal breaks in stomach or duodenum — caused by H. pylori (most common) or NSAIDs.
Symptoms: burning epigastric pain, worse with food (gastric ulcer) or relieved by food (duodenal ulcer).
H. pylori eradication: triple therapy — PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin for 7–14 days. 95% cure rate.
Stop NSAIDs if causative; add PPI if NSAIDs must continue.
Retest H. pylori 4–6 weeks after treatment with breath test to confirm eradication.
red_flags: Haematemesis (vomiting blood) or melaena (black tarry stools) — peptic ulcer bleeding, emergency.
Sudden severe abdominal pain with rigid abdomen — perforation, emergency.
Weight loss, dysphagia, or progressive symptoms in older patients — malignancy investigation needed.
support_steps: H. pylori triple therapy: Omeprazole 20mg + Amoxicillin 1g + Clarithromycin 500mg — all twice daily for 7–14 days.
Avoid NSAIDs, aspirin (unless essential), smoking, and alcohol.
Continue PPI for 4–8 weeks after eradication for healing.
Retest H. pylori by breath test 4–6 weeks after completing antibiotics.
Consider endoscopy for confirmed eradication and healing of gastric ulcers.

topics.title: Appendicitis — recognition
core_guidance: Classic progression: periumbilical pain migrating to right iliac fossa (McBurney's point), nausea/vomiting, low-grade fever.
Presentation is variable — especially in children, elderly, and pregnant women.
Diagnosis: clinical assessment + inflammatory markers (CRP, WBC) + ultrasound/CT.
Treatment is surgical (appendicectomy) — laparoscopic in most cases.
Do not give food, water, or analgesics that mask symptoms before medical assessment.
red_flags: Peritonism (rigid abdomen, severe rebound tenderness) — possible perforation, emergency.
Fever above 38.5°C with right iliac fossa pain — do not delay assessment.
Any right-sided abdominal pain in pregnancy — urgent obstetric and surgical review.
support_steps: Go to A&E or emergency room for any right lower abdominal pain with fever — do not wait.
Nothing by mouth once appendicitis is suspected.
Do not apply heat to the abdomen.
If perforation is suspected: IV antibiotics pre-operatively.
Post-operatively: full recovery in 2–4 weeks for laparoscopic procedure.

topics.title: Viral hepatitis (A, B, C)
core_guidance: Hepatitis A: faeco-oral transmission; self-limiting; vaccination available; no chronic carrier state.
Hepatitis B: blood/sexual/vertical transmission; HBsAg persists >6 months = chronic carrier; treat if viral load >2000 IU/mL or liver damage.
Hepatitis C: blood-borne (IVDU, unsterilised equipment, blood products); curable with 8–12 week DAA therapy (>95% cure rate).
Jaundice, fatigue, right upper quadrant pain, dark urine — typical presentation.
Hepatitis B vaccination: 3-dose series — essential in Bangladesh where HBV is endemic.
red_flags: Acute liver failure: coagulopathy, encephalopathy, jaundice — emergency.
Hepatitis with fever and confusion — possible fulminant hepatitis.
Hepatocellular carcinoma risk in chronic HBV/HCV — 6-monthly AFP + ultrasound surveillance.
support_steps: Hepatitis A: rest, fluids, avoid alcohol; resolves spontaneously in 4–8 weeks.
Hepatitis B chronic: entecavir or tenofovir daily (highly effective, well-tolerated).
Hepatitis C: refer to hepatology; curative DAA therapy available.
Avoid alcohol in all hepatitis — accelerates fibrosis.
Vaccinate household contacts for hepatitis B.

topics.title: Gallstones and biliary colic
core_guidance: Biliary colic: severe right upper quadrant pain after fatty meals, radiating to right shoulder/back, lasting 30 min–6 hours.
Acute cholecystitis: persistent pain with fever, local tenderness, elevated CRP/WBC.
Diagnosis: abdominal ultrasound.
Symptomatic gallstones: laparoscopic cholecystectomy is definitive treatment.
During acute attack: nil by mouth, IV fluids, analgesia (NSAIDs/opioids), antibiotics for cholecystitis.
red_flags: Jaundice with right upper quadrant pain and fever (Charcot's triad) — ascending cholangitis, emergency.
Severe, worsening abdominal pain with rigidity — possible perforation.
Pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back, elevated amylase/lipase.
support_steps: During biliary colic: diclofenac 75mg IM or diclofenac 100mg suppository — most effective analgesic.
Low-fat diet reduces frequency of attacks pending surgery.
Refer for laparoscopic cholecystectomy after first symptomatic episode.
If cholecystitis: hospital admission for IV antibiotics and surgical planning.
Avoid high-fat meals, rapid weight loss, and prolonged fasting.

topics.title: Haemorrhoids (piles)
core_guidance: Haemorrhoids: engorged vascular cushions in anal canal — internal (above dentate line, painless bleeding) or external (painful).
Symptoms: bright red rectal bleeding on wiping, anal itching, prolapse, perianal discomfort.
First-line: high-fibre diet, hydration, stool softeners, topical treatments (hydrocortisone/lidocaine cream).
Grade 1–2: medical/dietary management. Grade 3–4: rubber band ligation, sclerotherapy, or haemorrhoidectomy.
Sitz baths (warm water 10–15 minutes twice daily) reduce pain and promote healing.
red_flags: Rectal bleeding in anyone over 40 or with change in bowel habit — colonoscopy to exclude colorectal cancer.
Thrombosed external haemorrhoid: sudden severe perianal pain — may need excision within 72 hours.
Perianal abscess: severe throbbing pain with swelling, fever — surgical drainage needed.
support_steps: Increase dietary fibre to 25–30g/day; drink 2+ litres water daily.
Avoid straining — spend no more than 5 minutes on the toilet.
Topical Proctosedyl or Anusol cream for symptom relief.
Sitz bath twice daily for acute discomfort.
Refer to surgeon for rubber band ligation if medical management fails.

topics.title: Constipation
core_guidance: Constipation: fewer than 3 stools/week, straining, hard stools, or sensation of incomplete evacuation.
First-line: increase fibre (25–30g/day), fluids (2L/day), physical activity.
Bulk-forming laxatives (psyllium, ispaghula husk) first; osmotic laxatives (lactulose, macrogol/PEG) second.
Stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl) for short-term use; avoid long-term overuse.
Identify and address medications causing constipation: opioids, iron, calcium channel blockers.
red_flags: Constipation with rectal bleeding, weight loss, or change in bowel habit in over-50 — colonoscopy.
Obstruction: absolute constipation, vomiting, distension, no flatus — emergency.
New onset constipation with hypothyroidism symptoms, diabetes, or hypercalcaemia.
support_steps: Dietary fibre: oats, lentils, beans, vegetables, prunes. Add gradually to avoid bloating.
Ispaghula husk (Fybogel) sachet once or twice daily — bulk-forming, safe long-term.
Macrogol (Movicol/Laxido) sachet 1–3 daily in water — osmotic, very effective and safe.
Toilet posture: feet on a footstool (squatting position) aids defecation.
Review medications — opioids always need concurrent laxative (typically macrogol).

topics.title: Inflammatory bowel disease — Crohn's and ulcerative colitis
core_guidance: UC: continuous colonic inflammation from rectum upward — bloody diarrhea, tenesmus, urgency.
Crohn's: skip-lesion transmural inflammation anywhere in GI tract — pain, diarrhea, weight loss, perianal disease.
Mesalazine (5-ASA) for mild-moderate UC; azathioprine/mercaptopurine for maintenance; biologics (infliximab, adalimumab) for severe/refractory.
Flares: oral/IV corticosteroids for induction; not for maintenance (bone/metabolic effects).
Nutritional support crucial in Crohn's — iron, B12, folate, vitamin D deficiencies common.
red_flags: Severe UC flare: >6 bloody stools/day, fever, tachycardia — hospital admission for IV steroids.
Toxic megacolon: distension, fever, shock — emergency surgical review.
Strictures, fistulae, or abscess in Crohn's — surgical intervention may be needed.
support_steps: Take maintenance medication even in remission — stopping risks flare.
Annual monitoring: FBC, CRP, ferritin, vitamin D, B12, and renal function.
IBD nutrition: ensure adequate iron, B12, and vitamin D; avoid trigger foods during flares.
Colonoscopy surveillance for colorectal cancer after 8–10 years of IBD.
Register with a gastroenterologist for specialist follow-up and biologics access.

topics.title: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid)
core_guidance: Hypothyroidism: insufficient thyroid hormone — fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, depression, bradycardia.
Diagnosis: elevated TSH; low FT4 confirms primary hypothyroidism.
Treatment: levothyroxine (thyroxine), started low and increased. Take on empty stomach 30–60 minutes before food.
Target TSH: 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for most patients; recheck 6–8 weeks after dose change.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis: autoimmune cause — check TPO antibodies.
red_flags: Myxoedema coma: severe hypothyroidism with hypothermia, bradycardia, unconsciousness — emergency.
New AF or heart failure developing on untreated hypothyroidism.
Hypothyroidism in pregnancy — requires careful dose adjustment, immediate referral.
support_steps: Levothyroxine: take every morning on empty stomach, 30–60 min before food or other medications.
Do not take calcium, iron, or antacids within 4 hours — they reduce absorption.
Recheck TSH 6–8 weeks after any dose change.
Symptoms may take 3–6 months to fully resolve after starting treatment.
Annual TSH monitoring once stable.

topics.title: Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid)
core_guidance: Hyperthyroidism: excess thyroid hormone — weight loss despite appetite, palpitations, heat intolerance, sweating, anxiety, tremor, diarrhea.
Graves' disease: most common cause — autoimmune, often with goitre and eye signs (exophthalmos).
Treatment options: antithyroid drugs (carbimazole, PTU), radioiodine, or surgery.
Carbimazole: start 20–40mg daily, taper to maintenance. Monitor FBC — agranulocytosis risk.
Beta-blockers (propranolol) for symptom control pending euthyroid state.
red_flags: Thyroid storm: extreme hyperthyroidism with fever, AF, confusion — medical emergency.
Agranulocytosis on carbimazole: fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers — STOP carbimazole, emergency FBC.
Severe exophthalmos with corneal exposure — ophthalmology urgency.
support_steps: Carbimazole: swallow whole with food. Report fever/sore throat immediately — stop drug if agranulocytosis suspected.
Propranolol 40mg twice daily for palpitations and tremor while awaiting euthyroid state.
TFTs every 4–6 weeks while titrating carbimazole.
Avoid high-iodine foods (seaweed, iodine supplements) — can precipitate thyroid storm.
Discuss definitive treatment (radioiodine or surgery) with endocrinologist after 12–18 months.

topics.title: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
core_guidance: PCOS: triad of irregular ovulation, hyperandrogenism (excess hair, acne), and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound.
Two of three criteria (Rotterdam) are sufficient for diagnosis.
Insulin resistance is central — weight loss of even 5–10% restores ovulation in overweight women.
Metformin improves insulin sensitivity, cycle regularity, and supports fertility.
OCP: regulates cycles and treats hyperandrogenism. Spironolactone/antiandrogens for hirsutism.
red_flags: Endometrial hyperplasia risk with prolonged anovulation — progestogen protection needed.
Metabolic syndrome screening: fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipids annually.
Mental health: depression and anxiety significantly higher in PCOS — screen regularly.
support_steps: Weight loss: 5–10% body weight significantly improves cycle regularity and fertility.
Metformin 500–1000mg twice daily improves metabolic and reproductive outcomes.
OCP for cycle regulation and hyperandrogenism: combined pill with anti-androgenic progestogen preferred.
For unwanted hair: eflornithine cream, laser hair removal, or spironolactone.
Annual metabolic screening: HbA1c, fasting glucose, lipids, BP.

topics.title: Gout and hyperuricaemia
core_guidance: Gout: monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints — typically first metatarsophalangeal joint (big toe) — intensely painful, red, swollen.
Acute attack: NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen) first-line; colchicine 500mcg 2–4 times daily; oral prednisolone if both contraindicated.
Urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol): start 2–4 weeks AFTER acute attack resolves; target serum urate <360 μmol/L.
Starting allopurinol during acute attack worsens the flare — always wait.
Dietary triggers: red meat, offal, shellfish, beer, fructose-sweetened drinks — reduce.
red_flags: Septic arthritis: fever with single hot swollen joint — needs joint aspiration to exclude infection.
Tophi (urate deposits) with chronic joint damage — requires aggressive urate lowering.
Gout in renal impairment — allopurinol dose adjustment needed.
support_steps: Acute flare: naproxen 500mg twice daily or colchicine 500mcg 3x daily with food for 5 days.
Rest and elevate the joint; ice pack wrapped in cloth.
Start allopurinol 100mg daily 2–4 weeks AFTER attack; increase by 100mg every 4 weeks to target.
Hydrate well — 2–3 L/day; alcohol (especially beer) significantly raises urate.
Reduce red meat, offal, and shellfish; increase low-fat dairy and cherries.

topics.title: Obesity and weight management
core_guidance: Obesity (BMI >30) drives type 2 diabetes, CVD, sleep apnoea, joint disease, and several cancers.
Sustained caloric deficit of 500–750 kcal/day produces 0.5–0.75 kg/week loss — realistic target.
Diet quality matters: Mediterranean, low-carbohydrate, and intermittent fasting all work if adhered to.
Physical activity: resistance training preserves muscle during weight loss; 150+ min/week moderate aerobic activity.
GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide/Ozempic) and orlistat are effective adjuncts to lifestyle changes.
red_flags: Rapid unexplained weight gain — exclude hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, medications.
Morbid obesity (BMI >40) with comorbidities — bariatric surgery evaluation.
Eating disorder behaviour underlying obesity.
support_steps: Set realistic target: 5–10% weight loss significantly reduces metabolic risk.
Track food intake honestly — most people underestimate by 30–40%.
Reduce ultra-processed foods, sugary drinks, and alcohol — highest calorie-to-nutrient ratio.
Build activity gradually: 10-minute walks after meals accumulate.
Orlistat 120mg with each main meal if lifestyle changes insufficient (reduces dietary fat absorption by 30%).

topics.title: Migraine — acute treatment and prevention
core_guidance: Migraine: moderate-severe unilateral pulsating headache, nausea, photophobia/phonophobia, lasting 4–72 hours.
Acute treatment: NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg) or paracetamol + triptan (sumatriptan 50–100mg oral, or nasal spray for fast onset).
Triptans most effective if taken early; do not take during aura.
Anti-emetic (metoclopramide 10mg) alongside analgesic — improves gastric motility and drug absorption.
Prevention (if >4 attacks/month): propranolol, topiramate, amitriptyline, or anti-CGRP injections (specialist).
red_flags: Thunderclap headache ('worst of life', sudden onset) — subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise, emergency.
New neurological symptoms with migraine — first-ever aura in an older patient needs investigation.
Headache with fever, neck stiffness, or rash — meningitis.
support_steps: At onset: sumatriptan 50mg + naproxen 500mg + metoclopramide 10mg — take together early.
Rest in dark, quiet room; cold compress on forehead.
Identify and avoid personal triggers: sleep changes, dehydration, certain foods, hormones, stress.
Keep a migraine diary to identify patterns.
If using acute medication >10 days/month: medication overuse headache developing — needs preventive therapy.

topics.title: Vertigo and dizziness
core_guidance: BPPV (benign paroxysmal positional vertigo): brief spinning triggered by head movement — dislodged otoliths. Treat with Epley manoeuvre.
Vestibular neuritis/labyrinthitis: viral inflammation — constant vertigo days to weeks; prochlorperazine for symptom relief.
Ménière's disease: recurrent episodic vertigo with unilateral tinnitus and hearing loss — betahistine and salt restriction.
Central vertigo (brainstem/cerebellar): usually associated with other neurological signs — urgent imaging needed.
Benign vs dangerous dizziness: HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) differentiates peripheral from central.
red_flags: Vertigo with sudden severe headache, double vision, dysarthria, or ataxia — posterior circulation stroke, emergency.
Vertigo with hearing loss and tinnitus starting acutely in the elderly — consider vascular cause.
Vertigo with loss of consciousness.
support_steps: BPPV: Epley manoeuvre — can be done at home with instruction (highly effective, 80% single-session cure).
Vestibular neuritis: prochlorperazine 5mg 3x daily for symptom relief (max 2 weeks).
Avoid driving during active vertigo episodes.
Vestibular rehabilitation exercises speed recovery for chronic dizziness.
Ménière's: low-salt diet (<1.5g/day) and betahistine 16mg 3x daily.

topics.title: Peripheral neuropathy
core_guidance: Peripheral neuropathy: damage to peripheral nerves — symptoms: burning/tingling/numbness, typically in 'glove and stocking' distribution.
Common causes: diabetes (most common), B12 deficiency, alcohol, hypothyroidism, chemotherapy, idiopathic.
Address the underlying cause first: optimise glucose control in diabetes, B12 replacement, reduce alcohol.
Neuropathic pain medications: amitriptyline 10–75mg nocte, duloxetine 60mg, pregabalin 75–300mg, gabapentin.
Foot care in diabetic neuropathy: daily inspection, well-fitting footwear, podiatry — prevent ulcers.
red_flags: Acute ascending weakness and areflexia — Guillain-Barré syndrome, emergency.
Neuropathy with systemic symptoms (weight loss, lymphadenopathy) — paraneoplastic cause.
Rapidly progressive neuropathy.
support_steps: Check B12, HbA1c, TFTs, LFTs, renal function — identify treatable cause.
Amitriptyline 10mg at night — start low, increase weekly to effect; helps sleep and pain.
Gabapentin 100–300mg 3x daily — increase slowly; dizziness and sedation common.
Diabetes: optimise HbA1c — tight control slows neuropathy progression.
Podiatry referral for foot care; never walk barefoot with diabetic neuropathy.

topics.title: Carpal tunnel syndrome
core_guidance: Carpal tunnel syndrome: compression of median nerve at wrist — tingling, numbness, pain in thumb, index, middle, and half of ring finger.
Typically worse at night and with sustained wrist flexion/extension (phone use, typing, driving).
Mild-moderate: nocturnal wrist splint in neutral position — effective in 50–70% of mild cases.
Corticosteroid injection into carpal tunnel: effective for 6–12 months, can be repeated.
Surgical decompression: carpal tunnel release — highly effective, >90% improvement.
red_flags: Thenar muscle wasting (thumb base atrophy) — advanced nerve damage, urgent surgical referral.
Bilateral symptoms — systemic cause (hypothyroidism, acromegaly, rheumatoid) to be investigated.
Progressive weakness in the hand despite treatment.
support_steps: Nocturnal wrist splint in neutral position — wear every night for 4–6 weeks.
Avoid extreme wrist positions; take frequent breaks from keyboard/phone.
NSAID (ibuprofen 400mg 3x daily) for inflammatory component.
Steroid injection for moderate-severe symptoms while awaiting surgery.
Surgical referral if splint and injection fail, or if thenar wasting present.

topics.title: Epilepsy management
core_guidance: Epilepsy: recurrent unprovoked seizures — diagnosed after 2+ unprovoked seizures or 1 seizure with high recurrence risk.
First-line AEDs: levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate (valproate avoided in women of childbearing age — teratogenic).
Seizure first aid: protect, time, recovery position, call 999 if >5 minutes.
Driving restrictions: must be seizure-free for 1 year (Bangladesh law varies — check locally).
Triggering factors: sleep deprivation, alcohol, missed medication, stress, illness — avoid.
red_flags: Status epilepticus (seizure >5 minutes, or no recovery between seizures) — call 999.
Post-ictal hemiplegia, confusion >1 hour, or first seizure ever — emergency evaluation.
Breakthrough seizures on established treatment — review medication compliance and triggers.
support_steps: Take AED at the same time daily — never miss a dose.
Seizure diary: record date, time, duration, type, potential trigger.
Inform DVLA/driving authority — do not drive until seizure-free for required period.
Wear a medical alert bracelet.
Women on valproate: mandatory effective contraception; switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam if planning pregnancy.

topics.title: Osteoporosis
core_guidance: Osteoporosis: reduced bone density — silent until fracture (hip, vertebral, wrist).
Risk factors: postmenopausal women, elderly men, long-term corticosteroids, smoking, alcohol, low calcium/vitamin D.
DEXA scan: T-score <-2.5 = osteoporosis; -1 to -2.5 = osteopenia.
First-line treatment: alendronate 70mg once weekly (take on empty stomach, standing, with full glass of water, no food/drink for 30 minutes after).
Calcium 1000–1200mg/day + Vitamin D 800–1000 IU alongside bisphosphonate.
red_flags: Hip fracture — surgical emergency: high 1-year mortality; admit immediately.
Vertebral fracture: sudden back pain in an older person after minimal trauma.
ONJ (osteonecrosis of jaw) on bisphosphonate — dental work risk; inform dentist.
support_steps: Alendronate 70mg once weekly: morning, fasting, with full glass of water, upright for 30 minutes.
Calcium 500mg twice daily with food + Vitamin D3 800 IU daily.
Weight-bearing exercise: walking, resistance training — builds bone density.
Falls prevention: remove home hazards, eye checks, review medications causing dizziness.
DEXA scan follow-up every 2–3 years to monitor response.

topics.title: Rheumatoid arthritis
core_guidance: RA: systemic autoimmune disease — symmetrical small joint inflammation (hands, wrists, feet), morning stiffness >1 hour.
Diagnosis: clinical + RF/anti-CCP antibodies + imaging. Early treatment prevents joint damage.
DMARDs (disease-modifying): methotrexate first-line. Folic acid 5mg weekly reduces side effects.
Biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-6): for inadequate DMARD response — specialist prescription.
NSAIDs/corticosteroids for symptom control but not disease modification.
red_flags: Rapidly progressive joint destruction or functional decline — escalate treatment urgently.
RA with new breathlessness — interstitial lung disease complication.
Fever with joint symptoms — septic arthritis must be excluded.
support_steps: Methotrexate: start 7.5–10mg weekly, monitor FBC and LFTs every 8–12 weeks.
Take folic acid 5mg once weekly (not on same day as methotrexate).
Hand therapy and splinting maintain function and reduce deformity.
Regular aerobic exercise and resistance training — reduces pain and improves function.
Annual flu and pneumococcal vaccination — immunosuppression increases infection risk.

topics.title: Lower back pain
core_guidance: 90% of acute lower back pain is non-specific (no identifiable structural cause) and resolves in 4–8 weeks.
Stay active — bed rest worsens outcomes; regular movement and walking aid recovery.
Analgesia: paracetamol first; NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen) more effective for inflammatory component.
Sciatica (radiating leg pain below knee): indicates nerve root compression; usually resolves in 6–12 weeks.
Physiotherapy and core strengthening exercises for subacute and chronic back pain.
red_flags: Cauda equina syndrome: bilateral leg weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anaesthesia — emergency surgery.
Back pain with fever and night sweats — spinal infection or malignancy.
New back pain in elderly after minimal trauma — vertebral fracture.
support_steps: Stay active: regular walks, avoid prolonged sitting/lying — movement is medicine for back pain.
Ibuprofen 400mg 3x daily with food for 5–7 days; add paracetamol if needed.
Heat application to affected area reduces muscle spasm.
Physiotherapy referral for core stability exercises after 4–6 weeks without resolution.
MRI only if neurological signs present or no improvement after 6 weeks.

topics.title: Fibromyalgia
core_guidance: Fibromyalgia: chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive symptoms ('fibro fog').
Central sensitisation: amplified pain processing in the CNS — not peripheral tissue damage.
Exercise is the most evidence-based treatment — particularly aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling, walking).
Low-dose amitriptyline 10–25mg nocte improves sleep and pain.
CBT addresses the pain catastrophising and disability cycle.
red_flags: New widespread pain — exclude inflammatory arthritis, hypothyroidism, coeliac, lupus before fibromyalgia diagnosis.
Progressive weakness or neurological symptoms — not fibromyalgia.
High ESR/CRP — suggests inflammatory cause.
support_steps: Graded aerobic exercise: start with 5–10 minutes daily, increase gradually — expect initial discomfort that improves.
Amitriptyline 10mg at night — improves sleep and reduces pain.
Pacing: balance activity and rest; avoid boom-bust cycles.
CBT for pain management — addresses catastrophising and kinesiophobia.
Hydrotherapy and gentle yoga have supporting evidence.

topics.title: Osteoarthritis
core_guidance: OA: degenerative joint disease — pain, stiffness (morning stiffness <30 minutes), crepitus, bony enlargement.
Most commonly affects knees, hips, hands, and lumbar spine.
Exercise is first-line — strengthens muscles around joint, reduces load; patients often resist this but it works.
Analgesia: topical NSAIDs (first-line for knee/hand OA), then oral paracetamol, then oral NSAIDs.
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection for flares — effective for 4–8 weeks.
red_flags: Sudden increase in joint pain with warmth and swelling — exclude gout flare or septic arthritis.
Rapid functional decline — reassess for surgical candidacy.
Severe pain at rest or night pain — may indicate secondary pathology.
support_steps: Quadriceps strengthening exercises for knee OA — reduces pain and improves function.
Weight loss if overweight — 1 kg weight loss reduces knee joint load by 4 kg.
Topical diclofenac gel to the affected joint twice daily — effective and minimises systemic effects.
Physiotherapy for tailored exercise programme.
Refer for joint replacement when pain and disability are severe despite optimal conservative management.

topics.title: Acne treatment
core_guidance: Acne: comedones, papules, pustules from follicular occlusion, sebum, and Cutibacterium acnes.
Mild acne: topical benzoyl peroxide 2.5–5% or adapalene (retinoid) nightly.
Moderate acne: topical retinoid + benzoyl peroxide + topical antibiotic (clindamycin) — limit antibiotic to 3 months.
Severe/nodular acne: oral antibiotics (doxycycline 100mg daily) + topical. OCP for hormonal acne in women.
Isotretinoin (Accutane/Roaccutane): highly effective for severe acne — requires specialist prescription, teratogenic, need contraception.
red_flags: Severe nodulocystic acne causing scarring — early isotretinoin referral.
Acne conglobata or fulminans — emergency dermatology.
Significant psychological impact or depression related to acne.
support_steps: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5% nightly — start slowly to prevent dryness; builds up over 2–4 weeks.
Adapalene 0.1% gel every other night, then nightly — causes initial purging (normal).
Non-comedogenic moisturiser and SPF30+ sunscreen — essential with retinoids.
Consistent use for 6–8 weeks before judging effectiveness.
Refer to dermatology for severe, scarring, or isotretinoin-candidate acne.

topics.title: Psoriasis
core_guidance: Psoriasis: chronic autoimmune skin condition — well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales.
Common sites: elbows, knees, scalp, lower back, umbilicus, and nail pitting.
Mild-moderate: topical calcipotriol (vitamin D analogue) + betamethasone cream — apply once daily.
Scalp psoriasis: calcipotriol scalp solution or coal tar shampoo.
Severe/extensive: phototherapy (UVB), methotrexate, ciclosporin, or biologics (adalimumab, ustekinumab) — specialist.
red_flags: Erythrodermic psoriasis (>90% body surface): thermoregulation failure — emergency.
Pustular psoriasis with fever — systemically unwell, emergency.
Psoriatic arthritis: joint swelling with psoriasis — DMARDs/biologics needed.
support_steps: Calcipotriol + betamethasone ointment once daily for 4 weeks, then reduce frequency.
Emollients applied generously daily to reduce scale and prevent cracking.
Avoid triggers: streptococcal infection, NSAIDs, beta-blockers, lithium, alcohol, stress.
Coal tar shampoo for scalp involvement — leave on 5–10 minutes before rinsing.
Dermatology referral for >10% body surface area involvement or joint involvement.

topics.title: Scabies and head lice
core_guidance: Scabies: intense nocturnal itch, burrows in finger webs/wrists/genitals — Sarcoptes scabiei mite.
Treatment: permethrin 5% cream head to toe overnight (8–12 hours); repeat in 7 days. All household contacts treated simultaneously.
Alternative: oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg single dose, repeat after 2 weeks — effective for crusted scabies.
Head lice: visual diagnosis on wet-combing. Dimeticone 4% lotion or permethrin 1% rinse.
Wash clothing and bedding at >60°C on day of treatment; seal non-washable items in bag for 72 hours.
red_flags: Norwegian/crusted scabies (hyperkeratotic, highly contagious) — requires oral ivermectin + topical.
Persistent itch >4 weeks after treatment — reinfestation or treatment failure.
Secondary bacterial infection from scratching — may need topical/oral antibiotics.
support_steps: Permethrin 5% cream: apply to entire body (neck to toe, include scalp in children/elderly), rinse after 8–12 hours.
Treat all household members on same day — untreated contacts re-infect.
Wash all clothing, bedding, and towels at ≥60°C on day of treatment.
Antihistamine (cetirizine) for itch relief — itching may persist 2–4 weeks after successful treatment.
Head lice: dimeticone lotion twice, 7 days apart + wet-combing with fine-toothed comb.

topics.title: Hair loss and alopecia
core_guidance: Androgenetic alopecia (pattern baldness): most common cause in men and women — DHT-mediated follicle miniaturisation.
Telogen effluvium: diffuse shedding 2–4 months after physiological stress (illness, surgery, childbirth, crash dieting).
Alopecia areata: patchy hair loss, autoimmune — intralesional corticosteroid injections or topical immunotherapy.
Minoxidil 2% (women) or 5% (men) solution applied twice daily — prevents progression, modest regrowth.
Finasteride 1mg daily (men only — not for women of childbearing age) — blocks DHT, effective for pattern baldness.
red_flags: Diffuse hair loss with fatigue — check thyroid (TSH), ferritin, FBC, B12.
Scarring alopecia (lichen planopilaris) — permanent loss if untreated, urgent dermatology.
Hair loss with systemic symptoms — lupus, secondary syphilis, nutritional deficiency.
support_steps: Telogen effluvium: reassure — typically reverses 6–12 months after trigger resolves; check ferritin and thyroid.
Minoxidil 5% solution twice daily: apply to dry scalp — takes 4–6 months for visible effect; must continue indefinitely.
Ensure ferritin >70 mcg/L for optimal hair growth — iron deficiency is reversible cause.
Avoid tight hairstyles, heat styling, and harsh chemical treatments.
Dermatology referral for alopecia areata, scarring alopecia, or uncertain diagnosis.

topics.title: Cellulitis and skin infections
core_guidance: Cellulitis: bacterial infection of dermis/subcutaneous tissue — red, warm, swollen, tender skin with spreading margins.
Mark the advancing edge with a pen at presentation — allows monitoring of spread.
Mild-moderate (no systemic symptoms): oral flucloxacillin 500mg 4x daily for 5–7 days (or cefalexin if penicillin-allergic).
Impetigo: honey-coloured crusts on face — topical mupirocin for localised; oral flucloxacillin for widespread.
Skin abscess: requires incision and drainage, not just antibiotics.
red_flags: Cellulitis with fever >38°C, rigors, or haemodynamic instability — IV antibiotics needed, hospital admission.
Necrotising fasciitis: rapidly spreading with dusky/greyish skin, severe pain, disproportionate systemically — surgical emergency.
Cellulitis around the eye (periorbital/orbital cellulitis) — hospital admission.
Cellulitis not improving after 48–72 hours on oral antibiotics.
support_steps: Mark the leading edge of redness with pen/marker to monitor spread.
Flucloxacillin 500mg 4x daily on empty stomach for 5–7 days.
Elevate affected limb above heart level to reduce swelling.
Return if redness extends beyond the mark, develops blisters, or systemic symptoms appear.
Hospital if failed oral antibiotics, rapid progression, or systemic illness.

topics.title: Urinary tract infection (UTI) — detailed
core_guidance: Uncomplicated lower UTI (cystitis): dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain — no fever.
First-line antibiotics: nitrofurantoin 100mg MR twice daily for 5 days OR trimethoprim 200mg twice daily for 7 days.
Drink plenty of water — increases urine flow and bacterial clearance.
Upper UTI (pyelonephritis): fever, loin pain, rigors — treat with ciprofloxacin 500mg 7–14 days; hospital if severe.
Urine dipstick and MSU culture guide treatment — especially in recurrent UTI.
red_flags: Fever >38°C with UTI symptoms — suspected pyelonephritis, needs 7–14 day course.
UTI in pregnancy — always treat even if asymptomatic; use cefalexin.
Recurrent UTIs (>3/year) — investigation for anatomical abnormality or resistant organism.
support_steps: Nitrofurantoin 100mg MR twice daily with food for 5 days (women); or trimethoprim 200mg twice daily for 7 days.
Drink 2+ litres water daily; urinate after sexual intercourse.
Urine dipstick or MSU to confirm before treating recurrent UTIs.
Cranberry products have modest evidence for recurrence prevention.
Post-coital low-dose prophylaxis or self-start antibiotic for frequently recurring UTI — GP discussion.

topics.title: Kidney stones (nephrolithiasis)
core_guidance: Renal colic: severe loin-to-groin pain (waves), haematuria, nausea/vomiting — one of the most severe pains.
Analgesia: diclofenac 75mg IM or 100mg suppository is most effective; add paracetamol/opioids.
Stones <5mm: 80% pass spontaneously in 4 weeks. Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) speed passage of ureteric stones.
High fluid intake (3+ L/day) is the most important long-term prevention for all stone types.
Dietary advice depends on stone type: calcium oxalate (most common) — reduce oxalate foods (spinach, chocolate), maintain normal calcium intake.
red_flags: Fever with renal colic — infected obstructed kidney, emergency (sepsis risk).
Anuria (no urine) with bilateral stones or single kidney.
Persistent pain uncontrolled by analgesia — hospital assessment.
support_steps: Diclofenac 100mg suppository or 75mg IM for acute colic — most effective analgesic.
Drink 3+ litres water daily to flush stone — dilute urine reduces new stone formation.
Tamsulosin 400 mcg daily for ureteric stones <10mm — facilitates passage.
Strain urine to collect stone for analysis (determines stone type and dietary advice).
Urology referral for stones >10mm, bilateral stones, infection, or failure to pass.

topics.title: Erectile dysfunction
core_guidance: ED is primarily vascular in older men — it is a cardiovascular risk marker (assess CV risk).
Common causes: vascular disease, diabetes, hypogonadism, medications (beta-blockers, SSRIs, thiazides), psychological.
Lifestyle first: smoking cessation, weight loss, exercise — improve endothelial function.
PDE5 inhibitors: sildenafil (Viagra) 25–100mg as needed, 1 hour before sex; tadalafil 5mg daily or 10–20mg as needed.
Contraindicated with nitrates (GTN, isosorbide) — dangerous hypotension.
red_flags: ED in a young man without obvious cause — check testosterone, prolactin, thyroid.
Sudden onset ED after new medication — likely drug-induced, consider switching.
ED with perineal/pelvic trauma or surgery — nerve/vascular injury.
support_steps: Stop smoking, reduce alcohol, achieve healthy weight — most impactful lifestyle steps.
Check HbA1c, testosterone, lipids, BP — treat underlying causes.
Sildenafil 50mg 1 hour before sex on empty stomach (food reduces absorption).
Tadalafil 5mg daily: allows spontaneity without planning.
Do NOT combine PDE5 inhibitors with nitrates (GTN) — risk of fatal hypotension.

topics.title: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)
core_guidance: BPH: benign enlargement of prostate — lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS): hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying, frequency, nocturia.
Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin 400 mcg or alfuzosin 10mg) — relax smooth muscle; symptom relief within days.
5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride 5mg): shrink prostate over 3–6 months; best for large prostates.
Avoid caffeine and evening fluids to reduce nocturia.
Urodynamics and PSA to differentiate from prostate cancer.
red_flags: Acute urinary retention (no urine, painful distended bladder) — emergency catheterisation.
Recurrent UTIs, visible haematuria, or rising PSA — investigate for malignancy.
Hydronephrosis or renal impairment from chronic retention.
support_steps: Tamsulosin 400 mcg once daily after food — works within days for flow improvement.
Finasteride 5mg daily: takes 3–6 months for size reduction; reduces PSA by 50%.
Limit fluids after 6pm; reduce caffeine and alcohol.
Double voiding: urinate, wait 30 seconds, urinate again.
Urgent catheterisation for acute retention; urology referral for surgery (TURP) if medications inadequate.

topics.title: Chronic kidney disease (CKD)
core_guidance: CKD: eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² for >3 months or kidney damage markers (proteinuria, haematuria).
Most common causes: diabetes and hypertension — optimal control of both is the most effective treatment.
ACE inhibitors/ARBs: first-line for proteinuric CKD — reduce proteinuria and slow progression.
Avoid nephrotoxic drugs: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast dye (or prehydrate if necessary).
Stage G5 (eGFR <15): renal replacement therapy planning — dialysis or transplant.
red_flags: Rapidly rising creatinine — acute-on-chronic or AKI precipitant (dehydration, sepsis, NSAIDs).
Hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5) — cardiac risk, emergency management.
Uraemic symptoms: confusion, pericarditis, severe nausea — uraemic emergency.
support_steps: Blood pressure target: <130/80 mmHg — use ACE inhibitor or ARB first-line.
Hold ACE inhibitor/ARB if acutely unwell with vomiting/diarrhoea (sick-day rules).
Dietary protein moderation (0.8g/kg/day) in advanced CKD.
Avoid NSAIDs — use paracetamol for pain.
Annual monitoring: eGFR, urine ACR (albumin:creatinine), electrolytes, FBC, calcium, phosphate.

topics.title: Glaucoma
core_guidance: Glaucoma: optic nerve damage, usually from raised intraocular pressure (IOP) — causes irreversible visual field loss.
Open-angle glaucoma (most common): asymptomatic until advanced — detected on routine eye test.
Angle-closure glaucoma: acute painful red eye, halos around lights, headache, vomiting — emergency.
Treatment: prostaglandin analogue drops (latanoprost 0.005%, once nightly) first-line.
Regular eye pressure and visual field monitoring — lifelong condition.
red_flags: Acute angle-closure: severely painful red eye with sudden vision loss and halos — ophthalmology emergency.
Sudden loss of peripheral or central vision — emergency ophthalmology.
IOP >30 mmHg on routine check — urgent ophthalmology review.
support_steps: Latanoprost 0.005% drop once nightly in affected eye — most common first-line.
Apply drop, close eye, press inner corner (nasolacrimal occlusion) for 1–2 minutes.
Never stop glaucoma drops without ophthalmologist advice — causes IOP rebound.
Regular 3–6 monthly check-ups: IOP, visual fields, and optic disc imaging.
Family members of glaucoma patients: annual eye pressure check after age 40.

topics.title: Dry eye syndrome
core_guidance: Dry eye: inadequate tear quality or quantity — burning, grittiness, watering (reflex tearing), blurred vision.
Exacerbating factors: screen time, air conditioning, contact lenses, antihistamines, antidepressants.
Lubricating eye drops (artificial tears): hypromellose, carbomer gel, or hyaluronate drops — use as often as needed.
Blepharitis (lid margin inflammation) is a common cause — daily warm compress + lid massage + lid hygiene.
Avoid preserved drops for frequent use — use preservative-free unit-dose vials.
red_flags: Dry eye with joint pain, dry mouth — Sjögren's syndrome, systemic investigation.
Corneal abrasion from severe dry eye — urgent ophthalmology.
Vision worsening or not improving with drops.
support_steps: Preservative-free lubricating drops (e.g., Hylo-Tear, Systane Ultra) — use every 1–2 hours during acute episodes.
Warm compress over closed eyelids for 10 minutes twice daily — melts blocked Meibomian glands.
Lid hygiene: clean lid margin with diluted baby shampoo or Blephasol wipe.
Increase omega-3 intake (oily fish, fish oil supplement) — modest benefit for tear quality.
Screen breaks: 20-20-20 rule (every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds).

topics.title: Stye and chalazion
core_guidance: Stye (hordeolum): acute painful red swelling on eyelid from bacterial infection of gland.
Chalazion: chronic painless firm eyelid lump from blocked Meibomian gland.
Treatment: warm compress 4x daily for 10 minutes + gentle massage — resolves most styes and chalazia.
Topical chloramphenicol ointment if secondary bacterial infection present.
Persistent chalazion (>4–6 weeks) may need incision and curettage by ophthalmologist.
red_flags: Spreading cellulitis from a stye — oral antibiotics and ophthalmology review.
Recurrent chalazia or eyelid thickening — biopsy to exclude sebaceous cell carcinoma.
Stye with fever or pre-septal cellulitis.
support_steps: Warm compress (clean cloth in warm water): hold firmly over closed eyelid for 10 minutes, 4 times daily.
Gentle massage after compress in direction of eyelid margin.
Do not squeeze or pop a stye.
Maintain eyelid hygiene to prevent recurrence.
Ophthalmology referral if not resolved after 4–6 weeks or if recurrent.

topics.title: Tinnitus
core_guidance: Tinnitus: perception of sound without external source — ringing, buzzing, hissing, clicking.
Most common cause: noise-induced hearing loss. Other causes: cerumen impaction, otitis media, Ménière's, medication (NSAIDs, loop diuretics, quinine).
No curative treatment exists — management focuses on habituation and reducing distress.
Sound therapy (white noise generators, background music) masks tinnitus and aids habituation.
CBT for tinnitus: strongest evidence for reducing distress and disability — not hearing the sound less, but being less bothered by it.
red_flags: Unilateral tinnitus — investigate for vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) with MRI.
Pulsatile tinnitus (synchronous with heartbeat) — vascular cause, needs urgent investigation.
Sudden onset tinnitus with hearing loss and vertigo — Ménière's or sudden sensorineural hearing loss.
support_steps: Remove earwax — treat any reversible cause first.
Sound enrichment: background music, fans, white noise apps — avoid silence which makes tinnitus louder.
Reduce caffeine, alcohol, and salt intake (can worsen tinnitus for some).
Ear protection in noisy environments — prevent further hearing damage.
CBT referral for significant distress — most effective psychological approach.

topics.title: Tonsillitis and sore throat
core_guidance: Most sore throats are viral — antibiotics not needed. FeverPAIN or Centor scores guide antibiotic use.
FeverPAIN ≥4: offer antibiotic (phenoxymethylpenicillin 500mg 4x daily for 10 days — longer than usual to prevent rheumatic fever).
Symptomatic relief: paracetamol/ibuprofen, difflam spray, ice cream, cold fluids.
Glandular fever (EBV): prominent fatigue, bilateral lymphadenopathy — avoid amoxicillin (causes rash). Rest, no contact sport for 4 weeks.
Tonsillectomy for recurrent tonsillitis (Paradise criteria: 7 episodes/year, or 5/year for 2 years, or 3/year for 3 years).
red_flags: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy): unilateral uvula deviation, muffled voice, trismus — urgent ENT/A&E.
Stridor or difficulty breathing from severe tonsillar enlargement.
Sore throat with neck stiffness — exclude meningitis/epiglottitis.
support_steps: Paracetamol 1g + ibuprofen 400mg alternately — better together than either alone for throat pain.
Difflam (benzydamine) spray or gargle — topical anti-inflammatory.
Phenoxymethylpenicillin if bacterial (score-guided): 500mg 4x daily for 10 days.
If penicillin-allergic: azithromycin 500mg daily for 5 days.
A&E immediately for drooling, stridor, or inability to swallow own saliva.

topics.title: BPPV and Ménière's disease
core_guidance: BPPV: brief (seconds to <1 min) positional vertigo triggered by head movement — otolithic debris in semicircular canal.
Epley manoeuvre: 80% success rate at first session — can be taught for home use.
Ménière's disease: episodic vertigo >20 minutes, unilateral fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, ear fullness.
Ménière's management: betahistine 16mg 3x daily (or 24mg twice daily), low-salt diet (<1.5g/day), diuretics.
Prochlorperazine 5mg: symptomatic vestibular sedation during acute attack — max 2 weeks.
red_flags: Sudden onset vertigo with headache, diplopia, or dysarthria — posterior circulation stroke.
Hearing loss accompanying new vertigo — audiologist and ENT evaluation.
BPPV not responding to 3 Epley manoeuvres — atypical or different diagnosis.
support_steps: BPPV: Epley manoeuvre — can be performed at home using video instructions, highly effective.
Ménière's: strict low-salt diet is the most controllable treatment factor.
Betahistine 16mg 3x daily taken consistently — takes weeks for full effect.
Vestibular rehabilitation exercises for persistent imbalance after acute vertigo.
ENT referral for Ménière's if medical management fails — intratympanic injection or surgery options.

topics.title: Pregnancy health and antenatal care
core_guidance: Folic acid 400 mcg/day from conception through first trimester — prevents neural tube defects.
First trimester: nausea (eat small frequent meals, ginger, vitamin B6); fatigue (rest); avoid alcohol, smoking, raw meat.
Antenatal care: 8–12 week booking appointment, anomaly scan at 18–21 weeks, glucose tolerance test at 24–28 weeks.
Safe medications in pregnancy: paracetamol (first-line pain/fever). Avoid NSAIDs after 28 weeks.
Danger signs: vaginal bleeding, severe abdominal pain, severe headache with visual disturbance, no fetal movement.
red_flags: Vaginal bleeding at any gestation — emergency assessment.
Pre-eclampsia: headache, visual changes, upper abdominal pain, facial swelling after 20 weeks — emergency.
Decreased or absent fetal movements after 28 weeks — same-day assessment.
support_steps: Start folic acid 400 mcg (or 5mg if high risk) before conception and until 12 weeks.
Vitamin D 10 mcg (400 IU) throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Ginger tea, dry crackers, or vitamin B6 25mg 3x daily for nausea.
Avoid: alcohol, raw/undercooked meat, unpasteurised cheese, vitamin A supplements.
Register with a midwife/obstetrician at 8–10 weeks for structured antenatal care.

topics.title: Menopause
core_guidance: Menopause: 12 months after last menstrual period (average 51 years). Perimenopause: years before.
Symptoms: hot flushes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, mood changes, sleep disruption, joint pain, cognitive changes.
Hormone replacement therapy (HRT): most effective treatment for vasomotor symptoms — oestrogen + progestogen (if uterus intact).
Topical vaginal oestrogen: highly effective for genitourinary symptoms; minimal systemic absorption — safe long-term.
Non-hormonal options: CBT (effective for flushes), SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine), clonidine.
red_flags: Postmenopausal bleeding — endometrial cancer investigation with ultrasound and biopsy.
HRT contraindications: personal history of breast cancer, DVT/PE, unexplained vaginal bleeding.
Surgical menopause (oophorectomy) in young women — HRT strongly recommended to prevent CVD and osteoporosis.
support_steps: HRT: lowest effective dose, transdermal route (patch/gel) preferred (lower DVT risk than oral).
Vaginal oestrogen (Vagifem/Ovestin): insert nightly for 2 weeks, then twice weekly — safe indefinitely.
CBT for hot flushes: reduces frequency and distress by up to 50%.
Ensure calcium 1200mg/day and vitamin D 800 IU/day — osteoporosis prevention.
DEXA scan at menopause if additional risk factors; consider bisphosphonate alongside HRT if T-score <-2.5.

topics.title: Vaginal infections — BV, thrush, and trichomoniasis
core_guidance: BV (bacterial vaginosis): fishy odour, thin grey-white discharge, no itching. Treatment: metronidazole 400mg twice daily for 5 days OR 2g single dose.
Vaginal thrush (candidiasis): itching, thick white cottage-cheese discharge, soreness. Treatment: clotrimazole pessary 500mg single dose or fluconazole 150mg oral single dose.
Trichomoniasis: frothy yellow-green discharge, itching, fishy smell — STI. Treatment: metronidazole 2g single dose; treat partner.
BV and trichomoniasis in pregnancy — treat with metronidazole (after first trimester); fluconazole avoided in pregnancy.
Recurrent thrush (>4/year): fluconazole 150mg weekly for 6 months (maintenance).
red_flags: Vaginal discharge with pelvic pain, fever, or cervical motion tenderness — pelvic inflammatory disease.
Vaginal symptoms in pregnancy — specific treatment regimens needed.
New partner or multiple partners with vaginal discharge — STI screen recommended.
support_steps: BV: metronidazole 400mg twice daily for 5–7 days; avoid alcohol during course.
Thrush: clotrimazole 500mg pessary once at night OR fluconazole 150mg oral (not in pregnancy).
Avoid perfumed soaps, bubble baths, and douching — disrupts vaginal flora.
Wear cotton underwear and loose-fitting clothing.
Recurrent thrush: check blood glucose (diabetes risk), and ask about corticosteroid inhaler (oral candidiasis spreading).

topics.title: Endometriosis
core_guidance: Endometriosis: endometrial tissue outside uterus — severe dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, pelvic pain, subfertility.
Average delay to diagnosis is 7–10 years — high index of suspicion needed.
Medical management: NSAIDs + combined OCP (or progestogen-only pill) as first-line.
GnRH analogues (e.g., goserelin): temporary menopause state — highly effective for 6 months; add-back HRT reduces side effects.
Definitive diagnosis: laparoscopy with biopsy. Surgical excision improves symptoms and fertility.
red_flags: Endometrioma (chocolate cyst) >4cm — laparoscopic cystectomy before IVF.
Severe pain uncontrolled by analgesics — specialist pain management.
Deep endometriosis involving bowel or bladder — MDT surgical approach.
support_steps: NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg twice daily) started 1–2 days before expected period onset.
Combined OCP taken continuously (skip placebo pills) — reduces monthly endometrial stimulation.
Heat therapy for pelvic pain relief during flares.
Referral to endometriosis specialist — gynaecologist with laparoscopic expertise.
Fertility should be discussed early — endometriosis affects ovarian reserve over time.

topics.title: Testicular health and self-examination
core_guidance: Testicular torsion: sudden severe scrotal pain, elevated testis — SURGICAL EMERGENCY; viability falls rapidly after 6 hours.
Testicular cancer: painless testicular lump — most common solid cancer in men 15–35. Excellent cure rate if caught early.
Monthly self-examination: after warm bath, gently roll each testis between fingers — feel for lumps, swelling, or changes.
Epididymo-orchitis: gradual onset scrotal pain with fever — usually bacterial; doxycycline + ciprofloxacin for 2–4 weeks.
Varicocele: 'bag of worms' feeling in left scrotum — can cause subfertility.
red_flags: Sudden severe scrotal pain — testicular torsion until proven otherwise; emergency surgery within 6 hours.
Painless testicular lump — urgent ultrasound and tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG, LDH).
Testicular pain with fever and urinary symptoms — epididymo-orchitis, needs antibiotics.
support_steps: Any scrotal pain with suddenness or severity: A&E immediately — torsion is time-critical.
Painless lump: urgent GP referral for scrotal ultrasound within 2 weeks.
Epididymo-orchitis: doxycycline 100mg twice daily + ciprofloxacin 500mg twice daily for 2–4 weeks.
Testicular self-exam monthly after warm bath.
Testicular cancer: highly treatable — do not delay presentation out of embarrassment.

topics.title: Men's preventive health
core_guidance: Men are less likely to seek healthcare — proactive health checking is essential.
BP, BMI, waist circumference, cholesterol, and HbA1c screen from age 40 (or earlier with risk factors).
Mental health: men have 3x higher suicide rate than women — lower threshold to ask directly about mood and thoughts.
Testosterone deficiency (hypogonadism): fatigue, low mood, low libido, poor concentration, reduced muscle mass — check serum testosterone in the morning.
Bowel cancer screening: ages 60–74 (FIT test) in national programmes.
red_flags: Suicidal thoughts — men often mask distress with anger or alcohol.
Erectile dysfunction as a cardiovascular risk marker — full metabolic assessment.
PSA rising or urinary symptoms — prostate evaluation.
support_steps: Annual health check: weight, BMI, BP, waist measurement from age 40.
Fasting glucose and cholesterol every 3–5 years.
Bowel cancer: FIT test annually from age 50 or per national programme.
Skin: annual skin check for any changing moles.
Mental health: talk to someone — GP, counsellor, or helpline. Asking for help is a strength.

topics.title: Childhood vaccination schedule
core_guidance: Bangladesh EPI schedule: BCG at birth, OPV+PCV+Penta (DPT+HepB+Hib) at 6/10/14 weeks, MR at 9 months, Measles-Rubella booster at 15 months.
BCG: protects against TB meningitis and miliary TB — scar indicates successful take.
Pentavalent vaccine: covers diphtheria, pertussis, tetanus, Hepatitis B, and Haemophilus influenzae type B.
MR vaccine: measles and rubella — two doses required for 95%+ population immunity.
Vaccine myths: vaccines do not cause autism; mild fever/soreness after vaccination is normal and expected.
red_flags: High fever >39°C >48 hours after vaccination — assess for alternative cause.
Seizure after MMR/febrile illness (febrile seizure) — usually benign but needs assessment.
Anaphylaxis after vaccine (rare) — call 999; all vaccine sites should hold adrenaline.
support_steps: Paracetamol 10–15mg/kg after vaccination if feverish — not as pre-treatment (blunts immune response).
Keep vaccination card safe — cumulative record is important for school and travel.
Catch-up vaccinations available for children who missed doses.
Inform vaccinator of known egg allergy before MMR; severe allergies need observation post-vaccination.
Breastfeeding during vaccination significantly reduces infant distress.

topics.title: Common childhood infections
core_guidance: Chickenpox: itchy vesicular rash in crops, fever — calamine lotion, paracetamol; NO aspirin (Reye's). Isolate until all lesions crusted.
Measles: fever + 3 Cs (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) + Koplik spots + maculopapular rash. Notifiable disease — vitamin A supplementation.
Hand, foot and mouth disease (Coxsackie): vesicles in mouth, hands, and feet — self-limiting in 7–10 days; paracetamol for pain.
Roseola (HHV-6): high fever 3–5 days, then rosy rash appears as fever breaks — fever management only.
Scarlet fever: strep throat + sandpaper rash — penicillin V for 10 days; notifiable in UK.
red_flags: Chickenpox with secondary bacterial infection (spreading redness, fever) — antibiotics needed.
Measles with encephalitis: seizures, confusion — hospital emergency.
Any child with high fever and non-blanching purpuric rash — meningococcal, call 999.
support_steps: Chickenpox: calamine lotion for itching; cool bath; trim nails to prevent scratching and scarring.
Isolate chickenpox from pregnant women and immunocompromised individuals.
Measles: vitamin A 100,000–200,000 IU immediately (reduces complications).
All childhood rashes with fever: blanching test — press a glass on rash; non-blanching = emergency.
Maintain hydration during any febrile illness.

topics.title: Child nutrition and growth
core_guidance: Exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months; complementary foods introduced at 6 months while continuing breastfeeding.
Complementary foods: single-ingredient purées initially, progressing to family foods by 12 months.
Iron-rich foods essential from 6 months: meat, fish, lentils, fortified cereals — iron deficiency anaemia common in infants.
Avoid: honey under 1 year (botulism), whole nuts under 5 years (choking), cow's milk as main drink under 1 year.
Growth monitoring: weight and height every 3 months using WHO growth charts.
red_flags: Weight faltering (crossing 2 centile lines downward) — urgent medical assessment.
Persistent vomiting, diarrhea, or blood in stool in an infant.
Severe acute malnutrition: MUAC <11.5cm, oedema, or weight-for-height <-3SD.
support_steps: Introduce diverse foods early (from 6 months) — reduces food allergy risk.
Offer allergenic foods (peanut butter, egg, fish) early and maintain regular exposure.
Iron supplement (1mg/kg/day) for breastfed infants from 4–6 months if not receiving iron-fortified formula.
Fortified complementary foods or therapeutic feeding for undernourished children.
Community nutrition programme and RUTF (ready-to-use therapeutic food) for severe acute malnutrition.

topics.title: Breastfeeding and infant feeding
core_guidance: Breastfeeding: initiated within 1 hour of birth; colostrum is rich in antibodies and essential.
Correct latch: wide mouth, lower lip flanged out, most areola in mouth — reduces nipple pain.
Feed on demand (8–12 times/24 hours in newborns); let baby finish first breast before offering second.
Mastitis: red painful breast area, fever — continue breastfeeding (empties breast), antibiotics (flucloxacillin), warm compress.
Formula feeding: sterilise all equipment; use boiled water cooled to 70°C to make formula; do not microwave.
red_flags: Breast abscess: fluctuant painful lump with fever despite antibiotics — surgical drainage needed.
Infant losing >10% birth weight after day 5 — feeding assessment urgently.
Mastitis not improving after 24–48 hours of antibiotics.
support_steps: Establish latch with a lactation consultant — most problems stem from poor positioning.
Painful nipples: lanolin cream after feeds; air-dry; check latch.
Low milk supply: frequent feeding is the main driver of supply — do not supplement early without advice.
Mastitis: continue feeding/expressing, flucloxacillin 500mg 4x daily for 10–14 days.
Mental health: breastfeeding difficulties cause significant anxiety — support and reassurance are essential.

topics.title: Dengue fever
core_guidance: Dengue: sudden high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia ('breakbone fever'), rash, thrombocytopaenia.
No specific antiviral — management is supportive: hydration, paracetamol for fever.
AVOID NSAIDs and aspirin — increases bleeding risk due to thrombocytopaenia and platelet dysfunction.
Warning signs of severe dengue: abdominal pain, vomiting, bleeding, rapid deterioration — hospital immediately.
Dengue shock syndrome and dengue haemorrhagic fever — require IV fluid resuscitation.
red_flags: Warning signs: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding from gums/nose/skin, restlessness.
Platelet count <20,000 or any spontaneous bleeding.
Rapid deterioration, cold extremities, or altered consciousness — dengue shock.
support_steps: Paracetamol 1g every 4–6 hours — ONLY for fever control. No ibuprofen, aspirin, or diclofenac.
Oral rehydration: 2–3 litres fluid daily; coconut water and ORS are helpful.
Monitor platelet count daily during febrile phase.
Hospital admission for platelet <50,000, warning signs, or severe disease.
Mosquito protection: repellents, nets, long sleeves — Aedes mosquito bites in daytime.

topics.title: Typhoid fever
core_guidance: Typhoid: Salmonella typhi — sustained fever (stepladder pattern), headache, abdominal pain, relative bradycardia, rose spots on abdomen.
Diagnosis: blood culture (gold standard); Widal test has poor sensitivity/specificity.
Treatment: azithromycin 1g daily for 5–7 days (fluoroquinolone resistance common in Bangladesh).
Alternative: cefixime 400mg twice daily for 7–14 days.
Prevention: typhoid vaccine, safe food and water practices.
red_flags: Intestinal perforation: sudden abdominal pain, peritonitis — surgical emergency.
Typhoid encephalopathy: confusion, seizures — severe complication.
Fever not responding after 5 days of antibiotics — drug resistance, change antibiotic.
support_steps: Azithromycin 1g (or 500mg if tolerated) daily for 7 days — most effective for uncomplicated typhoid in Bangladesh.
Strict rest and high-calorie soft diet.
Oral rehydration; IV fluids if vomiting prevents adequate oral intake.
Report to public health — notifiable disease in Bangladesh.
Typhoid vaccination for travellers and household contacts.

topics.title: Malaria
core_guidance: Malaria: cyclical fever with chills, sweating, headache, myalgia — Plasmodium falciparum is most dangerous.
Diagnosis: rapid diagnostic test (RDT) or thick/thin blood film.
Bangladesh treatment (P. falciparum): artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) 6-dose regimen over 3 days.
P. vivax: chloroquine + primaquine (check G6PD status before primaquine — haemolysis risk).
Severe malaria: IV artesunate — hospital admission essential.
red_flags: Cerebral malaria: confusion, seizures, coma — emergency IV artesunate.
Severe malaria with respiratory distress, haemoglobinuria (blackwater fever), or AKI.
Malaria in pregnancy — always high-risk, aggressive treatment.
support_steps: Artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem): take with food — 4 tablets at 0, 8, 24, 36, 48, 60 hours.
Complete the full course even if feeling better after day 2.
Paracetamol for fever and myalgia.
Test all household members if multiple people febrile.
Prevention: permethrin-treated nets, DEET repellent, indoor residual spraying.

topics.title: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs)
core_guidance: Chlamydia: most common STI — often asymptomatic; treat with azithromycin 1g single dose or doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 7 days.
Gonorrhoea: purulent discharge, dysuria — ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (oral resistance is high).
Syphilis: primary (painless ulcer/chancre), secondary (rash), latent/tertiary — benzathine penicillin 2.4 MU IM.
Genital herpes: painful vesicles/ulcers — aciclovir 400mg 3x daily for 5 days; suppressive therapy for frequent recurrences.
Partner notification is essential for all STIs — treat partners concurrently.
red_flags: Pelvic inflammatory disease in women: pelvic pain, fever, discharge — risk of ectopic and infertility.
Syphilis in pregnancy — risk of congenital syphilis, treat immediately.
Any ulcer or unusual genital lesion in HIV-positive person.
support_steps: Full STI screen at sexual health clinic for any new partner or symptoms.
Chlamydia: azithromycin 1g single dose; notify partner for concurrent treatment.
Gonorrhoea: ceftriaxone 500mg IM — do not use oral due to resistance.
Condoms: only contraception that significantly reduces STI transmission.
HIV testing recommended alongside STI screen — annual for sexually active.

topics.title: HIV and AIDS
core_guidance: HIV: human immunodeficiency virus — transmitted via blood, sexual contact, breast milk.
Testing: recommended annually for sexually active adults; 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab test detects at 4 weeks.
ART (antiretroviral therapy): started immediately after diagnosis — single-tablet regimens; highly effective.
Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U): undetectable viral load prevents sexual transmission.
PEP (post-exposure prophylaxis): HIV medications started within 72 hours of exposure (preferably <4 hours).
red_flags: CD4 <200 cells/mm³ — AIDS-defining illness risk; Pneumocystis prophylaxis with cotrimoxazole.
Opportunistic infections: TB, PCP, CMV retinitis, cryptococcal meningitis — specialist management.
HIV encephalopathy or AIDS dementia.
support_steps: HIV test: confidential, available at sexual health clinics and hospitals.
PEP: start within 72 hours of potential exposure; available at A&E and sexual health clinics.
PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis): highly effective (>99%) for HIV-negative high-risk individuals.
ART adherence: take every day at the same time — missed doses allow viral replication.
Psychosocial support: HIV stigma causes significant mental health burden — connect with peer support groups.

topics.title: Chickenpox and shingles
core_guidance: Chickenpox (varicella): crops of itchy vesicles, mild fever — contagious from 2 days before rash until all crusted.
Treatment: supportive — calamine, paracetamol. Aciclovir 800mg 5x daily for 7 days if adult or immunocompromised (start within 24h of rash).
Shingles (herpes zoster): reactivation of latent VZV — painful unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash.
Shingles treatment: valaciclovir 1g 3x daily for 7 days or aciclovir 800mg 5x daily — start within 72 hours of rash.
Post-herpetic neuralgia: persistent pain after shingles resolves — amitriptyline, gabapentin, or topical lidocaine.
red_flags: Chickenpox in pregnancy — risk of pneumonitis and foetal harm; VZIG prophylaxis.
Shingles affecting the eye (herpes zoster ophthalmicus) — urgent ophthalmology.
Immunocompromised patient with chickenpox — IV aciclovir, hospital admission.
support_steps: Chickenpox: calamine lotion for itch; cool bath with bicarbonate of soda; trim nails.
Avoid contact with pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised during infectious period.
Shingles: start valaciclovir within 72 hours; pain management with ibuprofen/paracetamol.
Post-herpetic neuralgia: amitriptyline 10–25mg nocte; gabapentin 300mg 3x daily.
Shingles vaccination (Shingrix) recommended from age 50 — 90% effective.

topics.title: Cancer screening and early detection
core_guidance: Breast: monthly self-exam; mammogram every 2 years from age 40–50 (guidelines vary). Any new lump, skin change, nipple discharge — urgent referral.
Cervical: Pap smear or HPV test every 3–5 years from age 25. HPV vaccine (Gardasil) ideally before sexual debut.
Colorectal: FIT test (faecal immunochemical test) annually from age 50; colonoscopy for positive test.
Prostate: PSA test discussed from age 50 (or 45 with family history) — not universal screening; consider symptoms.
Oral: annual dental check detects early oral cancer — examine mouth for non-healing ulcers >3 weeks.
red_flags: Any new lump, unexplained weight loss, change in mole, or persistent cough >3 weeks — urgent GP assessment.
Postmenopausal vaginal bleeding — urgent endometrial investigation.
Blood in urine or stool without clear cause.
support_steps: Breast: monthly self-exam in shower; familiarise yourself with normal texture.
Cervical smear: attend every invitation; don't skip because there are no symptoms.
HPV vaccination: most effective when given before sexual debut (9–14 years optimal).
Bowel cancer: reduce processed meat, increase fibre, maintain healthy weight.
Any change from normal — tell your doctor. Early detection saves lives.

topics.title: Adult vaccination schedule
core_guidance: Annual influenza vaccine: recommended for all >65, pregnant women, healthcare workers, and those with chronic conditions.
Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV13 + PPSV23): for all >65 and immunocompromised; reduces pneumonia mortality.
Tetanus booster: every 10 years or after dirty wound if last dose >5 years.
Hepatitis B: 3-dose series for unvaccinated adults — essential for healthcare workers.
HPV: Gardasil 9 up to age 45 in previously unvaccinated adults.
red_flags: Anaphylaxis after previous dose of a vaccine — allergy review before re-vaccination.
Immunocompromised patients: avoid live vaccines (MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever).
Pregnancy: avoid live vaccines; influenza and pertussis are recommended.
support_steps: Flu vaccine: October–November every year.
Tetanus booster: after any significant wound if >5 years since last dose.
Hepatitis B: 3 doses (0, 1, 6 months); check anti-HBs titre 4 weeks after third dose.
Travel health appointment at least 6 weeks before travel to endemic areas.
Keep a vaccination record — needed for travel, healthcare work, and school entry.

topics.title: Smoking cessation
core_guidance: Combination NRT (patch + fast-acting form) doubles quit rates compared to no support.
Nicotine patch: 21mg (>10 cigarettes/day) or 14mg (<10/day) for 6–8 weeks, stepping down.
Varenicline (Champix/Chantix): most effective pharmacotherapy — start 1–2 weeks before quit date; 12-week course.
Bupropion: antidepressant also effective for cessation — start 1–2 weeks before quit date.
Behavioural support alongside medication doubles success rates.
red_flags: Varenicline: monitor for mood changes, depression, or suicidal ideation — rare but documented.
NRT in acute MI or unstable angina — medical review before use (usually still safer than smoking).
Smoking with oral contraceptive after age 35 — greatly increases CVT/stroke risk.
support_steps: Set a quit date — within 2 weeks of decision.
Combination NRT: 24-hour patch + lozenge/gum/inhaler for breakthrough cravings.
Identify and plan for triggers: alcohol, coffee, social situations.
