Each craving lasts 3–5 minutes — distract with activity, water, or deep breathing.
Smoking cessation clinics in Bangladesh through MOHFW — free support available.

topics.title: Exercise for health — prescription and guidance
core_guidance: Minimum: 150 minutes moderate-intensity aerobic activity/week (30 min x 5 days) OR 75 minutes vigorous.
Resistance training: 2+ sessions/week covering all major muscle groups — essential for metabolic health.
Even 10-minute walking bouts count — breaking prolonged sitting is independently beneficial.
Exercise is first-line treatment for: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, depression, anxiety, COPD, heart failure, osteoporosis.
Cardiovascular fitness (VO2max) is one of the strongest predictors of longevity.
red_flags: Chest pain, dizziness, or severe breathlessness during exercise — stop and seek assessment.
Exertional syncope (fainting during exercise) — cardiac evaluation before resuming.
New exercise programme in someone with known CVD or DM — medical clearance recommended.
support_steps: Start slowly: 10-minute walks, increase by 5 minutes per week.
Find activity you enjoy — adherence is the most important factor.
Habit stacking: attach exercise to an existing habit (walk after dinner, bike to work).
Resistance: bodyweight exercises (squats, push-ups) require no equipment.
Track steps: aim for 7,000–10,000 steps/day as a practical proxy.

topics.title: Falls prevention in the elderly
core_guidance: Falls are the leading cause of injury death in older adults — 1 in 3 adults over 65 falls each year.
Multifactorial risk: muscle weakness, balance impairment, medications (sedatives, diuretics, antihypertensives), vision, home hazards.
Medication review: >4 medications increases fall risk — deprescribe where possible, especially benzodiazepines and sedatives.
Exercise: Otago or Tai Chi balance and strength programmes reduce falls by 30–40%.
Home assessment: remove loose rugs, install grab rails, ensure adequate lighting.
red_flags: Fall with loss of consciousness — cardiac or neurological cause must be excluded.
Hip fracture after a fall: inability to weight bear, shortened/externally rotated leg — surgery needed.
Recurrent falls despite intervention — gait laboratory assessment and comprehensive geriatric assessment.
support_steps: Medication review: stop or reduce benzodiazepines, sedatives, and orthostatic antihypertensives.
Annual eye test — correct visual impairment reduces falls.
Exercise programme: minimum 2x/week balance and strength training.
Home modifications: grab rails in bathroom, non-slip mats, clear pathways.
Vitamin D 800–1000 IU daily + calcium — reduces fall risk independently of bone density.

topics.title: Dementia and Alzheimer's disease
core_guidance: Dementia: progressive cognitive decline affecting daily function — memory, language, executive function, personality.
Alzheimer's (60–70%): gradual onset, episodic memory first. Vascular dementia: stepwise, linked to CVD risk factors.
Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine): modest symptomatic benefit in Alzheimer's and Lewy body.
Behavioural symptoms (agitation, wandering): non-pharmacological first — routine, calm environment, validation therapy.
Advance care planning: while the person retains capacity — Lasting Power of Attorney, resuscitation preferences.
red_flags: Sudden confusion (delirium) in a person with dementia — identify and treat the precipitant (infection, pain, medication).
Rapid cognitive decline — exclude metabolic causes (hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, NPH).
Depression presenting as pseudodementia — trial of antidepressant before confirming dementia.
support_steps: Rule out reversible causes: thyroid, B12, folate, glucose, renal function, depression.
Donepezil 5–10mg nightly — for mild-moderate Alzheimer's.
Structured daily routine reduces agitation and wandering.
Carer support: equally important as patient care — respite, Alzheimer's Society, support groups.
Driving cessation discussion early — dementia legally requires notification to licensing authority.

topics.title: Polypharmacy and medication safety in elderly
core_guidance: Polypharmacy (>5 medications): associated with falls, confusion, adverse drug reactions, and hospital admissions.
Review all medications annually — question whether each drug's benefit still outweighs its risk in this individual.
Beers Criteria: medications to avoid/use with caution in elderly — benzodiazepines, antihistamines (diphenhydramine), NSAIDs, tricyclics.
Anticholinergic burden: combined anticholinergic drug effect causes confusion, constipation, urinary retention, falls.
Sick-day rules: hold ACE inhibitors, ARBs, diuretics, and metformin when acutely unwell with dehydration.
red_flags: Acute confusion in elderly — always check for medication cause (new drug, dose change, interaction).
Falls + recent medication addition — likely culprit.
Renal impairment developing on ACE inhibitor + diuretic + NSAID triple combination.
support_steps: Annual medication review — GP or pharmacist.
Brown bag review: bring ALL medications (including OTC) to each appointment.
Simplify: once-daily regimens, combination tablets, medication organisers.
Deprescribe gradually — start with the most problematic (benzodiazepines, long-term PPIs).
Sick-day rules: written plan of which drugs to hold when unwell.

topics.title: Insulin types and administration
core_guidance: Basal insulins (glargine/Lantus, detemir/Levemir): once or twice daily; provides 24-hour background cover.
Rapid-acting insulins (lispro/Humalog, aspart/NovoRapid, glulisine): inject 0–15 min before meals.
Intermediate insulin (NPH/isophane): twice daily; peaks at 4–10 hours.
Injection technique: pinch skin, 90° angle (45° if thin), rotate sites (lipohypertrophy from overuse of same site reduces absorption).
Hypoglycaemia: if glucose <4 mmol/L or symptomatic — 15g fast-acting carbohydrate; recheck in 15 minutes.
red_flags: Severe hypoglycaemia with unconsciousness — glucagon injection IM; if unavailable, call 999.
Lipohypertrophy (lumpy injection sites) — causes unpredictable insulin absorption.
DKA signs: fruity breath, deep breathing, vomiting — emergency.
support_steps: Rotate injection sites systematically — upper arms, thighs, abdomen, buttocks.
Store in-use pen at room temperature; spare insulin in fridge (never frozen).
Before meals: rapid insulin dose matches carbohydrate intake (insulin-to-carb ratio set by diabetes team).
Carry glucose source at all times; wear medical alert identification.
Sick-day rules: never skip insulin when unwell — test more frequently, adjust dose with diabetes team.

topics.title: Inhaler technique — MDI and dry powder
core_guidance: Pressurised MDI (pMDI): shake, exhale fully, seal lips, press and slowly inhale over 3–5 seconds, hold breath 10 seconds. Use spacer for better lung deposition.
Spacer: doubles drug delivery to lungs; essential for children and anyone with poor coordination.
Dry powder inhalers (Turbuhaler, Accuhaler, Handihaler): fast forceful inhalation required — opposite technique to MDI.
Common errors: not shaking MDI, inhaling too fast, not holding breath, not exhaling before use.
Rinse mouth and spit after inhaled corticosteroid — prevents oral thrush.
red_flags: Poor asthma/COPD control despite apparent adherence — check inhaler technique at every visit.
Oral thrush in someone using ICS — poor technique, inadequate rinsing.
Inhaler with dose counter at 0 — empty inhaler being used ineffectively.
support_steps: MDI: shake 5x, exhale fully, slow breath in over 5 seconds coordinated with press, hold 10 seconds.
Always use spacer with pMDI — especially children, elderly, or during asthma attacks.
DPI: do NOT shake; load dose, exhale away from device, forceful inhalation.
Clean spacer monthly with warm soapy water; air dry (do not towel — builds static).
Ask pharmacist or nurse to observe and correct inhaler technique at every medication review.

topics.title: Anticoagulants — warfarin and DOACs
core_guidance: Warfarin: target INR 2–3 (mechanical heart valve: 2.5–3.5). Extremely sensitive to diet, alcohol, and drug interactions.
DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban): no INR monitoring needed; fixed doses; fewer interactions than warfarin.
Warfarin reversal: minor bleeding — hold doses; major bleeding — vitamin K + PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate).
DOAC reversal: idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for rivaroxaban/apixaban.
Never miss doses of anticoagulant — but if missed: check the patient information leaflet for that specific drug.
red_flags: Any significant bleeding on anticoagulant — hold drug, seek medical review.
INR >5 — high bleeding risk; immediate dose hold and vitamin K consideration.
New interacting medication added to warfarin — recheck INR in 5–7 days.
support_steps: Warfarin: INR every 4–8 weeks when stable; eat consistent vitamin K diet (don't avoid, just be consistent).
DOAC: set phone alarm — missed dose with short half-life drugs is clinically significant.
Carry anticoagulant alert card at all times.
Inform all healthcare providers (dentist, surgeon) that you take an anticoagulant.
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin (unless specifically prescribed) — increases bleeding risk significantly.

topics.title: Fracture — recognition and first aid
core_guidance: Signs of fracture: pain, swelling, deformity, bruising, inability to bear weight, crepitus (grating sound/feel).
Immobilise the limb in the position found — do not attempt to straighten.
Improvised splint: padded rigid material (boards, magazines, rolled newspaper) extending above and below fracture.
Open fracture: bone visible through skin — cover with clean damp cloth, do not push bone back — call 999.
Spinal fracture: do not move — call 999, keep neck/back still using hands.
red_flags: Open fracture — infection and blood vessel/nerve injury risk, emergency.
Neurovascular compromise: numbness, tingling, or absent pulse below fracture — emergency.
Hip fracture in elderly: inability to weight bear after fall — emergency surgery needed.
support_steps: Immobilise in position found — above and below the fracture joint.
Ice wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling — 15–20 minutes.
Open fracture: clean damp dressing only; do not manipulate; call 999.
Do not give food or drink — surgery may be needed.
Elevate if possible while awaiting transport.

topics.title: Head injury — assessment and first aid
core_guidance: Concussion: brief alteration in consciousness after head impact — headache, confusion, amnesia, nausea.
Head injury red flags requiring immediate CT scan: loss of consciousness, amnesia, vomiting, seizure, focal neurological signs, coagulopathy.
Never leave a concussed person alone for the first 24 hours.
Return to sport/activity: gradual protocol — no same-day return; doctor clearance for sport after concussion.
Intracranial bleeding may develop after a lucid interval — 'talk and die' — early assessment is critical.
red_flags: Worsening headache, increasing confusion, one pupil larger than other, repeated vomiting — urgent CT.
GCS drop or new focal neurology — emergency.
Anticoagulated patient with any head injury — low threshold for CT.
support_steps: Any significant head injury: A&E for assessment if any red flag present.
Paracetamol for headache — avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk).
Rest from sport and screens for 24–48 hours minimum.
Written head injury instruction sheet: return immediately if vomiting >2 times, worsening headache, confusion.
Children with head injury: lower threshold for hospital assessment.

topics.title: Diabetic emergencies — hypoglycaemia and DKA
core_guidance: Hypoglycaemia (<4 mmol/L or symptomatic): sweating, tremor, confusion, palpitations, hunger.
Conscious patient: 15g fast-acting carbohydrate — 4–5 glucose tablets, 150ml orange juice, 5 jelly babies.
Recheck glucose in 15 minutes — repeat treatment if still <4; then eat a slow-release snack.
Unconscious hypoglycaemic: glucagon 1mg IM (if available); if not — call 999 (paramedics carry dextrose IV).
DKA: fruity breath, deep rapid breathing, vomiting, high glucose — hospital emergency (IV fluids + insulin).
red_flags: Unconsciousness from hypoglycaemia — glucagon injection or 999 immediately.
DKA signs: HGT >15 with ketones >3, vomiting, rapid breathing — emergency IV treatment.
Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) in type 2 DM: very high glucose, no ketones, extreme dehydration.
support_steps: Mild-moderate hypo: 4–5 glucose tablets or 150ml fruit juice; wait 15 minutes; recheck.
If still low: repeat carbohydrate; follow with slow-release carbohydrate (biscuit, bread).
Glucagon: inject into outer thigh or arm; works in 10–15 minutes — turn on side (may vomit).
Teach carers and family how to give glucagon.
Prevent nocturnal hypos: bedtime snack; basal insulin dose review.

topics.title: Snake bite first aid
core_guidance: Bangladesh venomous snakes: Russell's viper, cobras, kraits — significant cause of morbidity/mortality.
Immobilise the bitten limb below heart level — immobilisation slows venom spread.
Pressure immobilisation bandage for neurotoxic snakes (kraits, cobras) — NOT for cytotoxic/haemotoxic (vipers).
Hospital immediately — antivenom is the definitive treatment.
Do NOT: cut and suck, apply tourniquet, apply ice, use traditional remedies — all worsen outcomes.
red_flags: Ptosis (drooping eyelids), difficulty swallowing, respiratory weakness — neurotoxic venom, imminent respiratory failure.
Haemorrhagic signs (bleeding from bite, gums) — haemotoxic venom, DIC risk.
Tissue necrosis around bite site — cytotoxic venom, surgical debridement may be needed.
support_steps: Stay calm — exertion spreads venom faster.
Immobilise bitten limb with a splint or sling below heart level.
Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing from affected limb — swelling occurs.
Go to hospital immediately — do not delay for traditional treatment.
Note the time of bite and describe the snake if safely possible (helps guide antivenom).

topics.title: Hypothermia — recognition and treatment
core_guidance: Hypothermia: core temperature <35°C — shivering (mild), confusion (moderate), no shivering + unconscious (severe).
Gentle handling — hypothermic heart is prone to ventricular fibrillation with rough movement.
Remove wet clothing; insulate from ground; wrap in blankets or sleeping bag.
Warm the core first (not extremities) — rewarm trunk, not limbs (peripheral vasodilation drops BP).
Severe hypothermia: no signs of life — CPR should continue until rewarmed to 35°C ('not dead until warm and dead').
red_flags: Ventricular fibrillation in severe hypothermia — AED; may need multiple shocks as core temperature rises.
After rescue collapse: be aware of afterdrop (core temperature continues to fall after rescue).
Frostbite accompanying hypothermia — rewarm only after systemic hypothermia treated.
support_steps: Handle gently — do not rub extremities.
Remove wet clothing; horizontal position.
Warm drinks if conscious and can swallow; hot water bottles wrapped in cloth to armpits, groin, neck.
Call 999 — hospital rewarming for moderate-severe hypothermia.
CPR in severe hypothermia: continue until warm. Normal CPR protocol.

topics.title: Vitiligo — white patches on skin
core_guidance: Vitiligo is an autoimmune condition where melanocytes (pigment cells) are destroyed, causing white patches. It is NOT contagious.
First-line treatment: topical corticosteroids (e.g., mometasone cream) or topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus) applied to patches.
Narrowband UVB phototherapy is highly effective for widespread vitiligo — done in dermatology clinics 2–3×/week.
Sun protection (SPF 30+) on depigmented areas is essential — these areas burn very easily.
Cosmetic camouflage (Dermablend, DermaColor) can cover patches for social occasions.
Repigmentation is possible but takes 6–18 months; face and neck respond better than hands/feet.
Emotional support is critical — vitiligo causes significant psychological distress, especially in darker-skinned individuals.
red_flags: Rapidly spreading patches over weeks
Associated thyroid disease, diabetes, or other autoimmune symptoms — get bloodwork
Child under 10 — early specialist referral recommended
support_steps: See a dermatologist for confirmation and treatment plan
Check thyroid function (TSH) — vitiligo is associated with thyroid autoimmunity
Join support communities (Vitiligo Support International) to reduce isolation

topics.title: Contact dermatitis — allergic and irritant skin reactions
core_guidance: Irritant contact dermatitis (most common): caused by soaps, detergents, chemicals — skin barrier damage without immune involvement.
Allergic contact dermatitis: immune reaction to allergens (nickel in jewelry, fragrances, latex, hair dye, preservatives).
Treatment: immediately wash the area with mild soap and water; remove offending substance.
Topical hydrocortisone 1% (OTC) for mild cases; stronger prescription steroids for severe reactions.
Oral antihistamines (cetirizine 10mg, loratadine 10mg) reduce itch.
Cool compresses provide immediate itch relief.
Patch testing by a dermatologist identifies specific allergens.
Prevention: barrier creams (Vaseline), nitrile gloves instead of latex, fragrance-free products.
red_flags: Rash spreading beyond contact area (could be systemic reaction)
Facial/throat swelling — anaphylaxis risk, call 999
Rash not improving after 2 weeks of treatment
Oozing, crusting, spreading — possible superinfection
support_steps: Identify and avoid the trigger
For nickel allergy: coat jewelry with clear nail polish or switch to stainless steel/titanium
See dermatologist for patch testing if trigger unclear

topics.title: Hyperpigmentation and melasma
core_guidance: Melasma: brown patches on face (cheeks, forehead, upper lip) — triggered by sun, hormones (pregnancy, OCP), thyroid issues.
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH): dark marks left after acne, injury, or inflammation.
Sunscreen SPF 50+ DAILY is the single most important treatment — without it, all other treatments fail.
Topical treatments: hydroquinone 2–4% (most effective), kojic acid, azelaic acid, niacinamide (reduces melanin transfer), vitamin C serum.
Retinoids (tretinoin, adapalene) accelerate skin turnover and fade marks over 3–6 months.
Chemical peels (glycolic acid, salicylic acid) available at dermatology clinics.
Results take 3–6 months minimum; melasma often recurs without ongoing sun protection.
Avoid skin-lightening creams containing mercury or illegal steroids — widely sold in Bangladesh, dangerous.
red_flags: Rapidly changing mole or dark lesion with irregular borders — possible melanoma, see doctor urgently
Dark mark that bleeds, itches intensely, or has multiple colors
Using mercury-containing creams — these cause kidney damage
support_steps: Start with SPF 50 broad-spectrum sunscreen every morning (reapply every 2 hours if outdoors)
Add niacinamide serum and vitamin C — both safe, widely available
See dermatologist for tretinoin prescription or professional peels

topics.title: Fungal skin infections — ringworm, athlete's foot, jock itch, pityriasis
core_guidance: Ringworm (tinea corporis): circular scaly patches — NOT a worm. Highly contagious via skin contact or sharing towels/clothing.
Athlete's foot (tinea pedis): itching, peeling, blisters between toes — common in hot/humid conditions like Bangladesh.
Jock itch (tinea cruris): red, itchy rash in groin — common in men in hot climates.
Pityriasis versicolor: light or dark scaly patches on trunk — caused by Malassezia yeast, common in humid heat.
Treatment: topical antifungals — clotrimazole 1% cream (Canesten) or miconazole 2× daily for 2–4 weeks.
Ketoconazole 2% shampoo used as body wash for pityriasis versicolor — leave on 5 min before rinsing.
Keep area dry and exposed to air; wear cotton, loose clothing.
Oral terbinafine or fluconazole for extensive or resistant infections (prescription).
Do not share towels, clothing, or combs. Wash bedding at 60°C.
red_flags: Spreading rapidly despite treatment after 4 weeks
Scalp involvement in children — tinea capitis needs oral antifungals
Nail involvement (thickened, discolored nails) — needs oral treatment for months
Immunocompromised patient — more aggressive treatment needed
support_steps: Start clotrimazole cream 2× daily — available OTC as Canesten or generic
Keep the area clean and dry — moisture feeds fungal growth
Complete the full 2–4 week course even if it clears early — prevents recurrence

topics.title: Bell's palsy — sudden facial weakness
core_guidance: Bell's palsy: sudden one-sided facial weakness or paralysis, usually developing over hours to 2 days. Cause is likely viral inflammation of the facial nerve.
CRITICAL: Rule out stroke first — stroke causes weakness of face AND arm/leg; Bell's palsy affects ONLY the face (including forehead on same side).
Treatment (start within 72 hours): Prednisolone 50mg/day for 10 days — significantly improves recovery.
Combined antiviral (acyclovir) + steroid may be added for severe cases.
Eye care is essential — inability to close the eye causes corneal damage: use lubricating eye drops every 2 hours, tape eye shut at night.
Most patients (70–85%) recover fully within 3–6 months.
Physiotherapy (facial exercises) helps recovery.
Sunglasses outdoors protect the unprotected eye from wind and dust.
red_flags: Arm or leg weakness alongside facial weakness — this is STROKE, call 999 immediately
Both sides of face affected — not Bell's palsy, needs urgent investigation
Rash around ear or inside mouth + facial palsy — Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster), needs prompt antiviral treatment
No improvement after 3 months — needs specialist review
support_steps: Go to A&E immediately to exclude stroke
Start steroids within 72 hours of onset — this is the critical window
Get eye protection immediately — lubricating drops every 2 hours

topics.title: Parkinson's disease — management and support
core_guidance: Parkinson's is a progressive neurological condition where dopamine-producing brain cells die, causing tremor, rigidity, slowness, and balance problems.
Medications: levodopa/carbidopa (Sinemet, Syndopa) is the cornerstone — take consistently and never stop abruptly.
'Off' periods (medication wearing off) cause worsening symptoms — track timing and report to neurologist.
Exercise is as important as medication — regular aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) slows progression.
Physiotherapy: improves gait, balance, and reduces falls risk — essential.
Speech therapy for voice weakness and swallowing problems.
Non-motor symptoms: depression, sleep disorders, constipation, urinary problems — all treatable.
Home modifications: grab bars, non-slip mats, raised toilet seat — prevent falls.
Caregiver burnout is significant — carers need respite and support too.
red_flags: Sudden significant worsening — could indicate medication problem, infection, or Parkinson's emergency
High fever + severe muscle rigidity + confusion — Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome, medical emergency
New antipsychotic medication prescribed — many worsen Parkinson's (avoid haloperidol, metoclopramide)
Falls with head injury — always seek medical assessment
support_steps: Regular neurology follow-up every 3–6 months for medication adjustment
Enroll in physiotherapy and occupational therapy
Connect with Parkinson's support groups for patient and caregiver support

topics.title: Multiple sclerosis (MS) — support and management
core_guidance: MS is an autoimmune disease affecting the brain and spinal cord's myelin sheath, causing unpredictable neurological symptoms.
Common symptoms: fatigue (most disabling), numbness/tingling, vision problems, weakness, balance issues, bladder/bowel problems, cognitive fog.
Relapsing-remitting MS (most common): attacks (relapses) followed by recovery periods.
Disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) reduce relapse frequency — must be managed by neurologist.
Relapses treated with high-dose methylprednisolone IV — shortens relapse duration.
Fatigue management: pacing, energy conservation, avoid heat (Uhthoff's phenomenon — heat worsens symptoms temporarily).
Exercise: regular moderate exercise is beneficial, not harmful. Avoid overheating.
Physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and bladder/bowel specialists are key team members.
Mental health: depression and anxiety common — treat actively.
red_flags: Sudden vision loss — optic neuritis, needs urgent steroid treatment
Rapid worsening of walking or limb weakness over days
Severe bladder retention — medical emergency
First neurological episode suspicious of MS — urgent MRI referral
support_steps: Regular neurologist follow-up and MS nurse specialist support
MS Society resources and peer support groups
Annual flu vaccine and regular health screening recommended

topics.title: Memory problems and cognitive decline
core_guidance: Normal aging: occasional forgetfulness (names, where keys are) — not dementia. Dementia involves progressive decline in daily functioning.
Reversible causes of memory problems to exclude first: depression, thyroid disorder, vitamin B12 deficiency, sleep deprivation, medications, alcohol.
Concerning signs: getting lost in familiar places, repeating questions, unable to manage finances, personality changes, forgetting recent events.
Assessment: GP visit → cognitive tests (MMSE, MoCA) → blood tests → referral for brain imaging if indicated.
No cure for Alzheimer's/dementia, but early diagnosis allows planning and treatment of modifiable risk factors.
Medications (donepezil, rivastigmine) for Alzheimer's — modest benefit, must be prescribed by specialist.
Cognitive stimulation: social engagement, mentally challenging activities, learning new skills.
Vascular risk factors (blood pressure, diabetes, cholesterol, smoking) directly damage brain — controlling these protects cognition.
Caregiver education and respite care are essential.
red_flags: Sudden memory loss or confusion — could be stroke, delirium (infection), or medication toxicity
Young person (under 65) with memory problems — needs urgent investigation
Rapid progression over weeks to months — urgent neurology referral
Memory loss with gait problems or urinary incontinence — possible normal pressure hydrocephalus (treatable)
support_steps: Blood tests: TSH, B12, folate, full blood count, glucose, kidney/liver function
Review all medications — anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, sleep aids worsen cognition
Early specialist referral allows advance care planning while the person can participate

topics.title: Celiac disease and gluten intolerance
core_guidance: Celiac disease: autoimmune condition where gluten (in wheat, barley, rye) causes intestinal damage, leading to malabsorption.
Symptoms: diarrhea, bloating, abdominal pain, fatigue, weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, dermatitis herpetiformis (itchy skin rash).
Diagnosis: blood test (anti-tTG IgA antibody) then endoscopy — do NOT go gluten-free before testing as it invalidates results.
Treatment: strict lifelong gluten-free diet — even small amounts cause ongoing intestinal damage.
Gluten-free grains: rice, corn, potato, quinoa, certified oats. Avoid: wheat, barley, rye.
Watch for hidden gluten: sauces, marinades, processed foods, medications.
Nutritional deficiencies common: iron, calcium, folate, B12, vitamin D — supplement as needed.
Non-celiac gluten sensitivity (NCGS): similar symptoms without positive blood test or intestinal damage — managed same way (gluten-free diet).
red_flags: Continued symptoms despite strict gluten-free diet — recheck for hidden gluten sources or refractory celiac
Unexplained iron deficiency anemia in adult — celiac is a common cause, test for it
Bone fractures with minimal trauma — osteoporosis from long-term malabsorption
support_steps: Do not start gluten-free diet before blood test — testing requires active gluten consumption
Register with dietitian experienced in celiac for meal planning
Check all medications and supplements for gluten-containing fillers

topics.title: Lactose intolerance
core_guidance: Lactose intolerance: inability to digest lactose (milk sugar) due to low lactase enzyme. Very common in South and East Asian populations (up to 70–90%).
Symptoms: bloating, gas, cramps, diarrhea within 30 minutes to 2 hours after dairy.
Distinguish from milk protein allergy: lactose intolerance is not an allergy — no anaphylaxis risk.
Management: reduce or eliminate lactose-containing foods. Most people tolerate small amounts.
Lactase enzyme supplements (Lactaid) taken with dairy meals allow consumption.
Low-lactose alternatives: hard cheeses (minimal lactose), yogurt (bacteria pre-digest lactose), lactose-free milk.
Calcium sources without lactose: tofu, fortified plant milks, canned fish with bones, leafy greens, sesame seeds (til).
Do not over-restrict — some dairy can be tolerated and provides important nutrients.
red_flags: Symptoms with no dairy intake — other cause (IBS, celiac, infection)
Bloody diarrhea — not lactose intolerance, see doctor
Weight loss + persistent diarrhea — needs investigation
support_steps: Try a 2-week dairy elimination trial to confirm symptoms resolve
Reintroduce small amounts (yogurt first) to find personal tolerance threshold
Ensure adequate calcium from non-dairy sources

topics.title: Anal fissure — painful crack in the anus
core_guidance: Anal fissure: small tear in the lining of the anus, causing severe pain during and after bowel movements, and bright red blood on toilet paper.
Causes: hard stools (constipation), straining, diarrhea, childbirth.
Acute fissures (< 6 weeks) usually heal with conservative treatment.
Treatment: high-fiber diet + plenty of water to soften stools; sitz baths (warm water soaks 10–15 min, 2–3× daily) relieve spasm and promote healing.
Laxatives: ispaghula husk (Isabgol) or lactulose to keep stools soft.
Topical anesthetic: lidocaine 5% gel applied before bowel movements for pain relief.
Topical GTN (glyceryl trinitrate 0.4%) or diltiazem 2% cream relax the internal sphincter and promote healing — prescription required.
Botulinum toxin injection or surgery for chronic fissures (> 6 weeks) not responding to conservative treatment.
red_flags: Fissure in unusual location (not midline) — could indicate Crohn's disease, STI, or cancer
No improvement after 6 weeks of treatment
Fever + pain + swelling — possible abscess (surgical emergency)
support_steps: Start fiber and water immediately — this is the cornerstone of healing
Sitz baths 2–3× daily provide significant relief
See GP if no improvement in 4–6 weeks for prescription topical treatment

topics.title: Gestational diabetes
core_guidance: Gestational diabetes: high blood sugar during pregnancy that usually resolves after delivery. Affects 5–15% of pregnancies.
Risk factors: BMI > 30, previous GDM, family history, South Asian ethnicity (higher risk), previous large baby (>4.5kg).
Screened with oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) at 24–28 weeks.
Goals: fasting glucose < 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour post-meal < 7.8, 2-hour < 6.4.
First-line: dietary changes (low GI diet, portion control, regular small meals) + regular walking after meals.
If targets not met with diet: metformin or insulin — both safe in pregnancy. Insulin preferred in many cases.
Monitoring: blood glucose 4× daily (fasting + after each main meal).
Risks if uncontrolled: large baby (macrosomia), difficult delivery, neonatal hypoglycemia, increased c-section risk.
After delivery: 50% develop type 2 diabetes within 10 years — annual HbA1c screening recommended.
red_flags: Fasting glucose consistently > 7.0 or post-meal > 9.0 — needs urgent medication
Reduced fetal movements — contact obstetrics immediately
Signs of preeclampsia (headache, visual changes, swelling) alongside GDM — higher risk combination
support_steps: Start low-glycemic diet immediately: reduce rice/bread/sweets, increase vegetables, protein, and fiber
Walk 15–20 minutes after each meal — significantly lowers postprandial glucose
Frequent obstetric monitoring: growth scans, NST, close glucose surveillance

topics.title: Type 1 diabetes — insulin-dependent diabetes
core_guidance: Type 1 diabetes: autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells — no insulin production. Requires lifelong insulin.
Onset: often in childhood/young adults with rapid symptom development (days to weeks): extreme thirst, frequent urination, weight loss, fatigue.
Insulin therapy: multiple daily injections (basal-bolus) or insulin pump. Never skip insulin doses.
Basal insulin (e.g., glargine/Lantus, detemir) once or twice daily for background coverage.
Bolus insulin (rapid-acting: lispro/Humalog, aspart/NovoRapid) with each meal, carb-counted.
Target HbA1c: < 7% for most adults; < 7.5% for children.
Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar < 4 mmol/L): treat immediately with 15g fast carbs (glucose tablets, 150ml juice, 3–4 glucose sweets). Recheck in 15 min. Never drive when hypo.
Sick day rules: NEVER stop insulin during illness (even if not eating). Infection raises glucose. Check ketones if glucose > 14 mmol/L.
Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): medical emergency — hyperglycemia + ketones + vomiting/abdominal pain. Go to A&E immediately.
red_flags: DKA signs: fruity/acetone breath, vomiting, deep rapid breathing, confusion — CALL 999
Severe hypoglycemia (unconscious, cannot swallow): glucagon injection if available, call 999
Glucose persistently > 20 mmol/L — check ketones, contact diabetes team
New diagnosis suspected in child — same-day medical review required
support_steps: Never self-adjust basal insulin without guidance from diabetes team
Carry fast-acting glucose (glucose tablets) at ALL times
Wear medical alert ID; ensure family members know glucagon injection technique

topics.title: Peripheral artery disease (PAD)
core_guidance: PAD: narrowed arteries reducing blood flow to legs. Common in smokers, diabetics, and those with hypertension or high cholesterol.
Classic symptom: claudication — cramping/aching in calf (or thigh/buttock) that comes on with walking and resolves with rest.
Critical limb ischemia: rest pain, non-healing ulcers, or gangrene — urgent vascular surgery referral.
Risk factor management is the cornerstone: quit smoking (single most important), control blood pressure, cholesterol (statin), blood sugar.
Antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 75mg or clopidogrel — prescribed by doctor.
Supervised exercise program: walk until pain, rest, repeat — counterintuitively improves symptoms over weeks.
Foot care: inspect daily (especially diabetics), avoid barefoot walking, treat cuts immediately.
No tight socks or heat pads on feet — reduced sensation increases burn/injury risk.
Revascularization (angioplasty or bypass surgery) for severe cases.
red_flags: Sudden severe leg pain + pale cold leg = acute limb ischemia — CALL 999, surgical emergency within 6 hours
Non-healing foot ulcer + diabetes — high amputation risk without urgent vascular care
Rest pain at night — critical ischemia, urgent vascular referral
support_steps: Smoking cessation is the #1 most effective intervention — refer to cessation program
Daily foot inspection, especially for diabetics
Supervised exercise rehabilitation program

topics.title: Raynaud's phenomenon — cold fingers and color changes
core_guidance: Raynaud's: vasospasm of small arteries in fingers (and sometimes toes, nose, ears) in response to cold or stress. Fingers turn white → blue → red.
Primary Raynaud's: no underlying cause — common, not dangerous, manageable.
Secondary Raynaud's: caused by lupus, scleroderma, rheumatoid arthritis — more severe, treat underlying condition.
Management: keep whole body warm (not just hands); hand warmers, gloves, avoiding cold triggers.
Stop smoking (nicotine causes vasoconstriction).
Medications: nifedipine (CCB) 5–20mg daily during cold months — reduces frequency and severity.
Protect fingers: insulated gloves for handling frozen foods/cold objects.
Stress management (triggers attacks).
Biofeedback can help some patients.
red_flags: Skin ulcers or sores on fingertips — critical ischemia, urgent rheumatology referral
Associated joint pain, skin thickening, difficulty swallowing — secondary Raynaud's with connective tissue disease
Attacks in one hand only — investigate for local vascular or nerve compression
support_steps: Always carry gloves — prevention is easier than treatment
Blood tests: ANA, anti-Scl70, anti-Ro/La to exclude secondary causes if symptoms severe
See GP if attacks affect daily life — nifedipine is effective and well-tolerated

topics.title: Contraception and family planning
core_guidance: Barrier methods: male condom (98% effective with correct use) — only method that also prevents STIs. Female condom available.
Combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP): very effective (>99%), take at same time daily. Not suitable if: migraine with aura, history of blood clots, uncontrolled hypertension, smoker >35 years.
Progestogen-only pill (POP/mini-pill): suitable when estrogen contraindicated; must be taken within same 3-hour window daily.
Injectable: Depo-Provera (medroxyprogesterone) every 3 months — convenient, no daily pill.
Hormonal IUD (Mirena): highly effective 5–8 years, often stops periods — ideal for women wanting long-term contraception.
Copper IUD: non-hormonal, 10+ years, also used as emergency contraception if inserted within 5 days of unprotected sex.
Emergency contraception (levonorgestrel/'morning-after pill'): take within 72 hours (ideally within 12h). Available OTC.
In Bangladesh: family planning services through government health centers, NGOs (BRAC, Marie Stopes).
Male sterilization (vasectomy) and female sterilization (tubal ligation) — permanent options.
red_flags: Severe headache, vision changes, leg pain/swelling while on combined pill — possible blood clot or stroke, stop pill and seek emergency care
Missed pill(s) — follow specific missed-pill rules for each type
Emergency contraception does NOT protect against STIs — also consider STI testing after unprotected sex
support_steps: Consult with doctor or family planning clinic to find the best method for your health and circumstances
No contraception is 100% effective except abstinence — know your options
Condoms: even if using hormonal contraception, use condoms with new partners to prevent STIs

topics.title: PTSD and trauma responses
core_guidance: PTSD develops after experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event (accident, violence, disaster, assault). It is a normal nervous system response to abnormal events — not weakness.
Core symptoms: flashbacks/intrusive memories, nightmares, avoidance of reminders, emotional numbness, hypervigilance, sleep problems, irritability, concentration difficulty.
Onset can be delayed weeks or months after trauma.
Trauma-focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) and EMDR are the most effective treatments.
Medications: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) — first-line pharmacotherapy for PTSD.
Prazosin (alpha-blocker) can specifically help trauma nightmares.
Grounding techniques for flashbacks: 5-4-3-2-1 senses technique (name 5 things you see, 4 you hear, 3 you can touch...).
Avoid alcohol/drugs — common self-medication that worsens PTSD long-term.
Support groups and peer support from other trauma survivors can be very therapeutic.
red_flags: Active suicidal thoughts related to the trauma — crisis intervention needed immediately
Substance abuse developing alongside PTSD — dual treatment required
Complete functional shutdown (unable to leave home, eat, care for self) — urgent psychiatric support
support_steps: You do not have to talk about the trauma until you are ready — therapy works at your pace
Grounding techniques can interrupt flashbacks in the moment — practice them
Trauma-focused therapy is available; ask GP or mental health service for referral

topics.title: Specific phobias and intense fears
core_guidance: Specific phobia: intense, irrational fear of a particular object or situation causing significant distress or avoidance.
Common types: animals (spiders, dogs, snakes), blood/injection/injury, situational (heights, flying, closed spaces), natural environment (storms, water).
Exposure therapy (graded exposure/systematic desensitization) is highly effective — 80–90% improvement.
How exposure works: create fear hierarchy (least to most feared), gradually face each step while using relaxation techniques until anxiety reduces.
Virtual reality exposure therapy available for some phobias (flying, heights, spiders).
Blood/injection phobia: applied tension technique (tensing muscles to raise BP) prevents fainting.
Short-term: benzodiazepines for unavoidable one-off situations (dentist, flight). Not for regular use.
D-cycloserine may enhance exposure therapy — used in specialist settings.
Self-help exposure books (bibliotherapy) can be effective for motivated individuals.
red_flags: Phobia preventing necessary medical care (avoiding injections, blood tests, dentist) — prioritize this subtype for treatment
Multiple phobias and general avoidance pattern — may indicate social anxiety or agoraphobia
Fainting at sight of blood/needles — can be medical emergency in healthcare settings; use applied tension technique
support_steps: Avoidance is the main thing that maintains phobias — gradual exposure is the only way through
Self-help exposure hierarchy: list fears from 0–10 intensity, start practicing at level 2–3
Ask GP or psychology service for referral to CBT with graded exposure

topics.title: Relationship abuse and domestic violence support
core_guidance: Abuse in relationships includes: physical violence, emotional/psychological abuse, sexual abuse, financial abuse, and coercive control (monitoring, isolating, threatening).
Abuse is never the victim's fault. Abusers use cycles of tension → violence → honeymoon phase → tension to maintain control.
Safety planning: identify safe places to go, keep emergency items accessible (ID, money, medications), memorize important phone numbers.
Bangladesh National Helpline for Violence Against Women and Children: 10921 (toll-free).
Legal protection: restraining orders, Protection of Women Act. Police (999) can remove abuser in emergency.
Children witnessing domestic violence are also victims — their safety must be considered.
Healthcare providers must be told — injuries can be documented, referrals made to shelter/legal support.
Leaving is the most dangerous time — safety planning with specialist support is crucial.
Financial abuse: getting access to own money, opening separate bank account when safe.
red_flags: Immediate physical danger — call 999, go to nearest A&E or police station
Threats to kill or serious harm — treat all death threats as credible
Children at risk — child protection services must be notified
Stalking after separation — document all incidents, apply for restraining order
support_steps: You are not alone and you are not to blame
Bangladesh: National Helpline 10921 (24/7, toll-free for VAW/children)
Create a safety plan — even if you are not ready to leave, safety planning saves lives

topics.title: Personality disorders — understanding and support
core_guidance: Personality disorders: enduring patterns of inner experience and behavior that deviate markedly from cultural norms, causing distress or impairment. Begins in adolescence/early adulthood.

Borderline (Emotionally Unstable) PD (BPD/EUPD): most commonly treated. Features: intense fear of abandonment, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, self-harm, suicidal behavior.
Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) is the gold-standard treatment for BPD — teaches distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness.
Mentalization-Based Therapy (MBT) is also effective.
Medications are not first-line but may address specific symptoms (mood stabilizers for impulsivity, antidepressants for depression).
For family/carers: Family Connections program, validation-based communication, clear boundaries.
Recovery is possible — most people with BPD improve significantly over time with appropriate treatment.
Labels should not override seeing the person — avoid stigmatizing language.
red_flags: Active suicidal plan or recent serious self-harm — emergency psychiatric assessment
Dissociative episodes causing safety risk (driving, childcare)
Co-occurring substance abuse — dual diagnosis treatment needed
support_steps: DBT skills groups are available — ask mental health services for referral
Validation is more powerful than advice: 'that sounds incredibly painful' before problem-solving
Recovery takes time — celebrate small progress and consistency

topics.title: Tendinitis and common sports injuries
core_guidance: Tendinitis (tendinopathy): overuse or degeneration of tendons. Common sites: Achilles (back of heel), patellar (front of knee), rotator cuff (shoulder), lateral epicondyle (tennis elbow), medial epicondyle (golfer's elbow).
POLICE principle: Protection (avoid aggravating activity), Optimal Loading (gentle movement, not rest), Ice (15–20 min), Compression, Elevation.
Initial treatment: rest from aggravating activity, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400mg with food) for 5–7 days for pain, ice 2–3× daily.
Eccentric exercise program (tendon loading) is the most evidence-based rehabilitation — must be guided by physiotherapist.
Return to sport: gradual load progression over 6–12 weeks.
Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection for resistant cases — specialist intervention.
Achilles tendinopathy: avoid uphill running initially; calf raises (eccentric) are cornerstone of treatment.
Rotator cuff: physiotherapy is as effective as surgery for most cases — try 3 months first.
Prevention: proper warm-up, gradual training load increases (10% per week rule), adequate rest.
red_flags: Sudden 'snap' or 'pop' with immediate inability to walk/use limb — possible complete tendon rupture, needs urgent imaging
Fever + joint swelling + tendon pain — septic arthritis/tenosynovitis, medical emergency
Severe acute shoulder pain radiating to arm — could be cardiac event in older adults
support_steps: Rest from aggravating activity for 2–4 weeks (not complete rest)
Start eccentric exercise program with physiotherapist guidance
If no improvement after 3 months conservative treatment, consider imaging and specialist review

topics.title: Plantar fasciitis and heel pain
core_guidance: Plantar fasciitis: inflammation of the thick band of tissue on the bottom of the foot connecting heel bone to toes. The most common cause of heel pain.
Classic presentation: sharp pain at the bottom of the heel, worst with first steps in the morning or after prolonged rest, improves after a few minutes of walking.
Risk factors: prolonged standing/walking, tight calf muscles, high arches or flat feet, sudden increase in activity, obesity.
Conservative treatment (90%+ recover with non-surgical treatment):
1. Stretching: calf stretches + plantar fascia stretch (pull toes toward shin) 3× daily.
2. Supportive footwear: avoid flat shoes/barefoot walking; use arch support insoles (orthotic inserts).
3. NSAIDs: ibuprofen 400mg with food for 1–2 weeks for pain.
4. Ice: roll foot over frozen water bottle 2–3× daily.
Night splints: keep foot dorsiflexed during sleep — very effective.
Corticosteroid injection: for resistant cases after 6 weeks — good short-term relief.
Physiotherapy-guided rehabilitation.
red_flags: Pain in younger person (under 30) — stress fracture possible, needs X-ray
Heel pain with morning stiffness in spine and hips in young person — ankylosing spondylitis
Numbness or burning pain — tarsal tunnel syndrome (nerve compression)
support_steps: Start calf and plantar fascia stretches today — do them before getting out of bed each morning
Invest in good supportive footwear with arch support — essential for recovery
Recovery takes 3–12 months with consistent treatment; do not expect overnight results

topics.title: Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis)
core_guidance: Frozen shoulder: progressive stiffening and pain in the shoulder joint due to thickening and contraction of the joint capsule.
Three phases: Freezing (painful, 2–9 months), Frozen (less pain but severe stiffness, 4–12 months), Thawing (gradual improvement, 5–26 months).
Usually self-limiting but can take 2–3 years to fully resolve without treatment.
Risk factors: diabetes (3× increased risk), thyroid disease, previous shoulder injury, immobility, age 40–70.
Treatment: physiotherapy (stretching exercises), NSAIDs for pain, corticosteroid injection (significantly speeds recovery in freezing phase).
Physiotherapy exercises: pendulum exercises, towel stretches, cross-body reaches — must be done consistently daily.
Hydrodilatation: injection of saline into joint to stretch capsule — done by specialist under imaging guidance.
Surgery (arthroscopic capsular release) for severe persistent cases after 6 months of conservative treatment.
Diabetes: optimize blood sugar control — directly helps frozen shoulder resolution.
red_flags: Sudden severe shoulder pain after injury — rotator cuff tear (different condition)
Shoulder pain with fever/swelling in immunocompromised or IV drug user — septic arthritis
Pain radiating down arm to hand with neurological symptoms — cervical spine involvement
support_steps: Start daily stretching exercises immediately — consistency is key
Corticosteroid injection in the freezing (painful) phase significantly shortens recovery
If diabetic, improving blood sugar control accelerates shoulder recovery

topics.title: Reducing alcohol use
core_guidance: Safe drinking guidelines: no more than 14 units/week (UK), with at least 2–3 alcohol-free days. 1 unit = 25ml spirits or 250ml standard beer.
Short-term harms: accidents, injuries, poor sleep, anxiety, impaired judgment, violence.
Long-term harms: liver cirrhosis, liver cancer, pancreatitis, heart disease, 7 types of cancer, brain damage, dependency.
Practical cutting-back strategies: track drinks, alternate with water, delay first drink, choose lower-strength options, set a budget, avoid trigger situations.
AUDIT-C screening tool: 3 questions to assess whether drinking is hazardous (ask your GP).
Physical dependence: if experiencing shakes, sweating, or anxiety when not drinking — do NOT stop suddenly. Sudden alcohol withdrawal can cause seizures and death. Seek medical supervision.
Medically supervised detox: chlordiazepoxide or diazepam taper given in hospital or community under GP supervision.
Medications to maintain abstinence: naltrexone, acamprosate — prescribed by specialist.
Therapy: CBT, motivational interviewing, 12-step programs (Alcoholics Anonymous).
red_flags: Seizures, confusion, hallucinations when stopping alcohol — Delirium Tremens, medical emergency, call 999
Vomiting blood or black tarry stools — alcohol-related GI bleed
Jaundice (yellow skin/eyes), swollen abdomen — alcoholic hepatitis or cirrhosis
support_steps: If dependent (shakes when not drinking): NEVER stop suddenly — see GP for medically supervised detox
Alcoholics Anonymous Bangladesh: available in major cities, free support
Tell your doctor honestly about your drinking — they can help without judgment

topics.title: Dental and oral health
core_guidance: Oral hygiene essentials: brush 2× daily with fluoride toothpaste, floss or use interdental brushes daily, avoid sugary snacks between meals.
Toothache causes: tooth decay (cavity), abscess, cracked tooth, exposed root, gum disease.
Dental abscess: severe throbbing tooth pain + facial swelling + fever — requires antibiotics (amoxicillin 500mg 3× daily) + urgent dental drainage.
Pain relief while awaiting dentist: ibuprofen 400mg + paracetamol 1000mg alternating; clove oil on cotton ball on painful tooth (eugenol effect).
Gum disease (gingivitis/periodontitis): bleeding gums with brushing, bad breath, loose teeth — needs professional scaling and improved oral hygiene.
Mouth ulcers (aphthous): painful white ulcers, heal in 7–14 days. Benzydamine mouthwash (Difflam) reduces pain. If not healed in 3 weeks → see dentist (oral cancer risk).
Wisdom tooth pain: ibuprofen, warm saline rinses; if infected → antibiotics + referral for extraction.
Dental visits every 6–12 months for checkup and scale — prevents most serious problems.
Fluoride: use fluoride toothpaste, avoid rinsing mouth with water immediately after brushing.
red_flags: Facial swelling spreading to neck/throat or difficulty swallowing/breathing — Ludwig's angina, life-threatening, call 999
Tooth pain in jaw spreading to left arm/jaw/neck — could be cardiac pain, not dental
White or red patch in mouth not healing after 3 weeks — urgent dental/medical review (oral cancer)
High fever with dental abscess — hospital antibiotics needed
support_steps: Dental abscess: do not wait — see dentist same day or go to A&E if facial swelling present
Regular brushing and flossing is the most cost-effective healthcare intervention
Government dental services available in Bangladesh through upazila health complexes

topics.title: Travel health — vaccines, malaria prevention, and travel safety
core_guidance: Pre-travel planning: visit travel health clinic 4–6 weeks before departure.
Routine vaccines to be up-to-date: hepatitis A and B, typhoid, meningococcal, rabies (for rural/adventure travel), yellow fever (required for some countries).
Malaria prevention: check destination risk, take prophylaxis (doxycycline, atovaquone-proguanil, or mefloquine) as prescribed, use DEET repellent, sleep under insecticide-treated net.
Traveler's diarrhea: drink bottled/boiled water; eat only freshly cooked hot food. Treatment: oral rehydration salts (ORS) + ciprofloxacin 500mg twice daily for 3 days.
Altitude sickness (> 2500m): ascend slowly (<300m/day above 3000m). Acetazolamide 125–250mg twice daily for prevention (start 1–2 days before ascent).
Jetlag: melatonin 0.5–5mg at destination bedtime for first few nights; light exposure at destination in morning.
Insect protection: long sleeves, DEET 30–50%, permethrin-treated clothing for high-risk areas.
Health insurance: always get travel health insurance covering emergency evacuation.
Rabies: any animal bite abroad → immediate wound cleaning + post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) within 24 hours.
red_flags: Fever within 1 year of returning from malaria-endemic area — malaria test urgently (falciparum malaria can kill within 24 hours)
Dengue + spontaneous bleeding — dengue hemorrhagic fever, intensive care needed
Severe altitude sickness with confusion, cough, ataxia — HAPE/HACE, descend immediately, give dexamethasone
support_steps: Bring a basic travel health kit: ORS sachets, ciprofloxacin, antidiarrheal, antimalarial, antihistamine, wound supplies
Any animal bite abroad: clean wound for 15 min with soap and water then get PEP within 24h
Post-travel: report travel history to doctor if unwell within 1 year

topics.title: Nutrition — therapeutic diets and dietary advice
core_guidance: DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension): low sodium (< 2.3g/day), high potassium/magnesium/calcium foods — reduces blood pressure by 8–14 mmHg.
Mediterranean diet: olive oil, fish, legumes, whole grains, fruits, vegetables — reduces cardiovascular disease, dementia, and mortality.
Diabetic diet: low glycemic index (GI) foods (legumes, vegetables, whole grains), regular meal timing, avoid sugary drinks. Reduce white rice portions.
Heart-healthy: reduce saturated fat (red meat, butter, ghee), trans fat (fried fast food). Increase omega-3 (fish, flaxseed, walnuts).

Iron-rich foods for anemia: red meat, liver, dark leafy greens (palak/spinach), lentils (masur dal), fortified cereals. Pair with vitamin C (lemon juice) to enhance absorption. Avoid tea/coffee with iron-rich meals.
Calcium sources without dairy: sardines with bones, tofu, fortified soy milk, almonds, sesame seeds (til), broccoli, kale.
High-protein foods: eggs, chicken, fish, legumes (dal), soy, yogurt — 1.2–1.6g/kg for older adults and those recovering from illness.
Fiber: 25–30g/day. Sources: vegetables, fruits, legumes, oats, whole grain bread. Prevents constipation, lowers cholesterol, improves glycemic control.
red_flags: Unintentional weight loss > 5% in 3 months — always investigate for underlying disease
Extreme dietary restriction with distress about food — possible eating disorder
Supplements exceeding safe doses: vitamin A, vitamin D, iron — toxicity possible
support_steps: Aim for plate balance: half vegetables, quarter protein, quarter whole grain starch
In Bangladesh: dal-sabzi-mach with minimal oil and avoiding excess white rice aligns well with heart-healthy and diabetic diets
Registered dietitian referral for complex therapeutic diets (renal diet, PKU, eating disorder)

topics.title: Chronic pain management
core_guidance: Chronic pain: pain persisting > 3 months beyond expected healing time, or associated with chronic disease. Affects 20–30% of adults.
Chronic pain involves changes in the brain's pain-processing system (central sensitization) — not just tissue damage. This is not 'imagined'.
Biopsychosocial model: pain is influenced by physical, psychological, and social factors simultaneously.
Medications: paracetamol regular dosing; NSAIDs (short-term); low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10–75mg) for nerve pain; duloxetine; gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain.
Opioids: limited role in chronic non-cancer pain — risk of dependence, tolerance, and worsened pain (opioid-induced hyperalgesia).
Psychological therapies: CBT for pain, acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT), mindfulness-based stress reduction — as effective as medications for many.
Exercise: aerobic exercise and graded activity are among the most evidence-based treatments — reduces pain through endorphin release and central sensitization reversal.
Pain management programs: multidisciplinary programs (physiotherapy + psychology + medical) show best outcomes.
Pacing: balance activity and rest to avoid boom-bust cycles.
red_flags: New or worsening pain — always investigate before attributing to chronic pain diagnosis
Pain with fever, weight loss, night sweats — possible malignancy
Pain waking from sleep repeatedly — needs investigation
Suicidal thoughts related to pain — immediate mental health referral
support_steps: Pain is real even when tests are normal — do not dismiss or minimize
Set meaningful functional goals (walking more, returning to activity) rather than just pain reduction
Multidisciplinary pain clinic referral for complex chronic pain

topics.title: Palliative care and end-of-life support
core_guidance: Palliative care: specialist care focused on relief from pain, symptoms, and stress of serious illness — appropriate from diagnosis, not just at end of life.

Symptom control: pain (WHO analgesic ladder: paracetamol → NSAIDs → weak opioid → strong opioid), nausea (ondansetron, metoclopramide), breathlessness (low-dose oral morphine), constipation (always accompany opioids with laxatives).
Communication: honest conversations about prognosis and goals of care empower patients to make meaningful choices.
Advance care planning: discussions about preferences for resuscitation, hospitalization, location of death while patient has capacity.
Spiritual and cultural needs: important in Muslim-majority Bangladesh — involve religious support, ensure privacy for prayer, respect halal requirements.
Family support: grief often begins before death (anticipatory grief). Family needs support too.
Practical: government hospitals in Bangladesh have palliative care units (NICRH, BSMMU). Hospice Bangladesh provides community palliative support.
Caregiver guidance: preventing pressure sores, mouth care, managing medications at home.
red_flags: Uncontrolled pain despite opioids — needs specialist palliative care review
Terminal agitation/restlessness — specific medication protocols exist
Caregiver collapse — respite care and support services needed
support_steps: Request palliative care team involvement early — not just in the final days
Bangladesh Hospice and Palliative Care Association (BHPCA) provides guidance and support
Ensure religious and cultural wishes are documented and communicated to all caregivers

topics.title: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) — support and understanding
core_guidance: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD): neurodevelopmental condition characterized by differences in social communication and interaction, and restricted/repetitive behaviors and sensory sensitivities.
ASD is a spectrum — ranges from highly verbal and academically able to those with significant intellectual disability and no speech.
Signs in young children: delayed speech, limited eye contact, not responding to name, repetitive behaviors (stimming), intense interest in specific topics, sensory sensitivities.
Diagnosis: multidisciplinary assessment (developmental pediatrician, psychologist, speech therapist) — no single test.
Interventions: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA), speech and language therapy, occupational therapy (sensory integration), social skills groups.
Communication supports: picture exchange communication systems (PECS), AAC devices for non-verbal children.
Sensory accommodations: reduce overwhelming stimuli (ear defenders, sunglasses, predictable routines).
Education: inclusive education with support, individual education plans (IEP).
Medications do NOT treat autism core features but may help associated conditions: ADHD, anxiety, sleep problems, irritability (risperidone for severe aggression).
Bangladesh: Society for Autistic Children (SWAPNO), government autism schools and resource centers.
red_flags: Regression of previously acquired skills (speech, social) — needs urgent developmental assessment
Self-injurious behaviors — behavior therapy assessment needed
Seizures — co-occur in 25–30% of ASD cases, needs neurological evaluation
support_steps: Early intervention (before age 5) produces the greatest gains — refer as soon as concerns arise
Bangladesh Autism Resource Center: contact the National Academy for Autism and Neurodevelopmental Disorders (NAAND)
Connect with other autism families — peer support and practical guidance is invaluable

topics.title: Children's mental health — anxiety, depression, and behavioral issues
core_guidance: Children experience anxiety and depression too — symptoms differ from adults (stomach aches, headaches, school refusal, irritability rather than sadness).
School refusal: rarely truancy — usually severe anxiety. Forcing back to school immediately worsens it. Gradual reintegration with school + therapy support.
Childhood anxiety: CBT adapted for children (Coping Cat program) is highly effective. Parent involvement essential.
Childhood depression: persistent sad/irritable mood > 2 weeks, loss of enjoyment, social withdrawal, sleep changes. Fluoxetine (Prozac) — only SSRI recommended for under-18s in many guidelines.
Behavioral problems: ADHD, oppositional defiant disorder (ODD), conduct disorder — respond to structured parenting programs (Triple P, Incredible Years), behavioral therapy.
Teen self-harm: usually as emotion regulation, not suicidal intent — requires compassionate, non-judgmental response. Ask directly about suicidal thoughts.
Screen time and social media: excessive use associated with depression and anxiety in teens — encourage limits and face-to-face activities.
Trauma-informed approach: behavioral problems often mask underlying trauma.
Bangladesh CAMHS services: limited but growing — NIMH outpatient, private child psychiatry.
red_flags: Any expression of suicidal thoughts or self-harm in a child/teen — immediate mental health assessment
Psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusional thinking) in child — urgent child psychiatry
Severe eating restriction with rapid weight loss in teen — eating disorder, urgent referral
Sudden behavioral change after a specific event — assess for trauma/abuse
support_steps: Ask directly and calmly about suicidal thoughts — it does not increase risk and opens conversation
School counselors are first-line resources in Bangladesh — involve them early
Parents seeking help for a child's mental health is a sign of strength, not failure

topics.title: Antidepressants — how they work, side effects, and what to expect
core_guidance: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram, citalopram) are first-line antidepressants — safest, well-tolerated.
Takes 2–4 weeks to see mood effect; full benefit at 6–8 weeks. Do not stop early if no immediate effect.
Common SSRI side effects (usually temporary, first 1–2 weeks): nausea, headache, insomnia, increased anxiety. Take with food.
Sexual side effects (reduced libido, delayed orgasm) are common and persist — discuss with prescriber if problematic.
SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine): also effective for anxiety and pain. Duloxetine: good for fibromyalgia, neuropathic pain.
Tricyclics (amitriptyline, nortriptyline): older drugs, sedating — useful at low dose for pain, insomnia, migraine prevention.
NEVER stop antidepressants abruptly — taper slowly under doctor guidance to avoid discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, flu-like, electric shock sensations).
Serotonin syndrome: agitation, tremor, sweating, rapid heart rate, high fever after starting/increasing — medical emergency, stop medication and seek help.
Treat for minimum 6 months after recovery; recurrent depression may need lifelong treatment.
red_flags: Increased suicidal thoughts in first 2 weeks (especially in under-25s) — contact prescriber immediately, do not stop alone
Serotonin syndrome signs — stop medication, go to A&E
Manic episode triggered — SSRIs can unmask bipolar disorder; needs mood stabilizer added
support_steps: Give the medication at least 4–6 weeks before concluding it is not working
Keep a mood diary in the first weeks — helps identify trends and side effects to report
Never stop without tapering — tell your doctor if you want to stop

topics.title: Blood pressure medications — types, side effects, and adherence
core_guidance: Four main classes: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril), ARBs (losartan, valsartan), calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine), thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide).
Amlodipine: widely used, once daily, generally well-tolerated. Side effect: ankle swelling (common, not dangerous). Take at same time daily.
ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril): dry cough in 10–15% — if problematic, switch to ARB (losartan). Monitor potassium and kidney function.
ARBs (losartan, telmisartan): similar to ACE inhibitors but without cough. Good in diabetes/kidney disease.
Beta-blockers (atenolol, metoprolol, bisoprolol): reduce heart rate. Do not stop suddenly (rebound hypertension, angina). Side effects: fatigue, cold hands, erectile dysfunction.
Thiazide diuretics: cause increased urination initially. Monitor electrolytes (can lower potassium).
Adherence is critical — BP medications only work if taken daily. Missing doses is the #1 cause of treatment failure.
Check BP at home (target < 135/85 at home) — 'white coat hypertension' is common.
Combination pills improve adherence — ask doctor about fixed-dose combinations.
red_flags: Suddenly stopping beta-blockers — can precipitate angina or heart attack
Dizziness on standing (orthostatic hypotension) — fall risk, especially in elderly
Swelling of lips/face/tongue on ACE inhibitor — angioedema, medical emergency, stop immediately
support_steps: Take medications at the same time every day — morning with breakfast is easiest to remember
Home BP monitoring is more accurate than clinic readings for treatment decisions
Lifestyle changes (salt reduction, exercise, weight loss) can reduce medication requirements

topics.title: Diabetes medications — metformin, sulfonylureas, SGLT2, GLP-1, and insulin
core_guidance: Metformin: first-line for type 2 diabetes. Reduces liver glucose production. GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) — take with food, start low dose. Reduces cardiovascular risk.
Sulfonylureas (glibenclamide, glimepiride, gliclazide): stimulate insulin release. Risk of hypoglycemia (especially glibenclamide). Inexpensive, widely available in Bangladesh.
DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, vildagliptin): weight neutral, low hypo risk. Well tolerated. More expensive.
SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin): reduce glucose via urine. Additional benefits: heart failure, kidney protection. Risk of genital thrush infections — maintain hygiene.
GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide/Ozempic, liraglutide): significant weight loss + glucose lowering. Weekly injection. Strong cardiovascular benefit.
Insulin types: rapid-acting (before meals), long-acting (basal, once/twice daily). Never skip insulin in type 1 diabetes.
Hypoglycemia on sulfonylurea/insulin: treat with 15g fast carbs (glucose tablets, fruit juice). Recheck in 15 min.
HbA1c target: < 7% for most; < 8% for elderly/those with hypoglycemia risk.
Sick day rules: continue metformin unless vomiting; insulin doses need adjustment during illness — contact diabetes team.
red_flags: Recurrent hypoglycemia on sulfonylurea — dose reduction needed, inform prescriber
Metformin + contrast dye for CT scan — hold metformin 48h before/after (lactic acidosis risk)
SGLT2 inhibitor + illness/surgery/fasting — stop temporarily, DKA risk even in type 2
support_steps: Never stop diabetes medications without consulting doctor — even if feeling well
Monitor blood glucose at home (fasting + 2h post-meal) to guide dose adjustments
Bangladesh: generic metformin and glibenclamide are inexpensive and widely available in government hospitals

topics.title: Hearing loss — types, causes, and management
core_guidance: Types: conductive (outer/middle ear — often reversible) vs. sensorineural (inner ear/nerve — usually permanent).

Earwax impaction: most common reversible cause. Symptoms: muffled hearing, fullness, tinnitus. Treatment: olive oil drops daily for 1 week to soften, then irrigation. Do NOT use cotton buds — pushes wax deeper.
Glue ear (otitis media with effusion): common in children — fluid in middle ear. Most resolve spontaneously in 3 months. Grommets if persistent.
Age-related hearing loss (presbycusis): gradual high-frequency loss — normal aging. Hearing aids are the treatment.
Noise-induced hearing loss: cumulative damage from loud noise exposure. Wear ear protection. Irreversible.
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL): sudden one-sided hearing loss — treat as emergency. High-dose steroids within 72 hours dramatically improve outcome. Go to A&E/ENT same day.
Hearing aids: highly effective — modern ones are discreet. Available through government ENT services in Bangladesh.
Cochlear implants: for profound deafness unresponsive to hearing aids — specialist assessment needed.
red_flags: Sudden one-sided hearing loss — emergency, needs same-day steroid treatment
Hearing loss with vertigo and tinnitus — Menière's disease
Hearing loss in one ear with facial weakness — urgent investigation for tumor
Child with speech delay + suspected hearing loss — immediate audiology referral
support_steps: Earwax: try olive oil drops for 1 week before seeking irrigation — often sufficient
Never insert objects (cotton buds, fingers) into ear canal
Sudden hearing loss: go to A&E today — every hour counts for steroid treatment

topics.title: Allergic rhinitis — hay fever and year-round nasal allergy
core_guidance: Allergic rhinitis: immune response to allergens (pollen, dust mites, pet dander, mold) causing sneezing, runny/blocked nose, itchy eyes.
Seasonal (hay fever): pollen season. Perennial: year-round (dust mites, pets).
Step 1: intranasal corticosteroid spray (mometasone/Nasonex, fluticasone/Flixonase, budesonide) — most effective treatment. Use daily, takes 3–7 days for full effect. Safe for long-term use.
Step 2: add non-sedating antihistamine: cetirizine 10mg or loratadine 10mg daily. In Bangladesh: Zetid (cetirizine), Alatrol (loratadine).
Nasal saline irrigation (Neti pot or saline spray) reduces symptoms and is safe — can use 2× daily.
Allergen avoidance: HEPA air purifier, allergen-proof mattress covers (for dust mites), wash bedding at 60°C weekly.
Eye symptoms: antihistamine eye drops (ketotifen) or sodium cromoglicate drops.
Allergen immunotherapy (desensitization): for refractory cases — subcutaneous injections or sublingual drops over 3 years. Disease-modifying.
Avoid decongestant nasal sprays (xylometazoline/Otrivin) for more than 3–5 days — rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).
red_flags: Unilateral (one-sided) nasal symptoms only — not typical allergy, needs ENT review
Nosebleeds frequently on nasal steroid — check technique, reduce dose, ENT review
Smell loss — may indicate nasal polyps, needs endoscopy
support_steps: Nasal steroid spray is the cornerstone — use it correctly (aim away from septum, sniff gently)
Reduce dust mites: wash bedding weekly at 60°C, use allergen-proof covers
Cetirizine and loratadine are available OTC at any pharmacy in Bangladesh

topics.title: Insomnia and sleep disorders
core_guidance: Insomnia: difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early, causing daytime impairment. Affects 30% of adults.
Sleep hygiene (first-line): consistent sleep/wake time (even weekends), dark/cool/quiet bedroom, no screens 1 hour before bed, no caffeine after 2pm, no alcohol (disrupts sleep architecture).
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I): most effective long-term treatment — 80% improvement. App-based CBT-I (Sleepio, Somryst) available.
CBT-I techniques: sleep restriction therapy, stimulus control (bed only for sleep/sex), relaxation techniques.
Melatonin 0.5–5mg: safe, mild effect for sleep onset. Best for jet lag and circadian rhythm issues. Less effective for maintenance insomnia.
Short-term sleeping aids (use maximum 2–4 weeks only): antihistamines (diphenhydramine/promethazine — sedating, next-day hangover effect), benzodiazepines (diazepam — effective but habit-forming).
Avoid long-term benzodiazepine or Z-drug (zolpidem) use — dependence, tolerance, cognitive impairment, falls in elderly.
Address underlying causes: anxiety, depression, pain, sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome, medications (steroids, beta-blockers, SSRIs).
Paradoxical intention: try to stay awake with eyes open — reduces sleep performance anxiety.
red_flags: Witnessed apneas (stopping breathing in sleep) + loud snoring + daytime sleepiness — sleep apnea, needs sleep study
Uncomfortable leg sensations at night relieved by movement — restless legs syndrome (iron deficiency often treatable cause)
Insomnia with bipolar disorder — sleep disruption can trigger manic episode
support_steps: Fix wake time first (not bedtime) — get up at the same time every day regardless of how little you slept
CBT-I is more effective than sleeping tablets and has no side effects — try it first
Bedtime worry journal: write down worries 1 hour before bed, close the book, tell yourself you've dealt with them

topics.title: Urinary incontinence and overactive bladder
core_guidance: Stress incontinence: urine leaks with coughing, sneezing, laughing, exercise — weakened pelvic floor. Common after childbirth and in menopause.
Urge incontinence: sudden strong urge to urinate with leaking before reaching toilet — overactive bladder (OAB).
Mixed incontinence: both types combined — most common in women.

Pelvic floor exercises (Kegel): most effective treatment for stress incontinence. Contract pelvic floor muscles (as if stopping urine flow) for 10 seconds, relax 10 seconds — 3 sets of 10 reps, 3× daily for 3–6 months.
Bladder training for urge incontinence: urge suppression techniques, gradually extend time between toilet visits.
Lifestyle: reduce caffeine, alcohol, fizzy drinks (irritate bladder). Lose weight if obese. Avoid constipation.
Medications for OAB: oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin (antimuscarinics) — reduce urgency; side effects: dry mouth, constipation, confusion in elderly.
Mirabegron (beta-3 agonist): alternative with fewer side effects.
Surgery for stress incontinence: mid-urethral sling — highly effective, day procedure.
Botox injections into bladder muscle — for refractory OAB.
red_flags: Blood in urine + incontinence — needs urgent urology investigation
New incontinence with back pain + leg weakness/numbness — cauda equina syndrome, emergency
Sudden onset incontinence after stroke or spinal injury
support_steps: Start pelvic floor exercises today — the most evidence-based intervention, zero cost
Reduce caffeine (tea, coffee, fizzy drinks) — can dramatically reduce urgency within days
Incontinence is not 'just part of aging' — it is treatable; discuss with GP

topics.title: Refractive errors — glasses, contact lenses, and vision correction
core_guidance: Myopia (short-sightedness): can see near clearly, blurry far. Very common. Corrected with concave lenses (minus prescription).
Hyperopia (long-sightedness): can see far clearly, may strain for near. Corrected with convex lenses (plus prescription).
Astigmatism: blurry at all distances due to irregular cornea — corrected with cylindrical lenses.
Presbyopia: age-related loss of near focus (from ~age 40–45). Reading glasses or progressive lenses.
Eye examination every 1–2 years recommended. Children should have school vision screening.
Glasses: safest, no maintenance. Contact lenses: convenient but require strict hygiene — replace as prescribed, never sleep in them, never use tap water.
Contact lens complications: microbial keratitis (eye infection) — symptoms: pain, redness, reduced vision. Remove lens immediately, see ophthalmologist same day.
Laser refractive surgery (LASIK, SMILE): safe and effective for stable prescriptions. Not suitable for very high prescriptions or thin corneas.
Children with myopia: myopia control interventions (orthokeratology, atropine drops) can slow progression.
red_flags: Sudden loss of vision, flashing lights, or curtain across vision — retinal detachment, emergency
Eye pain + vision loss + nausea — acute glaucoma, go to A&E immediately
Red painful eye in contact lens wearer — remove lens, go to eye casualty
support_steps: Children: blurry vision causes poor school performance — annual school eye checks are important
Contact lens wearers: if eye becomes red/painful, remove lens immediately — never wait
Regular eye examinations detect glaucoma, diabetes eye disease, macular degeneration early

topics.title: Diabetic eye disease — retinopathy and prevention
core_guidance: Diabetic retinopathy: damage to blood vessels in the retina caused by high blood sugar. Leading cause of preventable blindness in working-age adults.
Risk increases with duration of diabetes, poor blood sugar control, high blood pressure, and smoking.
Annual diabetic eye screening (dilated fundus examination) — mandatory for all diabetics. Catches disease before vision loss occurs.
Stages: background (mild, no vision threat) → pre-proliferative → proliferative (new blood vessels, high bleeding risk) → maculopathy (affects central vision).
Prevention is treatment: tight blood sugar control (HbA1c < 7%), blood pressure < 130/80, quit smoking — can halt or reverse early disease.
Laser photocoagulation: for proliferative retinopathy — seals leaking blood vessels.
Anti-VEGF injections (bevacizumab, ranibizumab): for diabetic macular oedema — very effective at preserving vision.
Vitrectomy surgery: for vitreous hemorrhage or tractional retinal detachment.
Early detection + treatment prevents 90% of blindness from diabetic retinopathy.
red_flags: Sudden vision loss or floaters in a diabetic — vitreous hemorrhage or retinal detachment, go to A&E
Missing annual eye screening — every missed year increases blindness risk
support_steps: Annual eye screening is as important as HbA1c monitoring — never skip it
Every 1% reduction in HbA1c reduces retinopathy risk by 35%
