Bangladesh diabetic hospital (BIRDEM) has a dedicated ophthalmology department for diabetic eye care

topics.title: Knee pain — causes and management
core_guidance: Common causes by age: Young/active — patellofemoral pain syndrome (behind kneecap), ACL/meniscus injury, IT band syndrome. Older — osteoarthritis, bursitis.
POLICE principle: Protection, Optimal Loading, Ice (20 min), Compression (bandage), Elevation.
Patellofemoral pain (runner's knee): pain behind/around kneecap worsened by stairs, squatting, running. Physiotherapy + quadriceps strengthening is primary treatment.
Meniscus tear: twisting injury → joint line pain, locking, giving way. MRI for diagnosis; physiotherapy first; surgery if symptoms persist.
ACL tear: usually sports injury with loud pop, immediate swelling. MRI confirms. Surgical reconstruction if giving way episodes. Physiotherapy for months pre/post surgery.
Osteoarthritis: paracetamol + ibuprofen, physiotherapy, weight loss, knee brace. Knee replacement for end-stage.
Prepatellar bursitis (housemaid's knee): swelling over kneecap, pain kneeling. NSAIDs + padding + aspiration if large.
Red flag: hot red swollen knee + fever — septic arthritis (surgical emergency, drain and IV antibiotics same day).
Corticosteroid injection: for OA or bursitis — short-term relief, not curative.
red_flags: Hot red swollen knee + fever — septic arthritis, go to A&E immediately
Locked knee (cannot straighten) — meniscus fragment trapped, urgent orthopaedic review
Severe injury with immediate massive swelling — haemarthrosis (ACL or fracture), needs urgent imaging
support_steps: RICE for acute injury: Rest, Ice 20 min every 2 hours, Compression, Elevation
Physiotherapy-guided quadriceps strengthening is the cornerstone of most knee conditions
Weight loss of 5kg reduces knee OA pain significantly — prioritize this if overweight

topics.title: Neck pain and cervical spine problems
core_guidance: Most neck pain (90%+) is mechanical — muscle tension, poor posture, minor joint strain. Resolves in days to weeks.
Cervical spondylosis: age-related wear in neck vertebrae, disc degeneration. Very common on imaging after age 40 — does not always cause pain.

Cervical radiculopathy: nerve root compression causing pain/numbness/tingling/weakness radiating down arm (in dermatomal pattern). NSAIDs + physiotherapy first; nerve root block or surgery for severe persistent cases.
Whiplash: neck sprain from sudden acceleration/deceleration (road traffic accident). Most resolve within 3 months with active rehabilitation. Avoid collar immobilization — movement aids recovery.
Torticollis (wry neck): sudden painful neck rotation — usually muscle spasm. NSAIDs, heat, gentle stretching. Resolves in days.
Posture: computer/phone screen at eye level, ergonomic chair, regular breaks (every 30–45 min), neck stretches.
Treatment: NSAIDs, heat/ice, gentle mobility exercises, physiotherapy. Avoid prolonged bed rest.
Cervical myelopathy (spinal cord compression): clumsy hands, balance problems, leg weakness — needs urgent MRI and spine surgery.
red_flags: Neck pain after trauma (fall, accident) — immobilize, go to A&E — possible fracture
Neck pain + fever + headache + light sensitivity — meningitis, call 999
Arm weakness/numbness + difficulty with hand coordination or walking — myelopathy, urgent MRI
Night pain, unexplained weight loss, history of cancer — red flag for metastatic disease
support_steps: Most acute neck pain resolves in 2–6 weeks with gentle movement and NSAIDs
Ergonomics: adjust monitor to eye level, use headset for phone calls
Physiotherapy for persistent neck pain > 6 weeks or with arm symptoms

topics.title: Hepatitis B and C — chronic liver infection
core_guidance: Hepatitis B (HBV): transmitted via blood, unprotected sex, and mother-to-child. Vaccine-preventable — 3-dose vaccine series. Bangladesh has high HBV prevalence.
Chronic HBV: most infected as infants develop chronic infection. Monitored with HBsAg, HBeAg, viral load, liver function, and regular ultrasound.
HBV treatment: tenofovir or entecavir (antiviral) — suppress virus, prevent cirrhosis and liver cancer. Not a cure but controls disease.
Hepatitis C (HCV): transmitted primarily via blood (IV drug use, unsafe injections, transfusions). NO vaccine available.
Chronic HCV now curable: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) like sofosbuvir/daclatasvir — 12 weeks of pills, 95%+ cure rate. Available in Bangladesh (generic, affordable).
Liver cancer surveillance: HBV/HCV patients with cirrhosis need 6-monthly liver ultrasound + AFP.
Co-infection with HIV: antiretroviral therapy covers both HIV and HBV with tenofovir-containing regimens.
Alcohol must be completely avoided with chronic hepatitis — dramatically accelerates liver damage.
Household contacts and sexual partners of HBV patients should be vaccinated.
red_flags: Jaundice + abdominal pain + confusion in hepatitis patient — acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis, emergency
Rapidly increasing abdominal girth in cirrhosis — ascites, needs immediate medical management
Vomiting blood — variceal bleeding, call 999 (high mortality)
support_steps: Hepatitis B: get tested (HBsAg) and vaccinate all household contacts
Hepatitis C: treatment is now curative — Bangladesh has access to affordable generic DAAs
All hepatitis patients: completely avoid alcohol, hepatotoxic herbal remedies

topics.title: Sepsis — recognizing and responding to blood poisoning
core_guidance: Sepsis: life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Time-critical — every hour of delay increases mortality by 7%.
Think Sepsis mnemonic — any infection PLUS: Slurred speech/confusion, Extreme shivering or muscle pain, Passing no urine, Severe breathlessness, I feel I might die, Skin mottled/discolored.
Sepsis can develop from ANY infection: pneumonia, UTI, skin infection, abdominal infection.
Septic shock: sepsis + dangerously low blood pressure not responding to fluids — ICU needed.
At-risk groups: elderly, immunocompromised, diabetics, cancer patients, recent surgery, very young children.
ACTION: Call 999 / go to A&E immediately. In Bangladesh: go to nearest hospital emergency without delay.
Hospital treatment (Sepsis 6 bundle): blood cultures × 2 → IV antibiotics within 1 hour → IV fluids → oxygen → monitor lactate and urine output.
Sepsis in children: high fever + rash that doesn't fade under glass (non-blanching) = meningococcal septicemia — call 999 immediately.
Post-sepsis syndrome: survivors often experience fatigue, cognitive problems, PTSD for months — needs recognition and support.
red_flags: Any of the sepsis symptoms — call 999 or go to A&E immediately, do not wait
Non-blanching rash in child + fever — meningococcal disease, 999 immediately
Confusion + fever in elderly — could be sepsis even without typical symptoms
support_steps: Do not wait for symptoms to 'develop further' — act on suspicion of sepsis immediately
Tell A&E staff 'I think this could be sepsis' — they have protocols to act immediately
Prevention: vaccinations, wound care, not delaying treatment of infections

topics.title: Intestinal worms and parasites
core_guidance: Very common in Bangladesh due to sanitation conditions. Regular deworming recommended for children and at-risk adults.
Roundworm (Ascaris): most common — migrating larvae cause lung symptoms (Löffler's syndrome), then abdominal symptoms. Treatment: albendazole 400mg single dose or mebendazole 500mg single dose.
Hookworm: penetrates skin from soil, causes iron-deficiency anemia and protein malnutrition. Treatment: albendazole 400mg × 3 days.
Pinworm/threadworm: intense anal itching at night (worms lay eggs around anus). Common in children. Treatment: mebendazole 100mg single dose; treat whole family simultaneously.
Tapeworm: from undercooked pork or beef. May be asymptomatic or cause abdominal discomfort. Praziquantel or albendazole.
Giardia: diarrhea, bloating, foul-smelling greasy stools — from contaminated water. Treatment: metronidazole 400mg 3× daily for 5–7 days.
Amoebiasis: diarrhea (may be bloody), right-sided abdominal pain — from contaminated food/water. Treatment: metronidazole + diloxanide furoate.
Prevention: hand washing (especially before eating and after toilet), safe water, cooking meat thoroughly, clean vegetables.
WHO recommends mass deworming programs for school-age children in endemic areas.
red_flags: Visible worm in stool — bring sample to doctor for identification
Severe abdominal pain + vomiting + not passing gas — possible intestinal obstruction from worm mass
Seizures + headache in someone from endemic area who eats pork — neurocysticercosis (tapeworm cysts in brain)
support_steps: Annual deworming with albendazole 400mg recommended for all school-age children in Bangladesh
Whole family treatment for pinworm — reinfection is certain if others are not treated
Safe water and handwashing are the most effective preventive measures

topics.title: Preventive health — screening schedules and check-ups
core_guidance: Blood pressure: all adults every 2 years. Annual if > 120/80. Hypertension affects 25% of Bangladeshi adults.
Diabetes screening (HbA1c or fasting glucose): all adults > 45 every 3 years; earlier if overweight, family history, gestational diabetes history.
Cholesterol (fasting lipid profile): all adults > 35; earlier if cardiovascular risk factors.
Cervical cancer screening: women 25–65 years — Pap smear every 3 years or HPV test every 5 years. HPV vaccine for girls 9–14 years.
Breast cancer: monthly self-examination; clinical exam annually; mammogram every 2 years age 40–70.
Colorectal cancer screening: age 45+, colonoscopy every 10 years or fecal occult blood test annually.
Eye examination: every 2 years; annually if diabetic or glaucoma risk.
Dental check-up: every 6–12 months.
BMI, waist circumference, and lifestyle assessment annually.
Bangladesh government health card (DGHS) provides basic screening services through community clinics.
red_flags: Missed cervical screening for > 5 years — catch up urgently
Palpable breast lump — do not wait for next scheduled mammogram
Blood in stool at any age — investigate, do not attribute to hemorrhoids without evaluation
support_steps: Know your numbers: blood pressure, blood sugar, cholesterol — the three most important values
Upazila health complexes in Bangladesh offer many of these screening services free
Vaccination is also preventive: flu vaccine annually, pneumococcal vaccine for 65+, hepatitis B series if not vaccinated

topics.title: Wound care — cuts, lacerations, and wound management
core_guidance: Immediate care: apply firm direct pressure with clean cloth for 10–15 minutes without lifting.
Cleaning: irrigate wound thoroughly with clean running water or saline for 5–10 minutes — this is the most important infection prevention step.
Remove visible debris carefully with tweezers cleaned with alcohol.
Assessment: does it need stitches? Edges that don't stay together, wounds > 1cm on face or > 2cm elsewhere, deep wounds exposing fat/tendon/bone — need A&E.
Closure: steri-strips / butterfly closures for small gaping wounds. Hold edges together, apply strip across, leave 5 days.
Dressing: non-stick dressing + bandage. Change daily or when wet/soiled. Keep wound moist (not wet) — promotes healing.
Infection signs: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, red streaks spreading from wound, fever — start antibiotics (flucloxacillin or co-amoxiclav) and see doctor.
Tetanus: any puncture wound, animal bite, dirty wound — check vaccination status. If not vaccinated in 5 years → get booster.
Animal bites: always clean with soap+water 15 minutes, antibiotics (co-amoxiclav), tetanus, and rabies PEP consideration.
red_flags: Wound with red streaks spreading up arm/leg — spreading infection (lymphangitis), needs IV antibiotics
Wound + fever + confusion — possible sepsis
Any animal bite, especially dog/cat/bat/monkey — rabies PEP needed in endemic areas like Bangladesh
support_steps: Thorough irrigation is more important than any antiseptic — running water for 5–10 minutes
Animal bite in Bangladesh: wash 15 min, go to nearest government hospital for free rabies PEP
Wound not healing in 2 weeks in diabetic — see doctor urgently (diabetic foot risk)

topics.title: Food poisoning and gastroenteritis management
core_guidance: Food poisoning: usually caused by bacteria (Salmonella, E. coli, Campylobacter, Staph aureus, Listeria) or viruses (norovirus).
Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (watery or bloody), abdominal cramps, fever. Onset: 1–72 hours after eating contaminated food.
Most cases are self-limiting (2–5 days) — focus is on preventing dehydration.
Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS): first-line treatment. WHO formula: 1 litre water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt. Commercial: Gastrolyte, Pedialyte. Small frequent sips — not gulps.
Diet: clear fluids first, then bland foods (rice, bread, banana, crackers). Avoid dairy and fatty foods until recovery.

Antibiotics NOT needed for most food poisoning — virus or self-limiting bacteria. Use only for: severe Salmonella, Campylobacter in immunocompromised (ciprofloxacin or azithromycin), Listeria (ampicillin).
Loperamide (Imodium): slows diarrhea — useful for travel/urgent situations. Do NOT use if bloody diarrhea or high fever.
Food safety: cook meat to safe temperature, refrigerate promptly, wash hands and surfaces, separate raw and cooked food.
Rehydration assessment: urine color — pale yellow = hydrated, dark/absent = dehydrated.
red_flags: Bloody diarrhea + fever — possible E. coli O157 (do NOT give loperamide or antibiotics without expert guidance), hemolytic uremic syndrome risk
Signs of severe dehydration: unable to keep fluids down, sunken eyes, rapid weak pulse, minimal urine — IV fluids needed, go to hospital
Suspected Listeria in pregnancy — can cause miscarriage, stillbirth — urgent medical care
Symptoms persisting > 7 days — stool culture needed
support_steps: Start ORS immediately — do not wait until severely dehydrated
Children: dehydrate faster than adults; if child cannot keep any fluid down after 1 hour, go to hospital
Bangladesh: ORS sachets available free at government health centers and pharmacies

topics.title: Vitamin and mineral deficiencies
core_guidance: Vitamin D deficiency: extremely common worldwide including Bangladesh (paradoxically, despite sun exposure — darker skin, covered clothing, indoor work). Symptoms: bone pain, muscle weakness, fatigue, depression. Treatment: cholecalciferol 1000–2000 IU daily maintenance; 50,000 IU weekly for 8 weeks if severely deficient.

Vitamin B12 deficiency: common in vegetarians, elderly, metformin users, and with gut disease. Symptoms: fatigue, tingling/numbness hands and feet, poor memory, megaloblastic anemia. Treatment: IM cyanocobalamin injections (if malabsorption) or high-dose oral B12 1000mcg daily.

Iron deficiency anemia: most common nutritional deficiency globally. Symptoms: fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, pica (craving ice/soil). Treatment: ferrous sulfate 200mg 3× daily with vitamin C. Takes 3+ months to replenish stores. Find and treat the source of blood loss.

Folate deficiency: common in pregnancy, malnutrition, alcohol use. Critical for neural tube development. All women planning pregnancy: folic acid 400mcg daily (5mg if high risk). Green leafy vegetables.
Zinc deficiency: impairs immunity, wound healing, taste, and growth in children. Common with diarrheal illness. Bangladesh: zinc supplementation for child diarrhea reduces severity.
Iodine deficiency: causes goiter (enlarged thyroid) and hypothyroidism. Prevention: use iodized salt — universal in Bangladesh since 1990s.
Calcium deficiency: diet low in dairy and leafy greens. Risk: osteoporosis, muscle cramps. Supplement: calcium carbonate 500mg twice daily with vitamin D.
red_flags: B12 deficiency with neurological symptoms (neuropathy, cognitive decline) — aggressive treatment needed, nerve damage may not fully reverse
Severe iron deficiency in adult male or post-menopausal woman — must identify source of blood loss (GI cancer until proven otherwise)
Rickets in child — vitamin D + calcium supplementation + dietary review
support_steps: Vitamin D: most adults in Bangladesh benefit from supplementation 1000 IU daily
Folic acid: start 3 months before conception — not just at confirmation of pregnancy
Annual blood tests: CBC, iron studies, B12, folate, vitamin D recommended for at-risk groups

topics.title: Mental health medications — antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics
core_guidance: Antipsychotics: used for schizophrenia, bipolar mania, severe depression (augmentation), and other psychotic disorders.
Second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole): first-line. Side effects: weight gain, metabolic syndrome, sedation, raised prolactin (risperidone).

First-generation (typical) antipsychotics (haloperidol, chlorpromazine): still used for acute agitation, schizophrenia in resource-limited settings. Risk of extrapyramidal side effects (EPS): stiffness, restlessness, tardive dyskinesia.
Tardive dyskinesia: involuntary repetitive movements from long-term antipsychotics — often irreversible. Regular monitoring required.
Lithium: gold-standard for bipolar disorder. Narrow therapeutic window — requires regular blood level monitoring. Toxicity: tremor, nausea, confusion, polyuria. Avoid NSAIDs and dehydration.
Valproate: mood stabilizer for bipolar, epilepsy. Hepatotoxicity risk. AVOID in women of childbearing age (severe teratogen).

Benzodiazepines (diazepam, clonazepam, alprazolam): for acute anxiety, panic, seizures. Short-term only (< 2–4 weeks) — dependence and tolerance develop rapidly. Do NOT stop abruptly — risk of seizures.
Buspirone: non-habit-forming anxiolytic — takes 2–4 weeks, not effective for acute anxiety. Good for GAD maintenance.
NEVER stop psychiatric medications without consulting prescriber — relapse rates are very high.
red_flags: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS): high fever + severe rigidity + confusion on antipsychotic — stop medication, call 999
Lithium toxicity: tremor + diarrhea + confusion — check levels urgently, risk of renal failure
Sudden stopping of benzodiazepines — withdrawal seizures can be fatal
support_steps: Take medications as prescribed — stopping when feeling well is the most common cause of relapse
Lithium: stay hydrated, avoid NSAIDs, regular blood monitoring every 3–6 months
If experiencing side effects: discuss with prescriber before stopping — dose adjustment is usually the solution

topics.title: PMS and PMDD — premenstrual syndrome
core_guidance: PMS: physical and emotional symptoms in the luteal phase (2 weeks before period), improving within days of menstruation starting.
Physical: bloating, breast tenderness, headaches, fatigue. Emotional: mood swings, irritability, anxiety, low mood, difficulty concentrating.
PMDD: severe form — significantly impairs daily functioning and relationships. Requires treatment.
Lifestyle: regular aerobic exercise (reduces PMS by ~50%), regular sleep, reduce caffeine/salt/sugar in luteal phase.
Supplements with evidence: calcium carbonate 1200mg/day (reduces mood and physical symptoms), vitamin B6 50–100mg/day, magnesium 200–400mg/day.
Dietary: reduce salt (bloating), reduce caffeine (anxiety/breast tenderness), increase complex carbohydrates in luteal phase.
SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine): highly effective for PMDD. Can be taken continuously or only in luteal phase (day 14 to period).
Combined oral contraceptive pill (especially drospirenone-containing — Yasmin): reduces PMS/PMDD symptoms.
Cognitive behavioral therapy helps manage the psychological impact.
Track symptoms for 2 cycles to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment.
red_flags: Suicidal thoughts during luteal phase — PMDD emergency, needs immediate psychiatric review
Symptoms not resolving after period — not PMS/PMDD, other psychiatric or medical condition
support_steps: Track your cycle and symptoms for 2 months — this confirms diagnosis and helps identify most problematic days
Start with lifestyle interventions and calcium supplementation before medications
PMDD (severe): ask GP for referral or trial of luteal phase SSRIs — they are very effective

topics.title: Miscarriage and pregnancy loss
core_guidance: Miscarriage: loss of pregnancy before 24 weeks. 15–20% of recognized pregnancies. Risk increases with maternal age.
Types: threatened (bleeding with closed cervix — may continue), inevitable (open cervix), complete (all passed), incomplete (partial passage — needs medical or surgical management).
Symptoms: vaginal bleeding ± cramping in first trimester. Light spotting can be normal — significant bleeding needs assessment.
Management options for confirmed miscarriage: expectant (wait naturally), medical (misoprostol), surgical (ERPC). All are safe — choice depends on circumstances.

Ectopic pregnancy (life-threatening emergency): pregnancy outside the womb, usually in fallopian tube. Symptoms: one-sided pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding, shoulder tip pain (internal bleeding). Call 999/go to A&E.
Emotional impact: grief, guilt, anxiety about future pregnancies — all normal. Do not minimize the loss.
Investigations for recurrent miscarriage (3+): antiphospholipid syndrome (most important treatable cause), chromosomal analysis, uterine anatomy.
Future pregnancy: risk of miscarriage returns to baseline after one miscarriage. Early reassurance scan at 6–8 weeks recommended.
Bangladesh: early pregnancy assessment available at BSMMU, DMCH, and major private hospitals.
red_flags: Severe one-sided pain + vaginal bleeding + dizziness — ectopic pregnancy, CALL 999 IMMEDIATELY
Heavy bleeding soaking pads in 1 hour — hemorrhage, go to A&E
Fever + offensive discharge after miscarriage — septic miscarriage, IV antibiotics needed
support_steps: Any pregnancy bleeding: contact obstetric department same day for assessment
Ectopic pregnancy is a surgical emergency — do not wait at home if you have one-sided pain + bleeding
Emotional support: miscarriage grief is real — counseling and support groups are available

topics.title: Choking emergency — infant (under 1 year) specific protocol
core_guidance: NEVER perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) on infants under 1 year — can cause internal organ damage.
STEP 1 — Back blows: Hold infant face-down along your forearm, supporting head. Give 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand.
STEP 2 — Chest thrusts: Turn infant face-up on your forearm. Using 2 fingers, give 5 chest thrusts on lower half of breastbone (just below nipple line). NOT abdominal.
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts until object dislodged or infant becomes unconscious.
If infant becomes unconscious: start infant CPR (2 fingers on chest, 30 compressions then 2 rescue breaths). Call 999.
Do NOT do blind finger sweeps in the mouth — only remove object if you CAN SEE it.
CALL 999 after starting first aid — do not wait.
After successful removal: always seek medical assessment — even if infant seems fine.
red_flags: Infant unconscious — start CPR, call 999
Infant turning blue (cyanosis) — call 999 immediately while doing first aid
Any choking episode in infant — seek medical review even if object cleared
support_steps: Practice infant choking technique BEFORE it happens — YouTube WHO infant choking videos
Prevention: no small objects, coins, grapes, nuts, hard food for under-1s
Call 999 early — do not wait until infant deteriorates

topics.title: Allergic reactions — mild to moderate management
core_guidance: Mild-moderate allergic reaction: hives (raised itchy welts), mild swelling of lips/face without throat swelling, itching, runny nose, watery eyes — no breathing difficulty.
ANAPHYLAXIS (severe): breathing difficulty, throat tightening, voice change, collapse — this is a DIFFERENT emergency (use EpiPen, call 999).
Treatment for mild-moderate reaction: oral antihistamine — cetirizine 10mg or loratadine 10mg (non-sedating, preferred) OR diphenhydramine 25–50mg (sedating, faster onset).
In Bangladesh: cetirizine (Zetid, Alatrol 10mg) — first choice. Chlorphenamine (Piriton 4mg) — sedating option.
Remove or avoid the trigger (food, medication, insect sting — remove stinger by scraping sideways, not pinching).
Cool compress for local itching and swelling.
Monitor for 2 hours — mild reactions can progress to anaphylaxis.
Short course oral prednisolone (20–40mg for 3–5 days) for extensive hives not responding to antihistamines.
If allergic to food: strict avoidance, read all labels. Refer to allergist for testing and action plan.
red_flags: Throat tightening, difficulty swallowing, hoarse voice — progressing to anaphylaxis, use EpiPen + call 999
Difficulty breathing, wheeze, stridor — anaphylaxis
Collapse or loss of consciousness — anaphylaxis, call 999 immediately
support_steps: Keep antihistamines accessible at home and when traveling if you have known allergies
If reaction to unknown trigger: keep a food/activity diary to identify cause
EpiPen prescription: if you have had a moderate reaction, ask GP about EpiPen — prevents you being caught without it

topics.title: Fever management in children
core_guidance: Fever (temperature > 38°C) is not dangerous itself — it is the body fighting infection. The cause matters more than the number.
Measure axillary (armpit) temperature in under-2s with digital thermometer. Add 0.5°C for actual body temp.
Treat with paracetamol (acetaminophen): 10–15mg/kg every 4–6 hours. In Bangladesh: Napa syrup 120mg/5ml — give dose by weight.
Ibuprofen: 5–10mg/kg every 6–8 hours — do NOT use in under-3 months or dehydrated children. Brufen syrup in Bangladesh.
Alternate paracetamol and ibuprofen for high persistent fever — more effective than either alone.
Keep child hydrated: encourage fluids frequently. Dress lightly.

Febrile seizure: common in 6 months–6 years with rapid temperature rise. Child shakes, eyes roll, unresponsive for 1–5 minutes. Lay on side, protect from injury — do NOT put anything in mouth. Most stop within 5 min. Call 999 if > 5 min.
After febrile seizure: child will be drowsy — this is normal. Go to A&E for assessment.
Sponging with lukewarm (not cold) water reduces discomfort but does not reliably reduce temperature.
red_flags: Any fever in baby under 3 months — go to hospital immediately (serious infection cannot be excluded clinically)
Fever > 40°C not responding to antipyretics
Neck stiffness, non-blanching rash, photophobia — meningitis
Child inconsolably crying, bulging fontanelle, unusual drowsiness or unresponsiveness
Seizure lasting > 5 minutes — call 999
support_steps: Under 3 months: any fever = hospital. Do not wait.
Encourage fluids every 30 minutes — wet nappy every 3–4 hours confirms adequate hydration
Return to doctor if fever persists > 3 days without clear cause

topics.title: Suicide prevention — warning signs and how to help
core_guidance: Talking about suicide does NOT plant the idea — it reduces risk by opening conversation and reducing shame.

Warning signs: talking about wanting to die or being a burden; giving away possessions; sudden calm after period of depression; researching methods; saying goodbye; increased alcohol/drug use; withdrawing from loved ones.
Ask directly: 'Are you thinking about suicide?' — the question shows you care and opens the door.
Listen without judgment: validate their pain without minimizing it. 'That sounds unbearable' is more helpful than 'but you have so much to live for.'
Do not leave the person alone if there is immediate risk.
Remove means: remove medications, sharp objects, firearms from the environment.
Safety plan: identify warning signs → coping strategies → support people to contact → crisis lines → emergency services.
Bangladesh crisis lines: Kaan Pete Roi: 01779-554391 (emotional support helpline, in Bengali). Nearest psychiatric emergency: NIMH Dhaka, BSMMU.
After a suicide attempt: psychiatric assessment in A&E, safety planning, follow-up care — do not minimize or express anger.
Postvention: suicide loss affects families deeply — grief counseling and support groups for survivors.
red_flags: Stated plan + means available + timeline — immediate emergency psychiatric assessment, call 999
Person has made an attempt — call 999 or take to A&E immediately
Recent suicide in social network (contagion risk) — extra vigilance for vulnerable individuals nearby
support_steps: Ask. Listen. Stay. Connect them to help. Remove means. Follow up.
You do not need to solve it — your presence and willingness to listen saves lives
Kaan Pete Roi Bangladesh: 01779-554391 — free emotional support in Bengali

topics.title: Eye emergencies — chemical burns, foreign body, sudden vision loss
core_guidance: Chemical eye burn: IMMEDIATE ACTION — irrigate with large amounts of water for 15–20 minutes continuously. Remove contact lenses if present. Do not stop to call for help first — irrigate immediately. Then call 999/go to A&E.

Foreign body in eye: do NOT rub. Blink repeatedly, try flushing with clean water. If under upper eyelid, pull upper lid over lower to dislodge. If not removed, go to A&E — do not persist with attempts.
Penetrating eye injury (sharp object): do NOT remove the object. Cover eye lightly with clean cup, do not press. Go to A&E immediately.
Sudden painless vision loss: retinal artery occlusion or retinal detachment — ophthalmological emergency within hours. Call 999.
Painful red eye + vision loss + nausea: acute angle-closure glaucoma — go to A&E immediately, needs IV/oral acetazolamide and urgent laser treatment.

Arc eye (welder's flash): intense UV exposure causes corneal burn. Symptoms appear 6–12 hours later: severe pain, tearing, photophobia. Lubricating drops, rest, dark room — resolves in 24–48 hours. Paracetamol for pain.
Sub-conjunctival hemorrhage (bright red patch on white of eye): looks alarming but is harmless — caused by coughing/straining. No treatment needed. Resolves in 2–3 weeks.
red_flags: Chemical in eye — irrigate immediately for 15–20 min before anything else
Sudden vision loss — ophthalmological emergency, go to A&E same hour
Penetrating eye injury — do not remove object, cover and go to A&E
support_steps: Chemical burn: do not waste time calling — irrigate first, then call
Arc eye prevention: always wear appropriate eye protection when welding
Any sudden vision change: go to eye casualty same day — not 'wait and see'

topics.title: Heat exhaustion and preventing heat illness
core_guidance: Heat exhaustion (pre-heat stroke): heavy sweating, cool/pale/clammy skin, fast/weak pulse, nausea, muscle cramps, tiredness, weakness, dizziness, headache.
Treatment: move to cool place immediately, lie down with legs raised, remove excess clothing, apply cool wet cloths to skin, drink water or ORS if conscious.
Recovery expected within 30 minutes with cooling — if no improvement or symptoms worsen to heat stroke (hot/dry skin, confusion, loss of consciousness), call 999.
Heat stroke: core temperature > 40°C + neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures, unconsciousness) — MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Aggressive cooling while calling 999.
Prevention: avoid outdoor activity 10am–4pm in peak heat; stay hydrated (drink before feeling thirsty); wear loose light-colored cotton clothing; use fans/AC.
Vulnerable groups: elderly, infants, outdoor workers, athletes, those on diuretics/antipsychotics/anticholinergics — higher risk.
Bangladesh heat season: March–June — peak risk period. Dhaka has documented heat deaths annually.
Hydration: urine color is the best guide — pale yellow = adequately hydrated. Drink water + ORS for prolonged heat exposure.
Do not give fluids to unconscious person.
red_flags: Confusion, seizures, or loss of consciousness in heat — heat stroke, call 999, aggressive cooling
No sweating despite extreme heat + hot dry skin — heat stroke (not exhaustion)
Child/infant left in hot car — always a medical emergency
support_steps: Move to cool shade + water + rest — works for heat exhaustion when done early
Encourage elderly neighbors to check on each other during heat waves
Oral rehydration salts (ORS) better than plain water for prolonged heat exposure — replaces electrolytes

topics.title: Understanding common blood test results
core_guidance: Full blood count (CBC/FBC): Hemoglobin (Hb) — normal: men 130–175 g/L, women 120–160 g/L. Low = anemia. High = polycythemia.
White blood cells (WBC): 4–11 × 10⁹/L. High = infection/inflammation/leukemia. Low = viral infection, medication side effect, immunosuppression.
Platelets: 150–400 × 10⁹/L. Low = bleeding risk. High = infection, iron deficiency, reactive.
Kidney function: creatinine (men 62–115 µmol/L; women 53–97 µmol/L) + eGFR > 60 is normal. eGFR < 60 for > 3 months = chronic kidney disease.
Liver function: ALT < 40 U/L (enzyme leaked from damaged liver cells). Bilirubin < 21 µmol/L. Albumin 35–50 g/L (low = liver disease or malnutrition).
Thyroid: TSH 0.4–4.0 mIU/L. Low TSH = hyperthyroidism. High TSH = hypothyroidism.
HbA1c: < 42 mmol/mol (< 6.0%) = normal. 42–47 (6.0–6.4%) = pre-diabetes. ≥ 48 mmol/mol (≥ 6.5%) = diabetes.
CRP (C-Reactive Protein): < 10 mg/L normal. Elevated = inflammation/infection. Very high > 100 = significant infection.
Lipids: Total cholesterol < 5.0 mmol/L; LDL < 3.0 (< 2.0 if high CV risk); HDL > 1.0 (men) > 1.2 (women); Triglycerides < 1.7.
Always interpret results in clinical context — a single abnormal value rarely gives a diagnosis.
red_flags: Hemoglobin < 70 g/L — severe anemia, may need transfusion
WBC > 30 × 10⁹/L — possible leukemia, urgent haematology review
Platelets < 50 × 10⁹/L — significant bleeding risk
Creatinine rapidly rising — acute kidney injury, urgent nephrology
support_steps: Ask your doctor to explain what each abnormal value means for your specific situation
Single mildly abnormal result often needs to be repeated in 3–6 months rather than acted on immediately
Print or photograph your results — a record over time shows trends that matter more than single values

topics.title: Occupational health — work-related illnesses and ergonomics
core_guidance: Repetitive strain injury (RSI): overuse injuries from repetitive tasks — wrist, forearm, shoulder, neck. Prevention: ergonomic setup, regular micro-breaks (5 min every hour), stretching.
Computer workstation ergonomics: monitor at eye level, arms at 90°, feet flat on floor, lumbar support, keyboard and mouse at elbow height.
Work-related lower back pain: most common occupational injury. Ergonomic lifting technique (bend knees, keep load close, no twisting), ergonomic chairs, alternating sitting/standing.
Noise-induced hearing loss: irreversible. Wear hearing protection (earplugs/earmuffs) when noise > 85 dB. Legal requirement in Bangladesh occupational safety law.
Chemical exposure: MSDS (material safety data sheet) for every chemical used. Gloves, eye protection, respiratory protection as required.
Work-related stress: chronic workplace stress → burnout, depression, cardiovascular disease. Solutions: workload management, boundaries, supervisor communication, EAP (employee assistance program).
Shift work: increases risk of metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, breast cancer, depression. Sleep hygiene critical for shift workers.
Garment industry workers (Bangladesh): heat stress, standing strain, chemical exposure (dye/solvent) — specific risks requiring employer safety protocols.
Farmers/outdoor workers: pesticide exposure, heat illness, leptospirosis (from flooding water).
red_flags: Numbness/tingling in hands with computer work > 6 months — carpal tunnel syndrome, needs assessment
Chemical splash to skin/eyes — emergency decontamination
Symptoms of pesticide poisoning: nausea, miosis (small pupils), sweating, muscle fasciculations — organophosphate emergency
support_steps: Report work injuries and illnesses — workers have legal rights to occupational health assessment in Bangladesh
Ergonomic adjustments are low-cost and prevent costly injury
Workplace mental health: speaking to supervisor about workload is a right, not a weakness

topics.title: Transgender and gender-diverse health
core_guidance: Gender dysphoria: distress from incongruence between gender identity and assigned sex at birth. A recognized medical condition requiring support, not a mental illness or personal failure.
Gender-affirming care improves mental health outcomes: reduces depression, anxiety, and suicide risk significantly.
Social transition: using correct name and pronouns has immediate positive mental health effects.
Hormone therapy (masculinizing/feminizing): requires specialist (endocrinologist or gender clinic) assessment. Regular monitoring of blood pressure, lipids, hematocrit, hormones.
Testosterone therapy (trans men): deepens voice, redistributes fat, grows facial hair, stops periods. Risks: polycythemia, lipid changes.
Estrogen + anti-androgen therapy (trans women): breast development, fat redistribution, reduced body hair. Risks: blood clots, especially in smokers.
Preventive health: maintain all cancer screening appropriate to anatomy — e.g., trans men retain cervixes and may need Pap smears.
Mental health co-morbidities are common (depression, anxiety, PTSD from discrimination) — treat actively.
Bangladesh: hijra community has unique cultural identity and specific health needs including STI risk, mental health burden, and marginalization barriers to care.
Respect, non-judgmental care, and use of correct pronouns/name are basic requirements.
red_flags: Suicidal ideation in trans youth — emergency psychiatric assessment; trans youth have very high suicide rates
Deep vein thrombosis symptoms on hormone therapy — chest pain or leg swelling
Unsupervised hormone use (online/unregulated sources) — liver, cardiovascular, and bone risks without monitoring
support_steps: Bandhu Social Welfare Society (Bangladesh): LGBTQ+ health services and peer support
Gender-affirming care is evidence-based medical care — access should not be a barrier
Family acceptance is protective — involve family in support where safe to do so

topics.title: Refugee and migrant health
core_guidance: Refugees and migrants face compounded health risks: infectious disease, malnutrition, mental health trauma, limited healthcare access, and language barriers.
Bangladesh hosts over 1 million Rohingya refugees (Cox's Bazar) — largest refugee settlement globally.
Priority infectious disease risks in camp settings: cholera, measles, malaria, typhoid, skin infections (scabies, fungal), waterborne diseases, TB.
Cholera prevention: oral cholera vaccine (OCV), safe water (boiling, chlorination), handwashing with soap, latrines.
Mental health: extremely high rates of PTSD, depression, and anxiety from displacement, violence, and loss. Culturally appropriate mental health support needed.
Malnutrition: acute malnutrition in children — screen with MUAC (mid-upper arm circumference). < 115mm = severe acute malnutrition, needs therapeutic feeding.
Reproductive health: high rates of unplanned pregnancy, maternal mortality, GBV. Access to family planning, safe delivery, and GBV response services essential.
Vaccination catch-up for children: measles-rubella vaccine priority, plus standard EPI vaccines.
MSF, UNHCR, WHO, and NGO health facilities provide primary and secondary care in Cox's Bazar.
red_flags: Cholera: profuse watery diarrhea + severe dehydration — IV fluids + ORS + antibiotics (azithromycin/doxycycline)
Severe acute malnutrition with complications — inpatient therapeutic feeding center
Measles rash in unvaccinated cluster — outbreak response and mass vaccination
support_steps: Cox's Bazar: MSF, UNHCR, WHO, Bangladesh government facilities provide free health services to Rohingya
Kala-azar (visceral leishmaniasis): endemic in parts of Bangladesh — fever + weight loss + splenomegaly — refer to national kala-azar program
Mental health: UNHCR and NGO mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) programs available in camps

topics.title: Medication safety — avoiding errors, interactions, and safe storage
core_guidance: The 5 Rights: Right patient, Right drug, Right dose, Right route, Right time — check all five before taking or giving any medication.

Common dangerous drug interactions to avoid: warfarin + NSAIDs (bleeding), metformin + contrast dye (lactic acidosis), MAOIs + SSRIs (serotonin syndrome), statins + amiodarone (myopathy), lithium + NSAIDs (toxicity).
Polypharmacy (5+ medications): risk of drug interactions and adverse effects multiplies. Annual medication review — remove any unnecessary drugs.
Storage: most medications room temperature, away from heat/humidity (not bathroom cabinet). Insulin: refrigerate but not frozen. Syrup medications: check if refrigeration required.
Expired medications: most drugs remain potent beyond expiry but some (tetracyclines, insulin) can degrade dangerously. When in doubt, replace.
Medication adherence: use pill organizers, phone alarms, fixed routine (with meals). Missing doses is the most common cause of treatment failure.
Crushing tablets: never crush slow-release tablets (SR, XR, XL, LA, ER) — dangerous dose dumping. Ask pharmacist if crushing is safe.
Antibiotics: always complete the full course even when feeling better — incomplete courses cause resistance.
Safe disposal: do not flush medications down toilet or throw in regular bin — return to pharmacy for safe disposal.
red_flags: Taking more than one anticoagulant or blood thinner without explicit medical instruction
Multiple prescribers without communication — high interaction and duplication risk
Child accessing medication storage — childproof caps, high storage
support_steps: Keep a complete medication list (name, dose, frequency) — carry it to all medical appointments
Ask pharmacist to check for interactions whenever a new medication is prescribed
Bangladesh: pharmacists at community pharmacies can advise on basic drug interactions and dosing

topics.title: Mental health resources and crisis contacts — Bangladesh
core_guidance: Kaan Pete Roi: 01779-554391 — free emotional support helpline in Bengali. Trained volunteers, non-judgmental.
National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH), Dhaka: 01700-700900 / 9676001 — national psychiatric hospital, outpatient, inpatient.
BSMMU (Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University) Psychiatry Department: tertiary referral center.
Dhaka Medical College Hospital Psychiatry: emergency psychiatric services.
Upazila Health Complexes: mental health services being integrated through community clinics program.
iCDDR,B mental health research and services.
Private psychiatry: multiple private clinics in Dhaka, Chittagong, Sylhet — typically BDT 800–2000 per consultation.
Online therapy: growing availability of video-based therapy in Bengali — ask GP for referrals.
School/college counselors: first point of contact for students.
Substance use: Bangladesh National Anti-Drug Center, NADC, and Ahmadiyya Muslim Jama'at rehabilitation centers.
Violence against women: Bangladesh National Helpline 10921 (24/7, free), One-Stop Crisis Centers (OCC) at district hospitals.
red_flags: Immediate suicide risk — Kaan Pete Roi: 01779-554391 or NIMH emergency, or go to nearest hospital A&E
Acute psychosis (hallucinations, severe agitation) — go to NIMH or DMCH psychiatric emergency
Domestic violence emergency — call 10921 or 999
support_steps: Save Kaan Pete Roi (01779-554391) in your phone — for yourself or to share with someone who needs it
Mental health care is available in Bangladesh — community clinics, upazila hospitals, and NIMH all provide services
Asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness — you deserve support
